PBS DIGITAL PRODUCTION GUIDEBOOK

# Page 2^page=2
2 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 3 WELCOME TO THE PBS DIGITAL PRODUCTION GUIDEBOOK ABOUT PBS PBS, with more than 330 member stations, offers all Americans the opportunity to explore new ideas and new worlds through television and digital content. Each month, PBS reaches over 36 million adults on linear primetime television, more than 16 million users on PBS-owned streaming platforms, 53 million viewers on YouTube, and 60 million people view PBS content on social media, inviting them to experience the worlds of science, history, nature, and public affairs and to take front-row seats to world-class drama and performances. PBS’s broad array of programs has been consistently honored by the industry’s most coveted award competitions. Teachers of children from pre-K through 12th grade turn to PBS LearningMedia for digital content and services that help bring classroom lessons to life. As the number one educational media brand, PBS KIDS helps children ages 2–8 build critical skills, enabling them to find success in school and life. Delivered through member stations, PBS KIDS offers high-quality content on TV — including a PBS KIDS channel — and streaming free on pbskids.org and the PBS KIDS Video app, games on the PBS KIDS Games app, and in communities across America. More information about PBS is available at PBS.org, one of the leading dot-org websites on the internet, Facebook, Instagram, or through our apps for mobile and connected devices. Specific program information and updates for press are available at pbs.org/pressroom or by following PBS Communications on X. ABOUT PBS DIGITAL STUDIOS PBS Digital Studios produces original digital programming designed to engage, enlighten and entertain online audiences. The PBS Digital Studios network has more than 30 million subscribers on YouTube, generating an average of 50 million views each month. Since 2012, it has launched greater than 120 original series and has accumulated more than 5 billion lifetime views on YouTube. Series include the Webby Award-winning BE SMART and SOUND FIELD as well as popular series such as WEATHERED, EONS, MONSTRUM and SPACE TIME. For more information on PBS Digital Studios, visit PBS.org. Contact digitalstudios@pbs.org. The PBS Digital Production Guidebook is designed to help stations create compelling, sustainable digital content for online platforms. This comprehensive guidebook provides detailed instruction on how to craft effective audience-first strategies, create content for online platforms within PBS brand standards and policies, implement digital-friendly systems and processes, and expand impact opportunities for digital media projects. This book is for digital enthusiasts of any level or specialty who want to make public media thrive on the internet. Station staffers are encouraged to read the guidebook and share it with their production, development, audience and operations teams.

# Page 3^page=3
4 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 5 A Letter From the Editors Welcome to the PBS Digital Production Guidebook! Since its founding, public media has always been an innovative experiment. Now, it is more essential than ever to expand our programming to reach audiences where they are: online. Equipping and empowering member stations with the relevant tools and skills to become digital experts ensures PBS’s relevancy and the continued positive impact of public media on our local and national communities. The PBS Digital Production Guidebook is the culmination of more than a decade of digital expertise and experimentation by PBS Digital Studios and our network partners — independent and station producers alike. It consists of hard lessons learned, greatest achievements and peer-to-peer case studies to illustrate the many ways public media is already successfully showing up to the competitive digital media market — and how you can, too! The pages within are designed in a workbook style, reminiscent of your favorite grade school textbook and, perhaps, when learning last felt fun. The internet is a vibrant, creative and curious place! All digital media experts must subscribe to these hallmarks. We hope the pages within inspire you and kickstart your imagination as well as provide you with valuable practical activities and case studies to advance your knowledge of foundational digital media strategies and best practices. This guidebook can be engaged independently by someone looking to advance their digital career or project, collaboratively by teams looking to build new skills and processes, and strategically by executives focused on content pipeline planning and scaled transformation. Users can read one chapter at a time as needs arise or deliberately work through an entire section across the quarter or year. We hope there will be even a few of you who read the book cover to cover! We have designed customized tables of content for relevant stakeholders to help navigate where to begin and when to dive deeper. This book is meant to be a digital media primer to equip stations with the necessary skills to launch digital properties and grow them. Although there is no one-size-fits-all approach to public media or online platforms, once you have mastered these digital foundations, your station will be primed to iterate and innovate as part of the competitive digital media market. If there is some wisdom we can impart from our own experiences moving from broadcast into digital and assembling this guidebook, it would be this: ● Learning takes time, dedication and enthusiasm. Be patient and thoughtful with yourself and your teams as you try new things. ● Viral videos and series are rare, not the norm. It takes time and practice to build great properties and authentic brands. ● Be insatiably curious about your audience, and center them above all else. ● Prioritize and encourage practicing again and again and again to become experts. ● Invite people in. Make it a team effort. We all do better with more creativity in the room. ● Celebrate the small wins along the way because there will be many of them! ● Take stock of the missteps and areas for improvement because there will also be plenty of them, too. Then, take productive action to make things better each time. But most of all: Keep going. The American public needs your powerful stories and perspectives, tailor-made for the internet, and public media has wildly talented, passionate and imaginative people ready to serve them in the digital present and future. We can’t wait to see what you make! Sincerely, Maribel Lopez, Sr. Director and Head of PBS Digital Studios Diana Fraser, Managing Editor of the PBS Digital Production Guidebook Amanda Granger, Co-Editor of the PBS Digital Production Guidebook

# Page 4^page=4
6 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 7 Designed for, by and With Stations The PBS Digital Production Guidebook was created using a human-centered design process, focusing on the target audience, PBS member stations, at every stage: ● RDCI Program: PBS Digital Studios received a grant from the Corporation of Public Broadcasting to build digital capacity at PBS member stations. Participating stations created compelling original digital series highlighting local stories with national appeal. Throughout the program, grantees provided detailed feedback on what stations needed to create successful digital content and workflow. ● Development: PBS Digital Studios conducted five focus groups representing 25 stations and 38 people from staffers to executives to discern what stations wanted from this text. ● Drafting: The guidebook outline was shared with focus group participants for feedback. ● Writing: Eighteen station partners were invited to contribute 38 case studies to relevant chapters and provide a courtesy review of in-progress chapters. ● Testing: Select member stations outside the PBS Digital Studios network participated in a pilot testing program, providing feedback on four early chapters. We are deeply grateful for each station’s time and energy spent ensuring this book became the best version of itself: Alabama Public Television Alaska Public Media Arizona PBS Blue Ridge PBS Austin PBS Buffalo Toronto Public Media Detroit PBS GBH Georgia Public Broadcasting Houston Public Media Idaho Public Television KQED Louisiana Public Broadcasting Montana PBS National Education Telecommunications Association (NETA) New Mexico PBS Pacific Islanders in Communications (PIC) PBS El Paso PBS Fort Wayne PBS North PBS North Carolina PBS SoCal PBS Wisconsin PBS Utah Public Media Connect (WCET/WPTD/WPTO) South Carolina ETV TPT — Twin Cities PBS WHYY WJCT Public Media WKAR The WNET Group WSKG WTIU Special thanks to PBS Station Services for their insight, connection and partnership during the creation of this guidebook.

# Page 5^page=5
8 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 9 Defining Digital This guidebook focuses on how to create effective original digital series for YouTube as well as vertical video platforms. We see “digital” as an adjective, extending to all areas that influence content creation and its sustainability: ● Production and Post-Production ● Scriptwriting and Researching ● Audience Development ● Marketing and Communications ● Fundraising In this guidebook, the term “digital” is not referring to digital product development, websites, broadcast or promotion. When we say “social video” or “vertical video,” we are referring to bespoke vertical videos created specifically for vertical video platforms such as Instagram Reels, TikTok, and YouTube Shorts. When we say “building digital capacity,” we are referring to accelerating station capabilities around producing digital content for YouTube and social and vertical video platforms. How to Use This Guidebook The PBS Digital Production Guidebook was created primarily with digital beginner and intermediate levels in mind. Although advanced users can still find valuable insights and practical exercises, it is more likely that they will use this guidebook to teach others who are newer to digital media. All users can use this book to develop a shared digital vocabulary and expectations across skill levels and areas of expertise. This book is designed to be as evergreen as possible. Although the “platform du jour” may change, relying on the core principles of digital best practices and rigorous PBS editorial standards will always remain relevant. It also acknowledges and embraces that digital is an evolving medium and requires digital learners to be flexible as it grows. This guidebook is intended to be used by individuals and teams alike. Sustainable digital transformation takes time, so consider which path is the most useful and appropriate for you. Starting in one lane does not prevent you from expanding or crossing into another in the future. Independent Professional Development ● Choose one to three new things to try in your upcoming projects. ● Find a study buddy, and complete one chapter a month together. ● Set SMART goals tied to the guidebook during your performance review. ● Organize lunch and learns for your team or colleagues to complete one chapter. Team Professional Development and Upskilling ● Integrate the guidebook into required new staff onboarding and training. ● Create SMART goals tied to the guidebook during performance reviews. ● Support staff in setting aside regular time to complete chapters devoted to their individual development. ● Use relevant chapters to try one to three new things in upcoming projects. ● Report back to your manager, mentor or executive sponsor on what you learned and any outcomes. ● Schedule team workshops or retreats to do chapter deep dives.

# Page 6^page=6
10 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 11 Station-Wide or Department Continuing Education ● Integrate the guidebook into required new staff onboarding and training. ● Leverage the guidebook as continuing education curriculum for staff. ● Require departments to read role-relevant chapters. ● Set goals to complete chapters or entire sections across the quarter or year. ● Create SMART goals tied to the guidebook for teams, and supervise progress. ● Integrate practice activities and learnings into current and upcoming projects. ● Organize lunch and learns to share what team(s) learned and any outcomes. ● Host day-long training workshops for staff to progress through chapters, share learnings and set actionable goals for the next quarter/year. ● Remember to set a regular workshop cadence and schedule follow-up meeting(s) to assess progress! Whichever path you choose, be intentional. Work with individuals and teams to help them understand why digital is a strategic priority and what role they play in making it successful. Work together to assign chapters that are relevant to their role or career interests. Support staff when they struggle, and champion them when they succeed. Key sections are associated with particular colors, which are outlined in the Table of Contents. American Sign Language graphics are used throughout as step markers for numbers 1 through 10. Chapters follow a consistent structure throughout the book and are a direct result of station focus group feedback, which requested the following order to best engage users: “Ideally, digital engagement is an all-in commitment from the executive team through layers of management to production teams, programmers, marketing, creative services, underwriting, business ops and so on. This can be daunting for stations that have been doing things a certain way for years who are challenged by change and not sure about digital production processes. Investigate the ‘what, why, how’ of digital production from the start. Speak with system resources like PBS Digital Studios and stations who are having success in the digital space. Find the digital natives on your teams — whether they are in production or not — to participate or lead digital initiatives. Pick a small project, and put your all into it. Once you find success, that will spur deeper engagement in the digital space.” — Don Geraci, Director of Content Houston Public Media ● Basic Practice Activity/Reflection Prompt ● Instructional Text ● Case Study ● Final Practice Activity/Template ● Parting Thoughts/Dive Deeper We strongly recommend that users complete the practice activities and ask colleagues for strategic feedback. Everybody needs practice when building a new skill, and these activities have been crafted to ensure productive outcomes and ready-made templates for your future work. Digital isn’t easy. If it was, the PBS Digital Production Guidebook wouldn’t be 314 pages long! But it doesn’t have to be daunting. You can read a single chapter and do it really well, and that will still have a positive impact. You can read the book cover to cover and overhaul your whole department with great care and effort across several years, and that will be transformational. Or you can choose to try something new here and there when it makes sense for your project. There are plenty of opportunistic, low-hanging fruits to choose from in these chapters that will have a big impact. The goal is that you use the guidebook, and often, however you choose. Good luck! “Confidence will come as you build new skills and create a mind shift in the culture. Give yourself time and space to experiment and learn. Start with something small by doing research, gathering data and filling a content gap. If something doesn’t take off right away, don’t abandon the commitment you’ve made to implementing these new workflows. Finally, reach out to the system and talk with colleagues at other stations to work through challenges.” — Lynne Bader, Vice President, Original Content Creation Buffalo Toronto Public Media

# Page 7^page=7
12 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 13 7 Digital Best Practices As you embark on the planning and execution of your new digital project, lead with an open mind, and be confident that with good preparation, you can succeed. 1. Digital budgets, timelines and deliverables are different from broadcast: Digital production doesn’t necessarily equate to faster or cheaper production. Although it does offer opportunities for innovation and flexibility, it requires careful planning and investment to achieve efficient and cost-effective results. The demand for engaging content on digital platforms often necessitates frequent updates and releases, which can extend production timelines and require consistent output. 2. Staffing your team appropriately is important: Deciding how to assign roles in a digital video production involves considering each team member’s skills, expertise and experience as well as the project’s specific requirements. One person cannot wear 20 hats. Responsibilities must be effectively delegated, and there must be a clear creative director calling the shots. For digital projects, the whole must be greater than the sum of its parts. 3. Keep it simple to start: It’s tempting to start a digital project wanting to do on-location shoots and high-end production, but the truth is, you don’t need an elaborate production to have a successful product on digital platforms. There’s no need for lavish field shoots, out-of-state travel or high-profile talent. Remember, if you have a great concept or story, it’s okay to compromise on bigger line items that may not pay off. 4. Marketing and social are essential throughout the project, not an afterthought at the end: It’s not enough to create trailers and promos at the end when the content is done. Audiences are looking for authenticity and content that is going to improve their lives, which means creating expressions of your digital series that are bespoke and original to engage people on different platforms. Plans for marketing and social media need to be present throughout your workflow. 5. This will be a new adventure and requires a growth mindset: Be prepared to practice this for several shows before you feel like a working expert. Every project will teach you new lessons as your audience and format evolve. 6. Establish audience and impact goals early in the planning process, and understand how to manage expectations: Start with smaller goals until you have a better understanding of the landscape and add touchpoints in your timeline to revisit your goals throughout the production process. 7. Be a solutions-based problem solver, and invite your team to the table: It’s virtually impossible to create a successful digital project in a bubble or by yourself. If you do, we’d consider that lightning in a bottle. Build a strong team with an established feedback culture, so when issues arise, it feels natural to consult each other and rebound quickly if plans go awry. 1 2 3 4 5 6 7

# Page 9^page=9
16 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 17 Guidebook Glossary Core Principles Digital Platforms: online platforms with video and content streaming capabilities that garner regular viewership including but not limited to YouTube and YouTube Shorts, Instagram Reels, TikTok and PBS online platforms. Digital Series: a set of episodes of video content, each with a distinct and self-contained topic with national appeal, that are released online ranging from 8 to 20+ episodes per season or series. Digital Timeline: a cyclical production process that constantly produces the next YouTube episode before the previous episode is finished, with the goal to publish every 2 to 4 weeks, on average. Digital Video Styles: different types of storytelling based on varying pacing, scripting, cinematography and editing of your video, such as Explainer, Video Essay and Documentary. Digital Workflow: a method to set up, plan and manage your new project and resources to streamline your work, typically relying on a cyclical production schedule instead of linear phases. Edutainment: a video genre that combines education and entertainment to make learning more enjoyable and engaging. Iteration: doing something again and again, usually to improve it — for example, using data from a pilot episode one would iterate, or make changes, to reach a desired result. Longform: PBS content that exceeds short-form length for the relevant platform, which is usually a “standard” YouTube video length of eight minutes or more. Short form: PBS content that is shorter than longform length for the relevant platform, typically three minutes or less but can extend up to 25 minutes. Vertical Video: videos that are produced and consumed vertically on platforms such as YouTube Shorts, TikTok or Instagram Reels. Partners and People Distributor: intermediaries that distribute your content to a target audience, typically handling logistics, marketing, rights management and other outlet connections — some distributors can also be funders. Funder: a person or organization that provides money for something. Multiplatform: single projects with original content deliverables that stretch across multiple platforms, both broadcast and digital. PBS LearningMedia: a PBS digital platform that offers free, media-based content intentionally designed to complement classroom instruction for pre-K–12 educators and students. Standards and Practices (S&P): policies that articulate the core principles that ensure the quality and integrity of all content distributed by PBS — all content produced by PBS and its member stations must adhere to Standards and Practices. Subject-Matter Expert (SME)/Advisor: someone who supports your series by providing key knowledge and/or lived experience that can weigh in on story ideas, research, fact-checking or scripts and guide you to other experts or community members in the space — SMEs, if paid, cannot appear on camera. Target Audience: the specific, intended audience to watch, engage or respond to a video or channel, or the people you want to communicate with.

# Page 10^page=10
18 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 19 Tools Comparables: often referred to as “comps,” pieces of media that are similar in look and feel, market, budget, cast, distribution strategy or story, or a combination thereof, to what you are developing. Digital Script: a thorough, well-researched and fact-checked script to guide the content and pacing of a digital video project. Landscape Analysis: a process that allows you to identify any series, brands, talent, communities, agencies and organizations who could positively or negatively affect the success of your series. Outline: a detailed, structured document that lays out the key points and flow of a YouTube video, including the introduction, main content sections, conclusion and call-to-action needed for the story arc. Pitch Deck: a visually rich document that encapsulates your series idea and direction — it contains all the key information to pitch stakeholders, whether they are outside funders or internal leadership. Pre-Production Report: a process document created by the production team to ensure the content and production will be representative of a wide variety of lived experiences. Project Brief: a quick document with essential high-level project information created at the beginning of a project before a kickoff meeting, summarizing the goals, scope, target audience, deliverables and key deadlines — this is often the basis for a more detailed Scope of Work or Statement of Work. Analytics A/B Testing: a quantitative research method that compares two or more versions of content to determine which one performs best. Call-to-Action: an action you are asking a viewer to take after watching your video, typically delivered by a host at the end of a video. CTR: video “click-through rate,” which is the percentage of people who click on a video to watch it compared to the total number of people who saw the video thumbnail or preview. Retention: a percentage representing the amount of time people spend consuming your video — for example, an audience retention rate of 65% means that on average, viewers watch 65% of your video. Subscriber: those who sign up to your channel get notified of your new content and are more likely to watch your new videos. Thumbnail: the image that previews a YouTube video, used to grab viewers’ attention and entice them to watch a video with relevant images and key words. Views: on YouTube, each time a user plays a video and watches it for at least 30 seconds — if a user watches a video multiple times, each time is counted as a new view. Watch Time: the total amount of time viewers spend watching a video — high watch times indicate that viewers are engaged with the content. YouTube Title: the name of your YouTube video that helps viewers decide if they want to watch — it is also a piece of metadata that determines how YouTube’s algorithm recommends videos to viewers. Accessibility Audio Description: also referred to as a video description, described video or visual description, a form of narration used to provide information surrounding key visual elements in a media work primarily for the benefit of blind and low-vision consumers — audio description can also benefit students, language learners, multitaskers and people who are neurodivergent. Captions: time-synchronized transcription of an audio track (including both speech and nonspeech elements) and can be read while watching video content — captions may be open (always on) or closed (able to be turned on or off) and allow the D/deaf and hard of hearing community to access content; like audio description, captions have other use cases as well, including access for people who want or need to watch content with the sound off. On-Screen ASL: American Sign Language (ASL) interpretation of content that appears on screen — for many people who are Deaf in the United States, ASL is their native language, and translation from English can be essential for full comprehension. Subtitles: time-synchronized translation of another language designed to support comprehension for hearing audiences who are nonspeakers — for PBS, any non-English dialogue must have English subtitles, but unlike captions, subtitles do not include nonspeech elements. Transcripts: a written text version of the audio content in a video.

# Page 11^page=11
20 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 21 Introducing a “never-done-before” project or initiative to your station and developing new workflows for them can be as challenging as it is rewarding. As digital technologies continue to rapidly evolve, public media must adapt and learn how to serve a wide range of audiences with content that reflects their lived experiences on platforms that resonate. Executive leaders, such as general managers, chief content officers, senior directors, executive producers and so on are essential to effective digital transformation because these roles oversee the creation and completion of digital strategic priorities and projects. You can have the best creative team, but without executive buy-in, projects falter and innovation stalls. As you dive in, remember: 1. Communicate often that the digital project is a strategic priority for your station. Create the expectation that digital projects are a shared station journey. Show how the work is contributing to overarching goals. Provide progress updates at All Staffs, and ask team leads to share key learnings so all station staff learn along the way. Celebrate wins together, and make it clear the success belongs to everyone. 2. Ensure the project team(s) have the necessary resources to implement a digital project. Digital leverages similar skill sets as broadcast but is a different medium. Think about how you can set teams up for success, remove barriers and resolve conflict to improve employee experience and output. 3. Digital is change management. It takes time to transform your station’s broadcast workflow into a digital-friendly one. Prepare to practice over and over to become a digital expert. Intentionally coach teams to train new and veteran staff to build new skills, and support them when they encounter challenges. By equipping yourself with essential leadership strategies and digital production foundations, you can effectively champion your teams and clear blockers from their path to set them up for success. This guidebook includes proven examples and case studies from successful station projects to inspire and help you get there. Good luck! Some chapters will be more relevant than others based on your role. Here is a customized list of key chapters for executive leadership to prioritize reading first to best support your teams. TITLE PAGE Leadership Strategies (All Chapters) 30 The PBS Audience 66 Crafting an Audience Strategy 70 Pitching Potential Funders and Distributors 140 Digital Workflow 101 146 Building a Digital Budget 150 Closing a Project and Looking Ahead 190 Understanding the YouTube Landscape 200 Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck 206 Digital Production Team Structure 212 Fostering Belonging Across Production 218 Executive Leadership Key Stakeholders

# Page 12^page=12
22 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 23 Public media is ripe with so many potential ideas for incredible digital projects. By leveraging best practices, stations can fully embrace digital and turn these ideas into reality. However, like broadcast, it requires a team and key creative personnel to band together to make the work shine. Key creative personnel includes the staff responsible for executing the vision of the show and could include a chief creative officer, executive producer, director of content, series director, producers, script writer or researcher, director of photography and editors or animators. As you dive in, remember: 1. Be true to your audience. Digital producers must be insatiably curious about and devoted to their audience. Building a reliable fanbase takes time but is accomplished by being consistent, knowledgeable and enthusiastic about your chosen target audience: their likes, dislikes and priorities. Making something for everyone is not a strategy. Leverage your audience or marketing teams to zero in on who they are and how you will reach them. 2. Scripting is essential. Digital edutainment thrives with a punchy, well-researched script that frontloads the awesome. A strong episode thesis question will give your script — and the rest of the production — a true north to follow. If you lose your way while making the story, so will the audience. Abandon “finding the story in the edit,” and replace it with tight scripting to create a stronger story and save precious time and resources. 3. Iterate in real time. Digital is about constantly learning and improving, so don’t wait until the end to change things for the better. If you or a team member get stuck, help each other find a solution together. Remember that digital timelines are fast, so every second counts. The first few episodes will be a mad dash, but with practice, iteration and collaboration, you will find your rhythm. To make things easier, this guidebook includes proven examples and case studies from successful station projects to inspire and help you stick the landing. Good luck! Some chapters will be more relevant than others based on your role. Here is a customized list of key chapters for key creative personnel to prioritize reading first to best support your teams. TITLE PAGE Securing Strategic Alignment 32 Cultivating Audience (All Chapters) 64 Pitching Potential Funders and Distributors 140 Preparing for Series Deliverables 178 Creating Series Branding and Key Art 182 Digital Storytelling (All Chapters) 198 Expanding Impact (All Chapters) 282 Key Creative Personnel Key Stakeholders

# Page 13^page=13
24 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 25 Production Management and Operations Fun Fact: The secret ingredient to digital is workflow! Digital workflow provides essential frameworks and tools to pave a path that allows you to take content risks to transform your station into a digital-first and multiplatform enterprise. Balancing consistency and routine in your production process while encouraging experimentation can be a challenge, but strong production management skills can help identify wins and blockers that can be shared with station leadership to inform overall strategy. The people leading this charge are often chief operating officers, production managers, coordinating producers, operations leads and media asset managers. As you begin, remember: 1. Adapting workflow is a culture shift. Change always brings challenges, which makes buy-in and constant communication critical to your success. You cannot transform your station’s digital process alone — it’s a team effort with executive buy-in! But you can develop a digital-first mindset, communicate clearly and often, and cultivate a solid logistical infrastructure to develop digital projects. 2. You can’t do it by sheer force of will. Long gone are the days of a one-person band taking on digital projects. Although digital platforms offer accessibility and flexibility, the production process still requires meticulous planning, scripting, filming and editing to maintain high-quality standards, all of which require a strong team, including production management and operations. 3. Digital requires resiliency, patience and great advocates. Your first project may not be a viral hit — few are. But it will be a critical testing ground for experimentation and learning what works and what doesn’t. Unlike broadcast’s one-way conversation, digital allows creators to talk to their audience in real time. However, it takes time to build a loyal following and great process. Bring people into your circle who understand the stakes, logistics and runway. Equipping yourself and your teams with foundational project management tools helps lead your station into a successful digital era. Leverage your production management, finance and legal departments and production staff to get things in order, ask questions and find answers together. You’ve got this! Some chapters will be more relevant than others based on your role. Here is a customized list of key chapters for production management and operations to prioritize reading first. TITLE PAGE Obtaining Stakeholder Buy-in 42 Managing Group Dynamics 47 Embracing Change 51 Crafting an Audience Strategy 70 Making Great Vertical Video 110 Reimagining Workflow (All Chapters) 138 Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck 206 Digital Production Team Structure 212 PBS Standards & Practices: Compliance and Fact-Checking 252 Building a Digital Editorial Timeline 258 Conducting a Show Evaluation 276 Key Stakeholders

# Page 14^page=14
26 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 27 Successful digital productions require strong, integrated teams to reach their goals. Marketing and fundraising are essential for the long-term sustainability of digital content. You are masterful storytellers in your own way, communicating the impact of your station’s work to existing and new target audiences, including members and donors. The people leading this charge in marketing often include a chief marketing officer, director of marketing, event managers and marketing, communications or digital specialists. In fundraising, these roles include vice president of development, development officers, partner managers and sponsorship roles. 1. Get involved and stay involved. Digital is a fast-moving enterprise, and it’s important your teams are accounted for in budgets and project meetings from the get-go. Being involved at the beginning means you can articulate your team’s needs, influence workflow and advocate for opportunities in real time comfortably and confidently throughout the project. Remember many project members may be diving into digital for the first time — help each other. 2. Digital series require clear marketing and fundraising strategies and follow-through to identify, find, retain and grow target audiences into loyal subscribers — and then keep making content for them. Who are you making this series for? Are you meeting your audience where they are? Do you know what they’re interested in? How will people find your show? Are you talking about it at events and with potential funding partners? Are you thinking outside the box on how to expand impact? Get the word out and keep it buzzing. 3. Tell the story of the work internally and externally. Your teams are messaging powerhouses. You can support the project teams by regularly sharing the importance and effectiveness of the station or series digital strategy — and its opportunities! — with stakeholders. Likewise, you can leverage similar strategies for reaching target audiences and making data-informed decisions in your own work. You are a vital part of making your station’s content grow and thrive. Be collaborative and build a solid foundation for your piece of the digital puzzle. Marketing and Fundraising Some chapters will be more relevant than others based on your role. Here is a customized list of key chapters for marketing and fundraising to prioritize reading first. 28 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 29 Public media needs digital enthusiasts in every area of our work, so don’t worry if the other key stakeholder sections don’t feel quite right yet — there is a place for you. Like broadcast, digital comprises many transferable skills and areas of expertise. For example, see the following: ● Did your engineering days make you a whiz at technical specs and data-informed decision making? Analytics is waiting for you! ● Do you love graphic design? Creating branding or thumbnails is a great place to start. ● Are you on the marketing team but want to jump into content? Scripting videos or episode thesis statements may be your next strong suit! ● Is your sales portfolio strictly broadcast but you are a dedicated YouTube viewer? Outlining a potential sales pitch deck to fund a digital project could be the next step. Whether you’re starting out in public media and eager to be part of its digital future or migrating after a storied broadcast career, there are three key principles that should guide your digital development: 1. Learn everything you can about audience development. Digital relies on understanding your target audience. The more you are familiar with audience development best practices, the stronger your ideas will be for any future project or new role. 2. Embrace lifelong learning. Digital is all about iteration — for instance, learning from the last episode or series and making decisions that will improve outcomes. What excites you in this guidebook most? Follow that passion. What SMART goal can help you learn and practice a new skill? Who can help you achieve your desired outcome? Embrace a growth mindset, and keep learning new digital skills. 3. Digital is fast, but growth takes time. Your station’s strategy will grow and change with time and practice. Be patient with yourself and your colleagues while holding each other accountable. Identify what skills you already have that could help your station, and take strategic, manageable steps to reach your goals. Stay curious and enthusiastic as you grow. You’ve got this! 32 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 33 Securing Strategic Alignment Every station has a strategy. Strategies are important so that station projects can succeed, engage and connect with the community and retain audiences. As you begin to create and launch new digital productions, it is important that they align with that strategy and the needs of your station. Then, you can leverage any new content or workflows you create to fill these “needs” or “gaps” and set the station up for success and strategic alignment. This section is a brief overview of business tools that you can use to meet needs and identify gaps in your station. For additional information, read the Business Tools course in the PBS Course Catalog. Assessing Station and Audience Needs Before you can outline a concept for a new show or project, it’s important to understand if it aligns with the needs of your station and your audience. Let’s dive into some tools to use with colleagues and leaders across the station. THE IMPACT EFFORT MATRIX Using a tool called the Impact Effort Matrix, you can evaluate the effort and resulting impact of any task at your station. Does the impact on the audience justify the amount of work you are doing? Maybe you have a digital series that has been on for years and the production is overwhelmingly extensive, but your audience just isn’t there. Leadership is trying to decide whether that show is worth the investment. The Impact Effort Matrix considers what level of effort certain projects or tasks take and what the resulting impact is. This helps people identify whether these projects or tasks should continue, change or be put on the back burner. An important step can be naming out loud what has historically “always been done.” This can open discussion for new or different approaches and encourages teams to reach an informed conclusion about what should be done. These are the steps or tasks or projects that take low effort but have high impact. SWOT VERSUS SOAR Alternatively, maybe you have a great idea but aren’t sure it aligns with the strengths or goals of the station. How do you determine if it aligns? Is there community competition with the concept you have identified? Are there other series concepts or ideas that could meet the goals of your station instead? Use the SWOT and/or SOAR analyses tools to answer these questions. ● SWOT - (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities and Threats) helps an organization evaluate its core strengths and weaknesses while also identifying how to use those to capitalize on opportunities and navigate any threats present in the community or system. ● SOAR - (Strengths, Opportunities, Aspirations and Results) helps you focus on your station’s strengths and how they align with your station’s needs and future goals. After completing these, you will have concrete examples outlining areas of high impact and low effort as well as strengths and weaknesses. FORCE-FIELD ANALYSIS Moving from assessment to workflow is the next step. Effectively changing to overcome weaknesses and meet aspirations through new content or workflow is where the Force-Field Analysis comes in. This analysis helps outline what resources can be used to create positive change with a specific goal and what resources are needed to overcome any barriers. It identifies what may need to change to meet the goal at hand and who or what might be preventing that change. Leadership Strategies

# Page 18^page=18
34 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 35 REFLECTION Station Name: South Carolina ETV Author Name: Tabitha Safdi South Carolina Educational Television (SCETV) employs a sprint-based approach to brainstorming for internal needs assessments, focusing on team development, content and organizational processes. Our “why” for using this method is to ensure inclusivity, focus and actionable outcomes. Recently, we applied this approach to develop a new content series aimed at engaging younger audiences in South Carolina. Our brainstorming process is very specific. 1. Location: The process begins by gathering relevant stakeholders, often in off-site sessions to stimulate creativity with a new setting. 2. Define purpose: The purpose of the session is clearly defined to keep discussions focused. As an example, we held a session and defined the purpose as working to create an engaging content framework that could resonate with younger audiences. We clearly defined the objectives: identify key themes, preferred formats and critical message points to make SCETV content relatable and appealing to a younger demographic. 3. Utilize sprints: SCETV uses timed sprints in which each participant writes down their ideas on sticky notes – one idea per note – ensuring everyone’s voice is heard. We emphasized that no idea was too small or too unconventional, fostering a free-flowing creative environment. 4. Clustering and analyzing: The sticky notes were then grouped into themes, such as “engagement through social media,” “youth-centric storytelling” and “interactive digital formats.” We discussed each cluster as a team, prioritizing themes that aligned with our target audience’s preferences. This inclusive approach surfaced diverse perspectives and gave the team a collective sense of ownership, which led to more relevant and innovative solutions. Outcome: This session enabled SCETV to map out a fresh content strategy targeting younger audiences, focusing on short-form video, interactive elements and platforms like Instagram and YouTube. The new approach not only strengthened the team’s cohesion but also resulted in a vertical video plan for one of our digital series that is now being tested for broader rollout to other series. Through this structured process, SCETV continues to innovate and adapt its content, staying relevant to all age groups in the South Carolina community. Conducting an Internal Needs Assessment at SCETV CASE STUDY Reflect on each of the tools discussed in this chapter: the Impact Effort Matrix, SWOT, SOAR and Force-Field Analysis. Questions to consider: 1. Which one might be most valuable to you in your work and why? 2. Is there one that stands out to you most? What do you like about it? 3. What might be a next step you can take to ensure strategic alignment with station goals? FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY Parting Thoughts As a leader conducting these analyses, it is important to bring together the appropriate station stakeholders and staff. Be sure to set clear expectations and an estimated timeline, and leave with a shared understanding of next steps. Each of these can take as long or as little as needed. Use what you learned to fill gaps and, by extension, meet the needs of your audiences. As you do this, take time to show one another your analyses. Discuss how you are applying what you learned in the creation of new projects. How you do this is up to you, but remember, this work cannot be done in a silo. We must grow, change and adapt — together! For additional resources on business tools and strategies, take the Business Tools course in the PBS Course Catalog. Leadership Strategies | Securing Strategic Alignment DIVE DEEPER

36 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 37 Vision Planning and Goal Setting Set Yourself Up for Success With a Clear Vision Plan and Goals With every project, it’s important to set you and your team up for success. Success means achieving the goals you’ve set for yourself and your team, aligned with your values and the organization’s objectives, while overcoming challenges you have identified. As a leader, setting yourself up for success might mean launching a new program with a set number of views to reach, establishing a project workflow with certain project deliverables or leveraging the gaps you’ve identified to meet your audiences. Ultimately, you define what success looks like. With strategic planning and reflection, you can better grasp what it means for you as a leader and be able to effectively see it through. Before beginning this chapter, complete the Securing Strategic Alignment chapter to evaluate where your station may be encountering challenges in meeting certain audiences or needs. Identifying Organizational and Team Values STEP 1: IDENTIFY YOUR VALUES Intentionally responding to your specific situation requires self-reflection on your values, vision and goals to understand your drivers, blockers and opportunities and how your team can navigate them. Understand individual, departmental and organizational values. ● Identify individual values: Reflect on what matters most to you, the beliefs that are most important and what fills you with purpose. Using the guided value process 1, identify two core values. ● Understand organizational values: Oftentimes, departments and organizations write out their values and missions in employee materials. Locate those documents and review them. What stands out to you? ● Align core values: Next, evaluate the alignment (or lack thereof) between your own values and the work you are doing. What overlap is there? Where do values differ? Are there opportunities to realign the work back to core values? The alignment pockets are what will continue to motivate you and your teams. Lean into them! STEP 2: ALIGN VISION WITH VALUES Use a strategic vision statement to identify your vision according to three key parts: Drivers: A perceived future that factors in the present; this requires a form of action or change Assumptions: What you know and believe to be true in this future that will help or hinder your vision statement Solutions: Your idea for success by leveraging the driver while accounting for assumptions Review examples of drivers, assumptions and solutions below. ● Drivers: What is driving the need to change or adjust? h Example: Broadcast viewership is declining, and streaming on YouTube or other channels is becoming vital to continue to engage with audiences. ● Assumptions: What are the assumed “knowns” about this driver/change? h Example: Our audiences on broadcast will continue to decline. h Example: Streaming audiences will be harder to reach because there is too much noise in the streaming environment. ● Solutions: What is the proposed change that responds to the forces and overcomes the “knowns”? h Example: Because broadcast viewership will continue to decline across public media, ABCD must expand multiplatform offerings and marketing efforts to engage with new audiences and retain current audiences. Remember, all goals set should tie back to the core values and overall vision for success. Now, outline your own strategic vision statement for a specific initiative or program (i.e., a change) using the three goalposts. Leadership Strategies

# Page 20^page=20
38 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 39 STEP 3: SET TARGET GOAL(S) Select a target goal 2 that inspires you, aligns with the core values and will motivate you to meet the vision. Learn more about various goal-setting strategies using What You Need to Know About Goal-Setting in the PBS Course Catalog to draft your own. REFLECTION Begin the goal-setting process by reflecting on three questions: Question 1: Does the goal inspire me? Question 2: What is my motivation behind achieving this goal? Question 3: Will I be present throughout the process? STEP 4: CREATE AN ACTION PL AN TO REACH THE GOAL Effectively communicating the plan to reach the goal is critical to measure output and impact. What specific measurements or benchmarks indicate you have met your goal? Don’t think about these metrics in a binary manner of “good versus bad.” Instead, consider, “What milestones will indicate that we or I have improved or met my goal?” This will help you identify a metric or benchmark to refer to that is founded in a growth mindset. Clearly communicate the plan to your team and make sure everyone understands the goals, their metrics and key performance indicators (KPIs) and any tasks they are responsible for completing. Success is a constant process of iteration and growth, not a quick checkmark on one goal or metric. Check in on your goals or metrics and evaluate them periodically. How will you continue to move forward with a new strategy, goal or metric? What feedback can your team provide to make meeting goals and metrics more effective or efficient? How will you build on the goals or plan at each check-in point? Growth is active, not stagnant! Case Study Series: Flyover Culture Station Name: WTIU Public Television Author Name: Payton Whaley WTIU’s main goal in creating “Flyover Culture” was to make a YouTube series. And with that answer came plenty of questions: ● What audience tends to watch YouTube more than any other platform? ● Where would they be compared to our broadcast footprint? ● What types of shows and creators interest them? “Flyover Culture” Vision, Goals and Data-Informed Decisions CASE STUDY YouTube has no shortage of avenues to explore regarding genre, length, format or any other factors. But you need to lock into a niche or you’ll be making content for everyone (and, by extension, no one). We found that niche to be “Midwestern pop-culture enthusiasts under 40.” From there, one question comes up with every episode: “Is this the kind of show they’d want to watch?” It’s crucial to keep those goals attainable. You won’t get a million subscribers out the gate, so it’s much better to grab 200 people and be their new favorite channel. Leadership Strategies | Vision Planning and Goal Setting DIVE DEEPER Additional information on how to make informed and data-based decisions can be found in the PBS Course Catalog under the Data-Driven Decision-Making Learning Path.

40 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 41 Parting Thoughts Success is rarely one-size-fits-all. Clearly defining what success means to you as a leader ensures your team can thrive with any project or new process. This clarity ensures the work is value-driven and can achieve your goals, meeting the metrics you’ve set together. Now It’s Your Turn! Create a vision using the vision template. 3. Set your SMART goals. REFLECTION Question 1: Do the values, vision and goals align? Question 2: Are there any areas where they do not align? 42 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 43 Building a Stakeholder Power Map Obtaining Stakeholder Buy-In Leadership Strategies When it comes to digital transformation and related projects, getting buy-in and setting an actionable plan are crucial elements to ensure alignment, commitment and a clear road map to achieve the overall vision and goals. This section will outline strategies for gaining buy-in and setting a detailed, concrete plan of action together. PRACTICE ACTIVITY TAKEAWAY TASK STEP 1: DEFINE THE PURP OSE OF THE MAP What do you need to get out of the process? This will inform the type of map you create. For example, if you need to… …increase stakeholder visibility, choose a sociogram. …get a stalled project back on track, a force-field analysis could be a good start. …understand stakeholder dependencies, try using a network diagram. …improve stakeholder communication and engagement, the power-interest grid is a valuable tool. STEP 2: IDENTIFY KEY STAKEHOLDERS Create a list of everyone connected to or involved in your project. Include folks across levels of power and interest. STEP 3: DETERMINE EACH STAKEHOLDER’S INVOLVEMENT AND INTEREST. Consider their needs and priorities. A one-on-one conversation is a great way to glean this. STEP 4: ENGAGE STAKEHOLDERS Look at your map and outline how you plan to engage and communicate with each stakeholder throughout the project. Be sure to include the information they need from you, how often updates will be provided and the best or preferred way to reach them. STEP 5: REFERENCE YOUR MAP Refer to your initial drafted stakeholder map throughout this chapter and your project. Is any information missing or changed? What have you learned that should be included to obtain leadership buy-in for your digital project? Guidebook users will be able to identify different stakeholders and clearly communicate how they are impacted by the work and how the stakeholder can create impact for the work. Build a stakeholder power map. A stakeholder power map identifies key stakeholders, outlines their influence and interest, contextualizes the scope of involvement and indicates how to engage them for your digital project. This step positions you as a leader who has drafted an actionable plan from which to build off. A stakeholder power map will likely look different for each group and project. 1. Review the stakeholder map options from Monday.com, a project management software. 2. Choose the right stakeholder mapping technique 3. 3. Follow the steps to create your own stakeholder power map.

# Page 23^page=23
44 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 45 Align your project with the needs of others and the organization. Understanding how the project benefits their own business priorities is a critical step to gaining support. Executives must prioritize the needs of the organization, so communicate how it helps meet those needs specifically. h Example: Explain clearly how the digital project will meet station needs and tell the story of how it happens. Will it reach diverse audiences, their fundraising goals, etc.? Follow up as the project progresses and meets those goals! STEP 3: DESIGNING AN ACTIONABLE PL AN There are many ways to set an actionable project plan, but every plan should include the following. Notice how these items build on lesson outputs from the previous chapters. ● A strong vision statement c A broad goal that provides a solution to a particular driver, whether a challenge or opportunity ● Clear objectives c Tactical goals that accomplish a portion of the vision statement ● Specific tactics c Steps taken to achieve the objective; there can be multiple steps per objective, but they should be as focused as possible ● Targets such as internal and/or external audiences or stakeholders c Groups or persons intended to be affected by the tactic ● Clear and actionable key performance indicators (KPIs) c The measure of a tactic’s progress toward completing an objective c Another type of performance evaluation is OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) 4. Not to be confused with KPIs, these action-oriented statements keep you on track by identifying the target and associated metrics. ● Evaluation mechanisms to review outcomes and set adjustments. With a clear action plan in place, consistently review the KPIs and evaluate the progress toward completing your overarching goals. Do not wait until the end of a project to evaluate whether it was successful. Instead, host short, evaluative conversations throughout the project to discern where you can improve or where you can celebrate wins — big or small! This will not only allow you to achieve your goal but also help solidify your role as a leader through trust, active listening, validation and follow-through. With your stakeholder power map in hand, the next step is to get support from internal or external stakeholders, and then set a concrete plan of action. Our brainstorming process is very specific. STEP 1: OBTAINING SUPP ORT AND BUY-IN Obtaining support and buy-in for new projects can be challenging, especially when the individuals you’re working with are at different levels or functional areas. To successfully get the support you need, it is important to employ effective strategies to influence these stakeholders. To influence others, you must position yourself as an informal leader by motivating colleagues and becoming an expert in a specific area. This way, you can earn their trust and support. Creating a Clear Action Plan STEP 2: P OSITIONING YOURSELF AS A LEADER Build connections with your colleagues, and allow them to get to know you. h Example: Learn about their role at the station and how that role helps the station succeed. Be an active listener, and give others your undivided attention. Repeat back to them what they say to ensure you are hearing them correctly. Individuals are more likely to support you if they feel respected and know their voices are being heard. h Example: Discuss how the public media system’s needs affect them professionally or personally. Understand and leverage that to motivate the work ahead. Be aware of your verbal and nonverbal cues. Use welcoming body language and confident and friendly verbal cues. h Example: Maintain open communication and reiterate how being involved in this project aligns with their aspirations. Articulate your genuine interest in their thoughts and opinions. Thank them for their time. h Example: Invite them into the process. Seek out their feedback. Obtain their advice on how to solve issues. Continue to thank them for their assistance. Develop expertise as it relates to your specific project, and display that knowledge through conversations (with research, questions, etc.) to illustrate you are trustworthy and deserving of their support in this area. h Example: Do research on the media landscape, your community, the station and the overarching public media system before moving forward with the project. Leadership Strategies | Obtaining Stakeholder Buy-In PRACTICE ACTIVITY

# Page 24^page=24
46 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 47 Parting Thoughts Ensuring the success of your project hinges on securing support from internal stakeholders and establishing a concrete action plan. By positioning yourself as an informal leader, building trust and aligning your project’s goals with the needs of the organization, you can garner the necessary buy-in. Then, by crafting a comprehensive plan with a clear vision, objectives, tactics and measurable KPIs, you will have an actionable road map to guide your project to successful completion. Together, these steps will help you navigate challenges and achieve your project’s goals effectively. For more on strategic project planning or strategy building, check out the Introduction to Project Planning course available in the PBS Course Catalog. DIVE DEEPER Leadership Strategies | Obtaining Stakeholder Buy-In Utilizing Stages of Group Development Theory Leading a group is challenging, especially when you know every single person will have a different perspective, skill set and goal. Knowing this ahead of time is important. As the leader, you can pay attention to the dynamics of the group and respond strategically when issues or successes arise. THE FIVE STAGES OF GROUP DEVELOPMENT THEORY To be most effective, utilize Stages of Group Development Theory 5, which outlines the five stages that a group goes through when working on a goal together. As you review each stage, consider this compilation of real-life scenarios and reflect on the strategies you could use to move to the next stage: h Scenario: A group of ten staff are brought together to develop a ten-episode digital-first series. Each team member is from a different department. The series will go behind the scenes on historical landmarks, how they came to be and what they mean today. The station’s General Manager gave this group the assignment and has now handed off the planning process. The team is responsible for getting this series off the ground. Managing Group Dynamics Leading by Example and Cultivating Positive Collaboration “How can I collaborate effectively with individuals in my organization who stretch across different generations, communication styles and disciplines?” This is the million-dollar question. Leading a new project, series or initiative is both exciting and nerve-racking, especially when bringing together colleagues from many different departments or geographic locations. This section will dive into group dynamics theory and subsequent strategies to give you tools to lead more effectively, cultivate positive collaborations and bring a group together across any project. Leadership Strategies

# Page 25^page=25
48 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 49 • Collaborative Excellence course in the PBS Course Catalog • Small Group Development Theory Chart Outline • Team Norms & Styles Deck Template DIVE DEEPER Explore these additional resources for more information: Performing: This is where groups can leverage the structures set in the past three stages to reach their goal without any challenges or issues. This is everyone’s favorite time. Leader Strategy: Take time to highlight all the progress the group (individually and together) has made. Share kudos with them in the way they prefer — some people like group shout-outs, and others prefer private compliments. h Scenario: Hurray! The team has successfully met their goal: the digital series is almost finished publishing and their rolling marketing strategy for YouTube and social channels went relatively smoothly. Before the debrief, the team will meet with the General Manager to highlight their successes as a group. Celebrating small and big wins together has everyone excited, and after writing down their individual wins they shout out other contributors’ work that made the project successful. The General Manager is pleased and the team feels uplifted by their peers. Adjourning: This is when the group has reached its goal and is getting ready to dissolve, also known as “breaking up.” Before group members officially part ways, pause and reflect on the successes or challenges. Celebrate all the work that has been done with one another! Leader Strategy: Conduct a thoughtful, productive postmortem with the group. Finish any reporting to stakeholders. Finally, celebrate all that has been done. A genuine “Thank you!” can go a long way. h Scenario: The digital series has wrapped and the team has collected all necessary data, outcomes and suggestions for the upcoming debrief presentation to the General Manager. Everyone is in good spirits, even knowing they encountered challenges along the way. They met their goal of producing a digital-first series with good audience engagement, plus a bonus: they built a new collaborative strategy within the station. Forming: This is the orientation stage. People are both anxious and excited to start working together. It is also when the leader is heavily relied on to set rules, expectations and the overarching goals. This stage can last for a short or long period of time as people get to know one another and outline roles or responsibilities. Leader Strategy: Set aside plenty of time for the group to get to know one another through icebreakers. Share communication style preferences. Ensure that the project or goal is explicitly explained and confirm that everyone is on the same page before stepping away. h Scenario: The ten-person group is uncomfortable with one another and has no digital workflow(s) in place, so they feel stalled. To get started, they will need to spend more time on teambuilding so everyone can share their expectations, skills and relevant experience. Together, they can align project goals and responsibilities to individual needs and wants. Storming: This is where many groups fail, because they begin to challenge the boundaries that were set in the forming stage, arguing over preferred working or communication styles. Ultimately, they begin to question the worth of the group’s initial goal. Leader Strategy: Encourage the group to participate in deeper icebreakers like personality or leadership assessments so they can use that information to better assign responsibilities and outline effective communication strategies. Shepherd the group through the conflict, answer their questions and reiterate the importance of the goal at hand. h Scenario: With connections built and clear responsibilities, the team kicked off the digital series. But now, some team members feel their communication methods and project management skills would be a better fit than others’. Various department staff are jockeying for authority over the project, wanting to “own” it. Remember conflicts are natural at this stage! This is a good time to have an open discussion led by the General Manager, who can reorient the group to the task at hand. Norming: This is when a group begins to gel. They work through their differences, appreciate the strengths of others in the group and respect the authority of the group leader. Groups are now comfortable socializing and asking for help or feedback. It’s common at this stage to fall back into storming when new tasks arise. This is a normal part of the process! Leader Strategy: Pay attention to the challenges or conflicts that may arise and help the group work through them. Find time for the group to socialize and build stronger relationships so they can manage conflicts on their own in the future. h Scenario: The open conversation led by the General Manager identified a strong communication rollout and workflow the team could rally behind. With this new plan in place, staff aligned themselves in roles and tasks that celebrated their skills and knowledge. With clear expectations and resolved tensions, the group began to bond outside the project: catching up at lunch, grabbing coffee and casually brainstorming. Issues begin to fade. Leadership Strategies | Managing Group Dynamics

# Page 26^page=26
50 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 51 Embracing Change How to Lead by Example Through Change and Challenges As you create new digital productions or lead new digital initiatives, it is common to face challenges, especially when driving transformational changes. Leaders frequently face conflicts and resistance — it’s part of driving change. Navigating these challenges, whether through new projects or organizational shifts, can be daunting. But with the right tools, you can manage these obstacles effectively. This chapter will help you become a change agent who advocates and leads confidently into the unknown. Change Is Constant Change is the process of stopping one way of working and starting a new way of working. For change agents or leaders, this is an exciting time because you are starting new projects and looking for ways to improve the organization. For change participants, this can be a scary experience that brings up feelings of fear, failure and worry about the future. REFLECT ON YOUR MOST RECENT COMPLETED PROJECT Reflection Parting Thoughts If you read through the scenario and stages and thought, “Wow, that happened to me!” or realized, “I’ve seen that happen at my station before,” know you are not alone. While the scenario is derived from a compilation of station experiences, it is an example of what can and does happen across the station system every day. Collaboration always comes with challenges, especially when beginning a new project or launching a new digital series. As the leader, take the time to prepare and leverage this newfound knowledge to ensure your teams can rely on you to lead and coach them through any group dynamics that may arise. This chapter includes content from the Collaborate With Your Team Learning Path on the PBS Course Catalog. Leadership Strategies | Managing Group Dynamics PRACTICE ACTIVITY REFLECTION Question 1: What was a group challenge you encountered? Question 2: What stage would you label it? Question 3: Now knowing the five stages, how do you think it could have been solved? Leadership Strategies

# Page 27^page=27
52 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 53 Guidebook users should be able to apply the stages of change to any relevant scenario. Now that you have read about common stages of change, reflect on a recent change in your own life. Question 1: What was the change? Question 2: How and why did you react that way to it? Using the Stages of Change STEP 1: ACTIVATING STAKEHOLDER SUPP ORT Be prepared to leverage executive and team support, which you secured in Obtaining Stakeholder Buy-In, before beginning a new project (a.k.a. change). STEP 2: LEVERAGE CHANGE FRAMEWORKS Leverage the Model of Change Management by Kurt Lewin, 7 which consists of three key stages: 1. Unfreeze: preparing for the desired change h Change agent role — motivate individuals and focus on rebuilding trust among your colleagues 2. Transition: implementing the desired change h Change agent role — explain the benefit of the change, why it is occurring and how it directly impacts them or their role 3. Refreeze: solidifying the desired change h Change agent role — celebrate the successes of the change and the effort the team has put into it 6 Steps to Manage Professional Change TAKEAWAY REFLECTION PRACTICE ACTIVITYSTAGES OF CHANGE One of the most common theories representing stages of change is the Kübler-Ross Change Curve. 6 People experience a variety of emotions when faced with change. Some individuals approach certain changes quickly and enthusiastically, whereas others may need time to process their emotions before moving forward in the curve. Both experiences are valid and normal responses to change. As a people manager or project leader, it is important to remember everyone goes through their own curve of emotions and therefore everyone may need slightly different support to fully embrace a new change. The Kübler-Ross Change Curve milestones include: 1. Denial: An immediate refusal to accept the situation at hand. 2. Resistance (aka change aversion): People develop stress and even anger toward the fact that things are changing and growing into new ways of working. 3. Exploration: People begin to explore the new possibilities and new opportunities and begin to look at the good that may come from the change. 4. Acceptance and New Commitment: People have accepted the change and have gone through an emotional shift from anxiety to renewed self-confidence. Hopefully, there were a few “ah ha” moments while reading. Resistors need help to fully comprehend why a change (the new project) is happening, why it is important and why they should look forward to it. It is important to note that everyone can be a potential resistor in the change process. As a change agent, your role is to understand the complexity of these stages and be able to support change participants as they move through their own change curve from denial and resistance to exploration and acceptance. Leadership Strategies | Embracing Change

# Page 28^page=28
54 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 55 Dealing With Change and Conflict CASE STUDY Station Name: Public Media Connect Author Name: Kellie May “Change can make people anxious.” “You have to help them through it.” Parting Thoughts Change is inevitable, but it doesn’t have to be chaotic. Build a foundation that embraces change and is open to resistance, celebrates excitement, fosters more productive conversations and alleviates individual stress before progressing with any goals or projects. When we were in the process of creating our station’s first digital strategic plan, we ran into some resistance from the staff — not because people didn’t want to embrace digital initiatives but because they were worried about the change those projects would bring to their day-to-day work. Having a task force that represented every department truly involved in the creation of the plan made a huge difference. We met monthly for a year as a group, and I held individual and department meetings where I actively listened to staff concerns, making sure every voice was heard, and then worked with the task force to make real adjustments to the plan along the way. By focusing on getting buy-in from the staff and leadership team and making the plan a truly collaborative document, we were able to help people overcome those fears and embrace the future. STEP 3: FACILITATE DIALOGUE Host a conversation with your colleagues to evaluate where their emotions or fears stem from. Use tools like the Force-Field Analysis or SWOT to evaluate how a change will strengthen the organization and how you’ll work together to overcome the expected weaknesses or leverage the station’s opportunities. Read the Securing Strategic Alignment chapter to learn about these tools. STEP 4: UNDERSTAND GROUP DYNAMICS Engage in a group discussion about generational motivations and conflict styles. Spend time learning about one another’s motivation and conflict style. Discuss how that could impact an individual’s desire to effectively move through change. Learn about these styles by paying for the Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument (TKI) assessment. 8 Leadership Strategies | Embracing Change STEP 5: NAVIGATE RESISTANCE Expect resistance and accept it. Pay attention to the dynamics of the group and consider where the aversion is coming from and why. Read more in the Managing Group Dynamics chapter. You can’t fight resistance — and you shouldn’t. But you can guide individuals through the process by being clear about the big picture, communicating often and being consistent with your actions and behaviors through the change process. STEP 6: REINFORCE THE CHANGE Continually name the change and provide a safe space for processing. It is important to name the change you are going through while reiterating the why. By doing this, you reinforce what is occurring (physically, emotionally, etc.) for the individuals involved and work to develop a safe space where individuals can express and process the emotions they experience throughout the change process. This helps not only reduce resistance but also provide the motivation for individuals to work through emotions and transition into the “refreeze” stage mentioned in Step 2. Tip: Each generation has different motivations that guide them in their work and through conflict or change. Consider how these can impact the change process — positively or negatively. Tip: Conflict styles are like a pie. Everyone uses all conflict styles in their life but likely are inclined toward one, whether due to position, power or life stage. TKI helps you consider what style will be most effective in which setting and with whom.
