56 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 57 Five Key Steps to Close the Loop STEP 1: REVIEW ORIGINAL GOAL(S) AND STRATEGIES. Reflect on the data available to measure these goals and how you will evaluate their success, failure or progress. Consider questions like the following: ● How should I collect data for this goal and subgoals? ● What type of data should I collect? ● How will that data help in my evaluation? It is important to answer these questions early and often. Don’t forget to determine a data collection and review process at the start of your project so that you can share information internally and respond to issues or feedback as they arise. STEP 2: COLLECT, DISTRIBUTE AND ANALYZE THE AVAIL ABLE DATA. Identify who you should share it with and in what form and for which stakeholders. Consider questions like the following: ● Who will this data be presented to? ● Will each stakeholder need or want the same information? ● Do they want the raw data or a synthesized analysis? ● Does the data need to be anonymized? ● Does the available data account for quantitative and qualitative information? ● After reviewing the data, how should I/we/they respond to it? What are recommended actions? If you’re not sure how to communicate data, take Introduction to Effective Analytics Communication in the PBS Course Catalog to get started. STEP 3: ESTABLISH A PROCESS TO RESP OND TO WHAT YOU ARE LEARNING THROUGH DATA COLLECTION. This is especially important in digital projects because the entire production process is meant to be iterative. Constantly reflect on data points to be more responsive to your audience and strategic with content choices. ● Set aside specific times to collect data. Unlocking Growth With Thoughtful, Data-Informed Reflection at Project’s End Closing the Loop Leadership Strategies With audiences constantly changing and growing, it is imperative that PBS adapt alongside them. Consistently evaluating if your projects connect with your audiences and/or meet your goals allows you to uncover opportunities for improvement and growth. Dedicating time to learn from your projects unlocks growth and more success. Analyzing data by conducting postmortems provides insights into what worked and what didn’t, helping refine processes, enhance efficiency and avoid repeating mistakes. By setting aside this time, you set a station culture of accountability and improvement, which ensures each future project can yield even better outcomes. This is a companion chapter to Closing a Project and Looking Ahead and Conducting a Show Evaluation. Learning From Your Projects After reading the first three chapters of the Leadership Strategies section, you have already achieved several key milestones: 1. Ensured your digital production aligns with your station’s strategic needs 2. Outlined clear goals that also reflect your station’s values 3. Garnered support within your station and established an action plan to achieve your goals Constantly evaluating the progress and success of your project should be done throughout the process and at its conclusion. “Closing the loop” on your goal incorporates data and feedback from audiences or colleagues and reflects on project team experiences to improve your project and/or overcome challenges.

58 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 59 ● Send routine updates to project stakeholders with main points and lessons learned so far. ● Routinely discuss the data and feedback with your team and brainstorm ways to respond to what you’re hearing and/or seeing. ● Outline action steps to respond to the data. ● Evaluate the effect of the action steps. Were your goal(s) met? STEP 4: CONDUCT A PROJECT DEBRIEF WITH YOUR TEAM(S) TO EVALUATE THE OVERALL OUTCOMES AND LESSONS LEARNED. ● A debrief or “postmortem” typically evaluates where the project was successful, where challenges were hit and why, and how best to move forward. It is important that you document this process and share the information with your team and any stakeholder involved in the process. Refer to the Retrospectives and Post-Mortems Conversation Guide for guiding principles of a debrief. ● Refer to the companion guidebook chapters Closing a Project and Looking Ahead and Conducting a Show Evaluation for a thorough breakdown of how to conclude and reflect on a digital project. STEP 5: REFLECT ON LESSONS LEARNED AND ITERATE FOR YOUR NEXT PROJECT. ● Using feedback from the debrief, create an action plan for how you will adapt and iterate on your processes and outcomes for future projects. This is how you fully close the loop. By setting a new goal(s) informed by the lessons you learned and the feedback you obtained internally and externally, you can take the first step toward improvement and growth in your future work. Parting Thoughts Consistently evaluating your project’s progress and success helps you achieve your goals, meet your audiences and foster continuous growth. “Closing the loop” incorporates data, feedback and team experiences in a timely manner to overcome challenges and enhance the project. Implementing a structured approach to data collection, analysis and response ensures alignment with your goals and supports informed decisions. The reflective practice enables you to set goals for future projects, driving ongoing growth and success that meets both your station’s and audience’s needs. Before starting to “close the loop,” take the opportunity to reach out to your network to learn about strategies they used to collect, evaluate and respond to data. There is no need to start from scratch when you’ve got a brain trust of folks across the system who love to help! Leadership Strategies | Closing the Loop Leveraging Public Media Networks How to Grow System-Wide Leaders and Relationships Engaging across the public media system is crucial for fostering constructive dialogue, sharing diverse perspectives and collectively addressing challenges. Engaging across the public media system is crucial for fostering constructive dialogue, Moreover, engaging across the public media system and leveraging networks is vital for the overall success of the PBS system. Building meaningful connections with your colleagues allows you to create change, instigate growth and encourage iteration across all functional areas. But most importantly, it establishes you as a leader in your own right. Do any of these phrases sound familiar? When leading new digital projects, remember you don’t have to start from scratch! There are others in the system who have done — and are doing — what you are attempting to do! Reach out to others — strangers or friends — to learn about their successes and challenges, discover what is unique or similar about your projects and use that collective knowledge to build a stronger, better road map to success. “You also had that challenge?” “Wow, I did not know other people were doing this!” “You dealt with that too?” Leadership Strategies

60 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 61 The Importance of Networking Networking is an important and fundamental component of any career. Networking gives us access to new people, ideas, mentors and approaches. Networking within the public media system can have unique benefits when starting a new project or initiative. WHY YOU SHOULD NETWORK Creating stronger and broader connections across the system can eliminate redundancies and improve efficiencies with project planning, creation, tools and distribution. h For example: PBS North Carolina’s National Productions team partnered with their National Productions’ digital executive producer to declare 2023 “The Year of YouTube” and used strategic insights from PBS Digital Studios production work to overhaul their station’s titles and thumbnails process for YouTube and social. Their year-over-year views increased by 153%, suggested video views increased by 203%, impressions were up by 80%, CTR was up by 9% and subscriber growth was up by 12%! Connecting with others reiterates that you are not alone in this process and that together, you can work through common challenges and celebrate successes. h For example: Based on their previous experience producing a series for PBS Digital Studios, PBS North Carolina agreed to consult on New Mexico PBS’s series “Sovereign Innovations.” Or, you could collaborate on a YouTube episode like Twin Cities PBS and Kansas City PBS did in “The Genius of Charlie Parker.” Reaching out to others sharing your interests, ideas, goals, challenges and successes can be both inspiring and educational, which can lead you down new paths and help you to gain new insights in yourself and in public media. h For example: Digital Voltage 2024 workshops brought together over 500 public media staffers to learn from their peers about best practices when making digital series. Watch the Digital Voltage 2024 recordings on the HUB. With a strong network in place and an understanding of common best practices, you can build a digital strategy for the future that is flexible, informed and successful. Leadership Strategies | Leveraging Public Media Networks HOW TO START NETWORKING  Reach out to PBS to find station contacts who have done or are doing similar work by emailing stations@pbs.org.  Reach out to relevant advisory councils and committees. You can also search for colleagues by station or department on people.pbs.org.  Connect with others in digital roles. If you do not know where to get started, join the PBS Digital Station Solutions Facebook group.  Ask coworkers, mentors or friends to introduce you to their colleagues and thought partners at other stations.  Identify projects or concepts you are interested in and reach out to others in discussion boards or through PBS Digital Studios training, or watch past conference presentations to find experts you may not have known existed in the system.  Attend an in-person training, conference or event on relevant topics. If you are not sure what types of training are available or where, review the PBS professional development offerings.  Network within your own station. Pull together individuals across divisions to learn about a new topic on a regular basis.  Apply for councils, committees or leadership development programs. Whether hosted by public media or other organizations, these are a great way to build your network and broaden your digital know-how.  Use this guidebook! Is there a particular station example that stands out to you or aligns with the work you are interested in? Reach out to them and ask them to share more about the work they did.

62 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 63 FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY Building a Networking Map A networking map can help illustrate what someone is interested in learning more about or seeking advice on, who they can learn from and what help they need to ask for to meet that goal. Guidebook users should be able to build a road map to support their personal networking goals. Create a network road map of your own. Step 1: Identify three to five areas where you want support or feedback. This could be at any point throughout the project planning, creation or distribution process or on a professional development goal. Step 2: Review your current public media resources. Identify any gaps where you do not have resources or a network to connect to. Who or what kinds of roles do you need access to? Step 3: Outline at least one to two steps to find or locate the information or people you need now that you have identified gaps and possible helpers. Can you find them online? Can you ask for an introduction? What else could you do? Step 4: Reach out and start expanding your network map! Parting Thoughts Any time you start a new project or initiative, it is worth pausing to ask, “Who do I know who has already done this?” Are they at your station? If not, where do they work? Your answer will help you better understand how strong or weak your current network is and what kinds of thinkers, leaders and doers you need to meet. Leaning on your public media system networks will not only build a strong network for you but also strengthen the entire public media community. The more successful and connected we are, the better public media functions as a whole. TAKEAWAY TASK “T Minus Zero” was a groundbreaking collaboration between WJCT Public Media and Central Florida Public Media, created to revolutionize space exploration coverage on social media. This initiative was particularly focused on the historic SpaceX Crew Dragon Demo-2 launch in 2020 — the first crewed launch from the U.S. since 2011. Together, the two stations effectively shared the workload of covering rocket launches, enabling a very small team to provide extensive, high-quality coverage. What began as a straightforward solution to insufficient coverage swiftly transformed into a robust network. Utilizing Facebook Pages cross posting, “T Minus Zero” shared its live videos with over 50 public media and commercial TV and radio stations, expanding its reach to a combined network audience of over 4.3 million followers. This strategy not only amplified viewer engagement but also demonstrated the potential of collaborative digital strategies in public media. CASE STUDY Creating Inter-Station and Interstellar Connections Case Study Series: T Minus Zero Station Name: WJCT Public Media and Central Florida Public Media Author Name: Ray Hollister Leadership Strategies | Leveraging Public Media Networks

70 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 71 ● Describe their interests. h What are their hobbies? (This is not just what they watch!) ● Describe their values. h Are they individualistic or communal? h Do they value facts or feelings? And so on… ● What else do they watch online? h Topics, channels, creators? ● What brands do they follow? ● What are their demographics? ● How many of those people are already watching your intended publishing platform(s)? h Did you answer, “I don’t know” to anything? Who could help you find those answers? PRACTICE ACTIVITY Let’s Get Started Think of a series you’re either developing or would like to develop. Be as specific as you can when answering this quick questionnaire! What are the primary and secondary goals of your series? STEP 1: IDENTIFY YOUR TARGET AUDIENCE Which Comes First? Leading With Audience Versus Concept You’re a creative person, and you may have a fun idea for a series. But who is going to watch it (besides you)? Although it’s possible to start with a great idea and back into a target audience, ideally it is the reverse: identifying who you want to reach, then developing a series to reach them. Or doing them together: fleshing out your primary audience as you develop your creative pitch. Not only is this part of your landscape analysis — read Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck for more — but it is also hard to overstate how interconnected the audience and the creative are. Audience development starts with a goal. Not every series will have the same goals. For example, you may be trying to reach a broad audience and achieve high numbers of views (perhaps you’ll monetize those views!). You may be courting awards success or telling a niche story that might not go viral but will provide deep meaning to a small core audience. Perhaps you will end up helping with fundraising or mission-driven goals in the process! Sometimes, you can achieve more than one goal — like the famed unicorn of big numbers + new audience. But being realistic about what you are trying to achieve is critical for informing your editorial strategy as well as your packaging and marketing strategy. Read more on that in future sections. STEP 2: DO YOUR HOMEWORK With a goal clearly in mind, it’s time to start developing an audience strategy, and naturally, that starts with The Art of War. “If you know your target audience and know yourself, you need not fear the result of a hundred YouTube uploads. If you know yourself but not your target audience, for every victory gained, you will also suffer a defeat. If you know neither, you will succumb every time.” — Sun Tzu Okay, maybe he didn’t say those exact words, but the lesson is universal. Step one in establishing an audience strategy is to understand yourself — meaning your own platforms and existing audience. Who do you reach right now? What cards are you holding? It’s never too early to do this step. Understanding yourself should happen whether you’re ideating on a new series or not! But if you’re about to start developing a show and haven’t done this step, do it first! Refer to Choosing Audience Goals for more on crafting achievable goals. Ask yourself or your team the following questions as you begin to develop your audience strategy: BRAINSTORM Question 1: How easy was it to envision your target audience? Question 2: If it was difficult to assess how well you currently reach that target, what kind of resources do you need to bridge the gap? Crafting an Audience Strategy Identifying a Target Audience and Strategizing to Reach Them So, you’re going to make a digital series. Great! Who are you planning to reach, and — more importantly — how do you plan to reach them? To build a successful series, we must have a clear understanding of audience. In this section, we’ll walk through the basics of establishing a successful audience strategy. Cultivating Audience

# Page 37^page=37
72 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 73 Establishing a clear sense of audience is critical to the success of a show. Setting clear goals, defining a clear target audience and having a clear understanding of your own publishing platforms are the foundational components that make up audience strategy. In the next section, we’ll talk about platforms and social media. Understand how these sample audience profiles look when applied to a show by reading the case study from “Weathered” on the next page. DIVE DEEPER Why would someone click on this? Just because you’ve made a show about [subject], it doesn’t mean that fans of [subject] are going to click. What’s unique about your series? What’s it saying that others aren’t? Why is your series going to be worth their time? Who is your secondary audience? Often, a primary audience is narrower, while a secondary audience may be broader. As you develop your series, keep this secondary audience in mind, because they can be critical to the series’ success. For example, a show targeted to the knitting community will do bigger numbers if there’s also appeal in there for craft enthusiasts more broadly. Aim narrow but catch wide. After you’ve defined your target audience and defined yourself, look at the overlap between those two segments. What does your Venn diagram look like? If you have a lot of overlap, you can focus on optimizing to your existing strengths. If not, a new approach will be needed. More on marketing and growth in later sections. You should continually refer back to your audience profile during the production process. After greenlighting, as you write and produce the show, it’s important to remind yourself of who you’re aiming for to keep yourself on track. And after the show has begun publishing, you’ll again want to refer back to the audience. Who actually showed up to watch? Did we reach who we set out to reach or someone else? What adjustments should be made? Understanding audience is not a straight line but a circle that never stops! Cultivating Audience | Crafting an Audience Strategy ● What channel will you be publishing on? h How active is it? How many monthly views does it generate? How many views do new uploads get? What other platforms (social media, website, etc.) are you also publishing on? ● How loyal is your audience? h Do you have repeat viewers? Do your views come from subscribers or nonsubscribers? What are your traffic sources? Do your videos get a lot of retention or are they more casual, quicker views? ● What are the demographics of your viewers? h Age, gender, geography… ● What kinds of content does your channel publish? h Are there differences in performance (views, engagement, etc.) or audience composition (demographics) between these different types of content? ● Who don’t you reach? And where are they? h For example, if you’re not reaching a lot of teens and twentysomethings on your YouTube channel, perhaps those groups are using other platforms and you should be publishing there. That sounds like a lot of questions (and it is). Fortunately, YouTube Analytics is robust in what it can tell you about your viewership. Social media platforms and Google Analytics will offer good insights as well. Additional research conducted via surveying can tell you things about your viewers that the platforms themselves do not. Read more in later chapters, Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics and Creating Engaging Community Tab Posts. STEP 3: DESCRIBE YOUR TARGET AUDIENCE IN DETAIL Describe your audience behaviorally, not just demographically. Gamers watch gaming shows. Foodies watch food shows. Sure, there are demographics that over-index in these categories, but trying to appeal to “women 18 to 34” is going to be difficult because there’s so much diversity of interests within a demo set. How specific or large is your audience? Not all topics have the same sized fan base. Be realistic about how large the pool is that you’re trying to tap into. A small niche topic will have different audience expectations (namely, depth and detail) versus a popular category like pop culture or science. How easy are they to identify or organize? Over the years, we’ve received pitches for shows targeted to “X for people who don’t like Y.” Although that is easy enough to imagine in your brain, it’s a very different thing to create an outreach strategy if there isn’t a cohesive community or interest in existence.

# Page 38^page=38
74 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 75 Case Study Series: Weathered Station Name: Balance Media Author Name: Trip Jennings “Weathered” Audience Profiles CASE STUDY Cultivating Audience | Crafting an Audience Strategy When “Weathered” launched, we didn’t have the same understanding of our series viewers as we do now. Through iteration and reviewing the data, we’ve developed a clearer picture of them and always try to deliver on their preferences. We find the audience of “Weathered” to be well-informed on climate science, and they appreciate a high level of detail. They are not looking for introductions or simplifications. We get very nuanced, opinionated conversations in our comments, and the videos with the most detail tend to do best. Our videos can attract a few vocal deniers, which leads to lively comment sections, but we do little moderating ourselves because our community is so passionate and informed. That said, although our videos are complex, the video titles must be instantly clear to get clicks. If we can’t describe a topic with the perfect title, we don’t do it! Our viewers prefer climate stories told in a way that is bold and unflinching. They want no punches pulled and can sniff out sugarcoated optimism. They have a “Tell it to me straight, doc” mindset and appreciate our realism. At the same time, we take care not to be alarmist. This is an audience who appreciates validation. They observe that the world around them is changing and can feel the effects themselves. We script our shows to speak directly to You-the-Viewer and acknowledge their experiences. This has the effect of making global-level concepts like climate personally relat- able, which people appreciate. On a technical level, “Weathered” viewers love visual aids like graphs and maps. We find them to be well dispersed across age ranges, and they tend to over-represent in the areas of the country most impacted by climate change. That said, we don’t aim for a particular demographic — our changing climate affects us all. Choosing Audience Goals A Quick Guide to Setting the Best Goal(s) for Your Series The terrible secret: There is no single pathway to success. This section is a quick guide to the different types of goals you may set for your digital series and strategies about how to follow through. Defining Success There are different ways to define success. Being clear from the start about your goals and metrics to evaluate progress is critical to being successful. Below are a few typical examples of digital series goal types and tips on how to achieve them. T YPES OF DIGITAL-SERIES GOALS High viewership: The classic. This is a good goal when the topic is broad and reach is the priority. h For example: A program about climate change wants to reach as many people as possible because it’s relevant to everyone. For this kind of goal, you would optimize the programming and packaging strategy to double down on what works best on your channel. In other words: Study what works and do a lot of it. Establishing a specific number for this goal means comparing past performance for look-alike programs and making a target projection. Use time-based benchmarks to assess average video views after one month or year, for example. Then calculate your goal accordingly. To learn more about this goal, read Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics. Increased viewership among a particular demographic: For digital media, “young people (18–44)” is a typical target audience, but your program could be more specific: c As a percentage of your audience h Build your program and packaging to be niche to speak to a certain subset of people. This is the “hyper-serving” approach. h The downside of this goal is if the target demographic doesn’t currently watch your programs, increasing their percentage could cost you existing fans. Ideally, this method is meant to be additive, not subtractive. It can be done! Cultivating Audience

# Page 39^page=39
76 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 77 c As a total number of people h For instance, maybe you want to reach more people aged 18 to 24. As opposed to hyper-targeting college students, you could make a broadly appealing series that college students, young adults, teens or even some older adults would be interested in. The logic here is that your target audience may be a smaller slice of the pie in terms of percentage, but it’s a larger pie, so you’re reaching more of them in total. h Use a multilayered approach here. Select topics and packaging that appeal to the core target, with enough broad appeal that the show will perform well algorithmically. h A good example of a multilayered approach is this YouTube thumbnail from “Sovereign Innovations.” There are inside jokes winking at Indigenous viewers, but it’s still clear to non-Indigenous audiences. Impact: Public media values not just total view count but also the quality of content and audience engagement. Doing exclusively what is popular for other brands does not always serve the public media mission or ethos. High-impact qualitative goals like making a difference in people’s lives or telling a story that needs to be told, regardless of how many people watch it, is part of who we are. This goal still requires a measurement tactic. Decide how you might quantify your success at the start of your project. For example: c Awards: Think about how you will leverage any awards in the future. Does it yield funding, prestige or something else? c Engagement rate: Comments divided by views can be an indication of how engaged people are, regardless of how many people viewed overall. c Personal anecdotes: Read Designing Effective Audience Surveys for more on soliciting impact statements. Determine which success metric best serves your project and available methods and track accordingly. Monetization Money is important in this business. But to take something of value, you must first give something of value. Building a viewership with returning, engaged viewers is steps one, two and three. Monetization is best considered after a series has been well established. Beware that some federal and grant funding sources can have restricted revenue clauses, which limit your opportunity for monetization on certain episodes or seasons. Learn and track what content you can or cannot monetize. Parting Thoughts Everyone wants their series to get all the views, help the station reach younger viewers, bring in the big bucks and win all the awards. But realistically, it’s important to pick a path. Choosing the right goal for your digital series will set you on a clear path and make it easier to measure success. Cultivating Audience | Choosing Audience Goals

78 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 79 STEP 1: KEY TERMS AND DEFINITIONS First, let’s start with a quick definition of key terms. ● Impression: Your video is put in front of someone, that is, your thumbnail is shown: Higher = better. ● Click-Through Rate (CTR): Clicks divided by impressions. Higher = better. ● View: On YouTube, that’s a play of at least 30 seconds. Higher = better. ● Retention: On YouTube, this is measured by average percent viewed and average view duration (both measure how long someone watches either as a percentage of run time or a number of minutes): Higher = better. ● Engagement: Means likes, comments and shares on the video, although you won’t find the term “engagement” in YouTube studio. Higher = better. ● Engagement Rate: The number of engagements divided by the number of views: Higher = better. ● Subscribers: The followers of your channel. However, in the context of an individual video’s performance it means people who subscribed after watching that video. Higher = better. Another little secret: All these statistics are intertwined with one another. YouTube’s system (a.k.a., the algorithm) looks at indicators like CTR (which signals interest), retention (which signals quality because people keep watching) or engagement (which signals user satisfaction) and uses that data to decide how many impressions to serve up. It’s rare to have any one statistic doing well while others fail. Digital Analytics Vocabulary Point being: by looking at just “Views” you’re seeing the product of a lot of other indicators. Did I just write “Higher = better” 7 times? See? Analytics is easy. Yes! YouTube Analytics 101 Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics Digital is all about the numbers. Numbers that you (yes, you!) can access with a few clicks. Numbers that you can (and should) be paying close attention to because digital is iterative. Understanding the data is essential for going from “We published something on YouTube” to “We have a successful YouTube show!” This section is a beginner’s guide to YouTube Analytics. ● What is the average click-through rate (CTR) on videos on your YouTube channel from the last year or so? ● What about the CTR for the first 24 hours of launch? ● What would your average view count be? ● What are your typical traffic sources? ● What kind of subscriber growth have you seen in the last year? ● What is the average age distribution of your audience? ● What’s the typical gender split of your audience? ● What is your typical retention rate on a video-per-video basis? The good news: The practice exercise wasn’t intended as a “gotcha” if you don’t know those numbers offhand or even what the terms mean. The other good news: If you can learn those few things, you’ll understand the most fundamental elements of analytics which have huge impacts. Even more good news: Those few things are, in fact, quite easy to learn. You don’t even need to do math! The dirty little secret: analytics is not hard. The bad news: Just kidding, there’s no bad news. PRACTICE ACTIVITY Let’s Get Started P OP QUIZ! NO GOOGLING ALLOWED. Guidebook users will be able to assess their current understanding of YouTube Analytics. TAKEAWAY Cultivating Audience

There are two main phases to Analytics work: The Study Phase and the React Phase (a.k.a. the Panic Phase) STEP 3: STUDY PHASE - PRE-PRODUCTION The Study Phase happens throughout the year. This is when you’re learning about your channel and your performance. It may be during pre-production of a series or after a series has concluded and you’re looking ahead for future projects. Harkening back to our Crafting an Audience Strategy section, here’s when you should be spending time getting to know your existing audience and your channel. ● Go to YouTube Studio’s Advanced Mode and start exporting video data into Google Sheets or Excel Docs! c In terms of views (or other data points), what’s rising to the top? What’s sinking to the bottom? What topics seem to be resonating? c You’ll probably notice the strong correlation between views and impressions, comments and watch time. h Note: If you’ve run ads but want to know purely about organic performance, use the filter feature to remove ads as a traffic source. ● Channel Pages: Someone went to a specific page and clicked into a video or followed a link from another channel’s page: a good measure of cross-promotion. ● YouTube Search: Someone went to YouTube, typed a query in and clicked your video as a result: Note that you can drill down from here and see what terms specifically are sending you traffic! h Note: that Google Search will also display YouTube videos, but this traffic gets counted under External, and you cannot see which terms are contributing traffic. h Having your video get good traffic from a search is a good sign that you’ve SEO-optimized your metadata. Well done! ● External: YouTube will keep track of which websites are sending you traffic: blogs, websites, social media platforms and so on. ● Playlists: Someone watched within a playlist. ● YouTube Ads: Congratulations on having a marketing budget. ● Direct or Unknown: Someone reached your video through something like a newsletter. Cultivating Audience | Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics STEP 2: EVEN MORE KEY TERMS AND DEFINITIONS Traffic sources are useful to know as well. That’s how a person got to your video. ● Browse Features: Mainly the YouTube home page, subscriptions page or other pages; that is, someone started browsing and got shown your video: algorithm driven ● Suggested: The videos appearing after or alongside a video you’re watching: algorithm driven ● Shorts Feed: The shorts-only tab, which autoplays: algorithm driven These statistics should ideally be a high percentage, perhaps 70% or more, because they mean that YouTube is recommending your video for you. This is how a majority of views on the site happen, meaning you’re accessing the largest possible pool of people. These other traffic sources are worth knowing, though they make up a lower percentage. Episode traffic sources.

# Page 42^page=42
82 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 83 STEP 4: REACT PHASE - DURING PUBLISHING The React Phase is when you’ve recently published a video, typically within the first 48 hours. Not only will you want to know how it’s doing, but you can also make modifications that affect how it does in real time. ● CTR is always a critical statistic, but the first few hours are the most critical. If typical videos on your channel average a 3 percent CTR over a long period of time, within the first one to two hours that number should ideally be at least 4 percent or higher. This is because YouTube serves new releases first to “low-hanging fruit” — usually loyal, repeat viewers — and launch is always your highest CTR. Some rules of thumb for hours one to two: c Two or more percentage points higher: You may have a hit on your hands. c One point higher: probably a typical performer c Same as average: likely to underwhelm c Below average at a launch: a very bad sign h YouTube Studio has a helpful feature on its dashboard that benchmarks new releases for you, ranking them against your 10 most recent uploads on the channel. This can be a handy shortcut for benchmarking. ● Impressions/Views are something you’ll also want to monitor even if they are likely to be correlated to your CTR. If Your Video Is Underperforming... The Interest Problem If your click-through rate is low, you’ve got an interest problem. Either the topic of the video or the way you’ve positioned it isn’t resonating. You can’t change the video now, but you can change your packaging, aka your video title and/or thumbnail. For more, dive into Creating Titles and Thumbnails. The Discovery Problem If your click-through rate is OK or good, but your impressions (and, by proxy, views) are low, you probably have a discovery problem. Your video isn’t being matched with enough people who want to see it. It may be niche. Or it may be that your metadata could be improved to include more relevant keywords that YouTube will match with appropriate audiences. The Dropoff Problem If people are clicking into your video but not watching for long, you probably have a dropoff problem. This is a negative signal to YouTube that your video 1) isn’t very good or 2) is misleadingly packaged, aka clickbait. In both cases, YouTube will stop recommending your video, leading to low view counts despite high CTRs. Review your packaging and make sure your video pays off on the promise made by your title and thumbnail. If it’s a quality issue, it’s a lesson for next time. Cultivating Audience | Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics YouTube viewers by age. ● Start benchmarking! c Earlier, we asked what your typical CTR or watch time is. Now’s the chance to learn what yours is. c Use the time-period feature to measure performance within different periods of time (last month, last year, etc.). h Note: Videos that have spent more time on YouTube will often have more views. Splitting up your analytics by time-period chunks will help put your video performance into better context. The advanced-level move is to benchmark each video at individual time periods, but that’s a manual process. ● Start categorizing. c Start classifying your videos so you can look for additional patterns. For example, a topic like news video, food video, science video, history video, etc. Nothing beats a spreadsheet for this kind of thing. c What about length? Are shorter videos doing better? Longer? c Consider the types of videos, such as full episodes, segments, bespoke videos, etc. ● Learn your demo information. c How old are your viewers? Where do they live geographically? Are they a subscriber to your channel? And so on. Learn what YouTube will tell you about your viewers. You can look at and export this data at the channel level, but it’s also helpful to review at a video-by-video level. (Unfortunately, you can’t export video-by-video splits in bulk; you must go one at a time.) It’ll help answer questions like “Do videos that appeal to younger viewers do better?” The answers can help inform programming decisions. ● Learn your traffic sources. c It’s always good to get more browse views, more search views, more suggested views, etc. Understanding these is more about understanding how much work you have cut out for you.

86 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 87 Designing Effective Audience Surveys Leveling Up Your Digital Series With Nuanced Insights Analytics are an important tool, but they can’t tell you everything. Sometimes you simply need to ask your audience. This section will discuss the rationale for implementing surveys and how to build them effectively. Putting survey insights to practical use can improve your digital productions. PRACTICE ACTIVITY Meet Your Target Audience These reveal what you want to learn when building a survey. With this information in hand, let’s dig into survey building blocks. Understanding Your Audience Is Tactical Crafting an Audience Strategy discussed the importance of knowing yourself, your series and overall brand and cited surveys as a tool to do so. This section will dig into the details, showing how and why surveys take your analytics to the next level. UNDERSTAND YOUR AUDIENCE’S INTERESTS First, it’s important to understand what you want to know about your audience and why. What does your audience enjoy? Knowing this is almost always the biggest benefit of surveys. Demographics don’t watch media — people do. Understanding your audience’s interests will be extremely illuminating and help you make better data-informed programming and marketing decisions. When talking about interests, get specific by considering the following: ● Viewing interests. What else do they watch online? Are they into gaming? Food reviews? Travel vlogs? Understanding what else people are watching — even if it’s not categories you generally make shows for — helps paint a clearer picture of your viewers and their values. Plus, you might identify an unexpected opportunity along the way, i.e., “Wow, our audience loves DIY videos. We should do something with that…” ● Other media they consume. What other YouTube channels, television networks or social media accounts do they follow? This fleshes out your understanding of your own audience. Ask yourself, “What makes them compelling?” Is it style, format, theme? Can you do that too? Bonus: Use these names for advertising-targeting purposes or identifying collaboration partners. ● Direct feedback on loglines. Do you have an idea (or ideas) for a digital series but are unsure which one to pursue? Let the viewers decide! Put loglines in front of fans and let them decide. Many PBS Digital Studios series were greenlit after audience surveying. ● Topics and subtopics. This idea is similar to loglines but less specific. This determines if your audience prefers certain topics like history, science, literature, etc. You could also drill down within those topics to look for subtopics. h For example: “Would you prefer to watch a show about art history, military history, political history, women’s history, etc.?” ● Values-based preferences. Knowing what topics your audience is interested in is important, but it’s just as critical to understand how they want to engage with your content, and — more importantly — why. h For example: “I watch nonfiction videos to learn practical things for my life” or “I prefer the video essay format.” With this data, you can identify interesting opportunities to meet your audience’s desires. TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y Guidebook users can discern what they want to know about their target audience. Let’s start by making a wish list. Think about your target audience. Write down everything you already know about them: likes science, aged 20 to 30, etc. Now, consider everything you don’t know about your audience. Admittedly, this could be a very long list, so let’s focus on what you would like to know about them: ● Are they a student? ● Do they have kids? ● Do they give money to the station? ● Why don’t they give money to the station? ● If we made a show about (insert subject here) would they watch it? ● And so on... Cultivating Audience

# Page 45^page=45
88 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 89 LOOK BEYOND YOUTUBE AND SOCIAL MEDIA ANALY TICS Second, it’s important to strategize how to evaluate your audience reach and impact. Looking beyond YouTube or social media analytics is important because these tools are limited to certain data points. Generally, platforms have powerful analytics tools (more on that in Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics) but will only tell you aggregated data about your viewers’ age, gender and geography. ● Expand audience data points. Do you want to know other things like level of education, geography or what languages they speak? You can’t get that from the platforms. But it could be highly relevant to the audience goals you’re setting, so that’s where surveys come in. ● Compare year-over-year (YOY) results. You can also track how any of these details change year over year (YOY) by conducting regular surveys. This is important for measuring impact. ● Identify audience overlap and cross-promotion opportunities. Do viewers of one of your series watch your others? Do they read your newsletters? Do they watch your broadcast shows? If so, which ones? ● Measure qualitative things like impact. Making an impact on your viewers’ lives is a critical part of the public media mission but quantifying it can be challenging using analytics alone. Unless you ask directly! Did they take action because of your content? Were they inspired to take a class, join a group, register to vote, discuss the topic with family or friends, etc.? ● Solicit open-ended feedback. Write-in answers are illuminating and come from the source. What does your audience want to see more or less of? Where can you improve? What else do you want to know? Add write-in questions to your survey. ● Customize your survey to meet your local needs. Use this as a chance to ask relevant questions about your local market. Ask questions about station support (or what would get them to support), local stories — whatever you need to know! CROSS-REFERENCE EVERY THING! We are going to skip ahead for a moment. Once you have the data in hand, the real superpower of audience surveys is segmentation. Segmentation analyzes how people answered one question based on how they answered another. For example: h How do respondents from different age categories differ when answering the question “Where do you consume your news?” If you want to reach younger audiences, what can you learn from that? h How do respondents who watch X program differ from respondents who watch Y program when it comes to donating to your local station? Is there a pattern? h How do topics of interest change by gender or age, etc.? You can always look at your data in aggregate, but slicing and dicing audiences by segment will tell you a lot more. Some of the most useful groupings are: c By show viewership status: Group people together by the shows they watch and compare responses. PBS Digital Studios also likes to ask about favorite shows and use that as a category to differentiate between the most dedicated fans and casual viewers. c By topical fandoms: Are they a “science fan” or “history buff” or “arts aficionado”? Ask directly if they would consider themselves as such, or assign a tag like that based on what shows they watch. c By demographics: Age, occupation, donor status, education or geography, and so on. Are there any interesting differences or consistencies there? REFLECTION Question 1: What do you think are the most important or useful comparisons for your work? Make sure those questions are included in your survey. Cultivating Audience | Designing Effective Audience Surveys

# Page 46^page=46
90 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 91 Designing and Analyzing the Survey Congratulations! By now, you have a list of possible questions for an audience survey. Narrow them down, order them by priority, try to keep it concise and use simple and straightforward phrasing. Tell the respondents up front how many questions are on your survey, so they know the estimated time to complete the survey. Five minutes is the best target; anything longer than 10 minutes is probably too much. STEP 1: CREATING THE SURVEY After setting your survey goals and determining what questions you want to ask your audience, it’s time to build an effective survey. There is no shortage of options when it comes to tools, and most have similar offerings — you can’t go wrong. Common survey tools include SurveyMonkey, Airtable or Google Forms. If you need inspiration or a reference because it’s your first time writing a survey, here is a sample based off a previous PBS Digital Studios audience survey. Notice how it tracks against the previously mentioned goals, detailing user references, viewing habits, demographics, impact and much more. STEP 2: MARKETING THE SURVEY Making sure your survey reaches the people you want to answer is critical to getting data you want and can use. Some ways to engage your potential survey respondents are: ● Verbal calls to action (CTAs) can be placed within active YouTube episodes. If you want to get a good snapshot of the viewership on your digital series, include verbal calls to action from your host, like in “The Misunderstood Legacy of Ninjas.” If you don’t have a host, a voiceover call to action can be added, like in “Making Technicolor Glass Art from Upcycled Junk.” Your call to action should ask for feedback and point to a “link in the description.” Don’t forget to add the link to your survey in the description! ● Leverage your other media, including YouTube’s community tab, social media or newsletters, to push surveys. You could even make a broadcast promo. Put it in front of whoever you want to capture as the audience. ● Use unique links! Most survey tools allow you to share different trackable source links. Just like UTM codes, which are the “?source=” you see in URLs, survey source links can tell a marketer where a click came from. Give each marketing placement its own unique link, whether it’s the platform (Instagram, newsletter, blog) or differentiating between your digital series (food YouTube show, history YouTube show, news YouTube show, etc.). ● Introduce incentives. These are always nice to offer if you can. That said, they add logistical complexity. An easy, nonfinancial incentive like “Help us pick new shows” or “Help shape the future of PBS” works quite well and requires no paperwork. READ THE FINE PRINT Survey-making is an entire profession. Keep in mind some caveats about opt-in surveys: ● Opt-in surveys are not necessarily representative of all your existing viewers! Rather, they’re representative of people who 1) watch to the very end of your YouTube video (only a small percentage of people do that), or subscribe to your newsletter or follow you on social media, etc., and 2) are willing to spend their time taking a survey. You surely have plenty of viewers who don’t tick these boxes! ● Opt-in surveys do not measure the same thing as randomly sourced surveys. This approach is only measuring your existing viewers. If you want feedback on your market overall, you need to take other steps. ● Opt-in surveys are for your most dedicated, die-hard fans. They capture your most important stakeholders: These are the first people to see your videos, can make or break its success and are critical to digital media. Parting Thoughts Don’t let perfect be the enemy of good. Writing and promoting surveys can be daunting and seem like the sort of thing that requires specialized expertise. But when’s the best time to start collecting audience data? Yesterday! But what if I don’t know exactly what to ask? Ask it anyway! When starting out, it’s more valuable to have the data than to collect it perfectly. ● Audience surveys don’t need to be complicated. You can ask a 30-question survey or a four-question survey. ● You can write a complicated survey with branching logic (“If person answers X, skip question 3, and if they answer Y, skip question 4”), or you can K.I.S.S. (“Keep It Simple, Smarty”). Both have value depending on intent and needs. ● Surveys don’t always have to be surveys. They can be individual polls posted on social media or on your community tab. The point is: ask questions! No matter how complex or simple your survey is, understanding your audience at a deeper level is critical to digital success. Cultivating AudienceCultivating Audience | Designing Effective Audience Surveys

# Page 47^page=47
92 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 93 TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y Online Collaborations, a.k.a. “Collabs” How and Why to Identify and Execute Collaborations Collaborating with other YouTubers is an excellent strategy for expanding your audience organically. In this section, we’ll discuss how to identify collaboration partners and how to execute a “collab”. Sharing Is Caring Collaborations (referred to in this document, as they are online, as “collabs”) are a simple concept: Two YouTubers appear in special videos on each other’s channel, with each host promoting the other channel. The goal is equally simple: I share my audience with you, you share yours with me, and we both walk away with a bigger audience. The nice thing about YouTube is it’s not a zero-sum game. Unlike the olden days of live television, where watching one show meant not watching something else and it fostered direct competition between channels, everything on YouTube is on-demand. Watching one channel doesn’t preclude you from watching another. In fact, you can often help one another, since YouTube’s algorithm often works by finding look-alike audiences. Let’s dive into how to make the most of collabs, starting with who to do them with. STEP 1: WHAT KIND OF COLL AB WILL YOUR AUDIENCE LIKE? Crafting an Audience Strategy talked about the importance of researching your audience, including articulating their interests and what other YouTube channels they may also watch. This important first step also applies here. Specific Tools to Identify Relevant Channels: ● Channels My Audience Watches: Available on the YouTube Studio Analytics > Audience tab, this will tell you specifically what else your audience is watching. This can be a great way to identify like-minded channels. If a good portion of your audience is watching them, it stands to reason their audience would probably enjoy watching your show too! ● Traffic Sources: Suggested videos will show which videos are recommending your channel. Like the above, this will also help you figure out channel overlap. ● Surveys: Ask your audience directly who else they watch. Lighter-lift feedback can be collected using your community tab’s polls or social media or by posing questions in the comments. Read more in Designing Effective Audience Surveys. ● Good old-fashioned research: Sometimes the best collaborations are with channels your audience may not know about. These likely do not show up in surveys or official statistics. Utilize YouTube’s search function to find channels covering similar topics. Poke around YouTube yourself. There are plenty of cool creators you can work with! REFLECTION Guidebook users will be able to identify favorite creators who could be a great collab. Let’s make a wish list. Write down a list of five to ten of your favorite YouTube channels or creators in which a recurring person is the on-camera host of each video. These don’t have to be related to your work! They can just be channels you like. Now think about a digital series you publish. Setting aside logistics for a second, imagine which of the channels you listed above could make for an interesting collaborator(s) with your series. Now that we’ve got the creative juices flowing, let’s talk collabs. PRACTICE ACTIVITY Let’s Plan a Collab Now that you’ve identified a potential collaborator, what kind of episode could you create together? Congratulations, you just designed a collab! Cultivating Audience

# Page 48^page=48
94 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 95 STEP 2: WHAT MAKES A GOOD COLL AB PARTNER? An ideal collab has the following traits: ● Large YouTube following: The bigger, the better! That said, if you’re looking at a channel as a possible collaboration partner, pay attention to the view counts on their recent uploads, not just their subscriber count or their Top Videos of All Time. “Recent Uploads” will tell you a lot more about the kind of viewership you can expect for your collab. c Ideally, the collaborator channel would be larger than yours, but that is a goal, not a rule. h Tip! When reaching out to partners, leverage the PBS brand name to make up for subscriber differential between your channels. ● Large social media following: Although it’s great to be able to get a plug from a big following on Instagram, TikTok or wherever, if your goal is to build an audience on YouTube, know that audiences moving between platforms is difficult. People like to stay where they are. That said, it can be done! ● Thematic overlap, but not 1:1 overlap: Collaborate with a partner who has some overlap with your channel but isn’t identical. c Too close: Not much point to a collaboration. If they are, what will each host offer the other? c Too far: Their audience may not find your channel relevant or the episode interesting because it falls outside their normal interests. ● A target audience aligned with yours: You may not know exactly who their audience is, but by watching their channel, you’ll get a sense of the channel’s values and if they align with the values you’re trying to appeal to. For example, a sense of humor, a level of educational depth or dedication to factual content, or an emphasis on elevating underrepresented voices. If they are a channel that is doing things with similar values, odds are good their audience is one you’ll want to attract. ● Ability to offer a two-way contribution: Collabs are ideally reciprocal: Each channel contributes to the other. c Consider how their expertise or point-of-view can add value to your channel and what value you can offer to theirs. h Example: The hosts of the personal finance-focused series “Two Cents” appeared on an episode of “Vitals” called “Pros and Cons of Traveling Abroad for Surgery.” While the “Vitals” team had the medical side of the story well-covered, the financial advisors from “Two Cents” were able to unpack the economic side in greater detail. In return, the “Vitals” hosts appeared in a “Two Cents” video called “Should You Buy Drugs Abroad?” to break down the health and safety risks in an otherwise money- focused episode. ● Willingness to cross-promote: The difference between a video with a featured guest and a true collaboration is a combination of reciprocity and promotion. We just discussed reciprocity, but will they also direct their audience to watch your channel’s video, and to like or subscribe? Are they willing to use other levers in their promotional arsenal like social media or the community tab? If not, then they’re lending their name and face, but you may not get their audience. h Tip! Review their channel for previous collabs. Did they promote them? If so, how much? STEP 3: WHAT ’S THE BEST T YPE OF COLL AB? Swapping segments is when the two creators film segments on their own for inclusion in the other’s video. For example: h “Two Cents” filmed an economics explainer segment for inclusion in this episode of “Vitals,” complete with on-camera explainers and graphics, and “Vitals” filmed this medical explainer for inclusion in this episode of “Two Cents.” h Create some sort of physical challenge or demonstration (see the “Rogue History” example on the following page). h Other examples include a response or commentary video. Filming together is even better, if you’re able to be in the same location with another creator, but this isn’t always possible. ● The “Monstrum” episode “What Happened During Our Scary Slumber Party” was a collaboration between several PBS Digital Studios hosts, filmed while at a conference together. Leverage in-person opportunities when they present themselves! ● Maiya May of “Weathered” appeared in person on a location shoot, and also filmed a separate in-studio segment for this episode of “Sharks Unknown.” h Tip: As you can see by these examples, you can also do collabs across your own “network” of shows or channels! For steps four and five, let’s walk through a real-life example between PBS North Carolina’s “Rogue History” and “Tasting History with Max Miller.” Cultivating Audience | Online Collaborations, a.k.a. “Collabs”

# Page 49^page=49
96 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 97 STEP 4: COORDINATING OUTREACH AND SECURING A COLL AB After deciding to try to collaborate with Max Miller from “Tasting History” on a pirate food-themed episode, the series EP found a business inquiry email address. He sent a cold email with the subject line “MEDIA INQUIRY: Rogue History — Behind the Patch Collabs?” The email described our show, mentioned the size of the channel and the PBS brand, and referenced previous “Tasting History” episodes about pirate food that inspired us. Max replied after 11 days, saying that he was open to it and wanted to hear our ideas. From here, the showrunner took over the communications and responded with three ideas based on the premise “What did pirates really eat?” We noted our preference for the first option, which Max liked as well because it was something new to his channel. We decided to move forward. STEP 5: FILMING A COLL AB Max requested that we publish the videos several months from then to avoid a busy period on his channel. To accommodate this, we needed to change our planned episode order and fast-track another topic to fill the gap. This speaks to the importance of flexibility because you are dealing with someone else’s schedule! After agreeing on the new publish date, we had our first call to discuss the shape of each channel’s episodes. For Max’s video, he offered to say that our idea had “inspired him” to make his episode, and he used reworded language from our one-sheet over B-roll we sent to him. For our video, we agreed with Max on a segment in which he’d cook and eat leather, based on a historical recipe. We filmed our segment before receiving Max’s footage, so we needed to be broad with our phrasing, but this ended up working out fine. The collaboration was successful at generating new subscribers for the channel. We thanked many commenters saying they came from “Tasting History”! Check out the final episodes: “Why Did Pirates Eat That? (feat. Tasting History)” and “Surviving on Leather.” STEP 6: CALL TO ACTION Collabs should include a spoken call to action — sometimes called a “shout-out” — from one host directing viewers to the other channel. This can be done multiple times in one video. Although the exact words should be customized, the general format goes like this: “I’m joined by [name] from the [channel name] channel. We’re going to be talking about [topic of episode]. I also did a video on their channel where [describe the other video]. I’ll put a link in the description so you can watch that episode. Be sure to subscribe to their channel while you’re there. [Say nice things about channel.]” Case Study Series: Rogue History Station Name: PBS North Carolina Collaborator Channel: Tasting History with Max Miller Author Name: Greg Jeske CASE STUDY Coordinating and Filming a Collab Cultivating Audience | Online Collaborations, a.k.a. “Collabs” STEP 7: PUBLISHING AND PROMOTION ● Get the timing right: Collabs should be coordinated so that episodes release at the same time. This is important since the call to action is inside each video. If the timing isn’t exact, the message directing viewers to another episode that hasn’t been published will be a missed opportunity as well as frustrating for audiences. ● Link in description: When each episode publishes, add a link in the description to direct viewers to the other episode. Adding additional reminders like “Check out our video and be sure to subscribe” helps. ● Tag them in your title! Write “Feat. @Name of Channel” in your episode title.

98 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 99 Parting Thoughts Do any of these challenges sound familiar? ● You want to reach new people, but you’re tired of waiting on “The Algorithm” to do it for you. ● You’re a PBS Member Station, so it’s not like you’re swimming in money to run elaborate marketing campaigns. ● It’s hard to move people from your other platforms like newsletters and social media to YouTube. Collabs are the answer to these problems and a great way to grow your audience organically within YouTube. By tapping into like-minded audiences, you can expand and naturally reach new people. Do some research, find the right partner, film something cool and watch the new viewers roll in. Cultivating Audience | Online Collaborations, a.k.a. “Collabs” ● Marketing the collab: Doing as much as you can to promote your collab partner will, in turn, incentivize them to promote you and set you up for additional collaborations in the future. It also shows other potential collaborators that you are a good potential partner. You can use the following for promotions: c A pinned comment that links to the collaborated episode c The community tab c Social media c Other organic promotions like email, newsletters, websites or whatever’s at your disposal ● Paid media: Hey, if you’ve got it… PART 8: MEASURE YOUR RESULTS In addition to the typical performance measurement, pay special attention to these details coming from channel pages: c New viewers c Subscribers added c Traffic Sources Ideally, all these indicators will be higher than usual, proving that the collab worked! DIVE DEEPER For more, check out YouTube’s own Collaboration Tips via YouTube Help. Happy Collab-ing!

# Page 51^page=51
100 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 101 TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y REFLECTION Leveraging Talent Getting the Most Value Out of Your Host and Talent YouTube is all about people. They don’t call it “You”Tube for nothing. The ability for viewers to connect with YouTubers, leave comments and form parasocial relationships is one of the most powerful facets of the platform. With that in mind, leveraging your talent can make a huge impact on your series’ success on the platform. This section will discuss best practices for talent engagement, what works when, and why. You’re excited. You just hired a really cool host to be in your new show! Fantastic! Not only are the promotional benefits of talent obvious, but there are also invisible benefits. Did someone ask for a boost from YouTube’s Algorithm???? YouTube likes reliability, so if it sees a popular name, it’s more likely to serve it up because it already knows people like that person. Hopefully, your talent contract outlines the terms of promotion for the series, and you are talking with them early and often about what shape promotion will take. This section does not cover traditional publicity (a.k.a. earned media) but instead focuses on practical tips and advice for digital producers and marketers on how to work with talent to engage audiences — theirs and yours. HOW TO EASILY PROMOTE AND AMPLIFY Talent brings with them their own fans. This is the most obvious and immediately beneficial way to make the most of your talent. ● Share with their networks. Having your talent share content should be a priority and a clear expectation of your work together. c Discuss with them in advance which platforms they prioritize and do what you can to prioritize their wishes. It will go a long way! h For example: if they are mostly on TikTok and Instagram, make sure they have a vertical version of the trailer. Prioritize their needs early and often, and they’ll be much more willing to promote throughout the series. c Keep in mind, your audience hears you talk about your new stuff all the time. Their audience may be new to PBS entirely. Promotion coming from them is often a lot more valuable than promotion coming from you. Plan accordingly. ● Make it easy. Sharing content should be turnkey. Provide pre-written messages (with different options!) and easy-to-download assets. c Formal tool kits like Social Press Kit are options that can make pre-written messaging and asset-sharing easy, but your mileage may vary if you find them more useful than a simple Google Doc. See how GBH did this for “25 Years with Lidia: A Culinary Jubilee” or how KQED did for a “Deep Look” episode on cockroaches. Cultivating Audience Question 1: What are the pros and cons of each host? Question 2: What strengths would you want to leverage? Question 3: What weaknesses might you need to navigate? Imagine you are making a YouTube show, and you are deciding between a few hosts. The choices include: An established YouTuber: They have their own channel that discusses something tangentially related to yours. They have a good following on YouTube but are small on other social media platforms. Because they’re already established on YouTube, they are doing this mostly as a job rather than a launchpad for their YouTube career. A popular content creator: This person is huge (1 million-plus followers) across social platforms, but has no YouTube presence. They’re interested in supporting the series, but YouTube is not their main platform. An up-and-comer: This person has a small but growing audience on either YouTube or a social platform. They’re eager to do whatever they can but don’t have the existing footprint. Guidebook users will be able to consider talent-based promotional strategies. Choosing a Host

# Page 52^page=52
102 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 103 ● Be clear on timing. Provide talent with a clear sense of your schedule: X publishes Tuesday at 11, Y publishes Wednesday at 1, etc. A content calendar that they can continually refer to is important. c Talent will often be unsure of what they’re allowed to post or if any content pieces are being held back or embargoed. Be clear about this up front. c Check in with talent when sharing assets. Confirm that they have what they need! ● Encourage free-flowing, personalized promotion. Your talent might be eager to share things with their network in unofficial capacities — maybe it’s a blog post or a cell-phone photo they took on set or even a custom video. Let them! c Unless you are working on a drama series, do not be precious about spoilers. If you’re like PBS Digital Studios and work in nonfiction, there is no such thing as spoilers. Let them post what they want. If you need to clarify messaging or timing, loop them in early about those specifics. c Give them assets and encouragement and say yes to everything (within reason). c Do give the details (time, date, channel), but don’t tell them what to say. Hearing talent say “Watch [name of series] starting October 1, only on the YouTube Channel Name, pbs.org and the PBS app” sounds lifeless and corporate. Cultivate enthusiasm by letting them do the promotion in their own words. ● Mind the network effect. Your talent may have connections to influential organizations or individuals, which can further extend your reach. It’s gauche to directly ask who else they’re connected to, but it’s helpful to find ways to discuss connections organically. TAILOR TO THE PL ATFORM Different platforms have their own nuances when it comes to talent. Platforms evolve with time but these are four examples to learn from in the current landscape. YOUTUBE If YouTube is your primary publishing channel, it’s an incredibly important place to help facilitate talent sharing. 1. Community tab: This is a surprisingly underused tool among a lot of YouTubers and stations. Since it allows direct linking to new videos without leaving the platform, it should be prioritized accordingly. If your talent already has an active YouTube channel, provide them with square images or GIFS and a bit of copy and have them link to your page. Read the chapter on how to write Creating Engaging Community Tab Posts. 2. Shorts: Offer vertical video to talent to share on Shorts instead of the station’s channel. INSTAGRAM ● Tagging: Always tag your talent when posting to Instagram. ● Collabing: Don’t just tag them! Collab with them. h The collaboration feature allows multiple parties to share the same video as a mutually hosted video with shared view counts. If you are the host, go to Tag > Invite Collaborator and find the account. They will get a notification asking to accept the collaboration, and the video will share to their followers just as if they had uploaded it themselves. Cultivating Audience | Leveraging Talent FACEBOOK ● Cross-posting: Facebook has a similar collaboration feature as Instagram: one video, multiple accounts. This is best leveraged on Facebook Reels and regular video posts. TIKTOK TikTok lacks a comparable collab/cross-post feature. If you’re working with someone with a large TikTok following and you’re looking to share things with their network, it’s best to try: ● Reposting: The repost feature is a great option that allows your videos to be reposted by another account. Their followers will see your video on their “For You Page” similar to how you might share a post on Facebook from your friend. ● Stitching: This is when a creator uses part (up to five seconds) of another TikTok video and adds to it. Discuss with talent a clip for them to react to or add on to. ● Dueting: This is when a creator split-screens another video and talks alongside it. This is mostly used for reaction videos or commentary. ● Natively uploading: Give them clips to share! Consider having the talent share the video natively instead of an official PBS channel, especially if clips or bespoke content is part of their agreement. Remember, they may have a bigger following than you do. Plus, your fans hear from you all the time. Tip! Turn the page to see a great example of how “Brave Spaces” did this. Tip: There are a lot of places your talent could be and not enough time to outline best practices for all. Platforms will rise and fall. The evergreen rule of thumb is to prioritize giving talent things to share and make it easy.

# Page 53^page=53
104 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 105 Case Study Series: Brave Spaces Station Name: Buffalo Toronto Public Media Author Name: Lynne Bader CASE STUDY “Brave Spaces” Host and Series Collabs Collabs were absolutely critical to vastly expand the reach of our series’ Instagram content. “Brave Spaces,” hosted by Devin-Norelle, leveraged several successful collabs. Devin-Norelle not only collab-posted videos to Instagram — with about 50,000 followers at the time, which increased the reach considerably — but also utilized personal connections with staff who run other major Instagram accounts, including @pride, @lgbt, @lgbtq and @glaad, which have millions of combined followers. This amplifying effect gave “Brave Spaces” far more viewership on Instagram than on any other platform: We gained over 15 times the organic viewership there than on YouTube. Cultivating Audience | Leveraging Talent THE P OWER OF ENGAGEMENT In addition to promotion, the other main way to work with talent is engagement. This allows them to interact directly with your fans and has several benefits: ● The Algorithm likes it: Comments from the brand are a signal to YouTube that the channel is engaged, and the algorithm rewards this. ● People like it: YouTube is, at its core, personal. Its original tagline was “Broadcast Yourself” (2005-2012). Having a relationship between viewer and publisher is central to what makes digital, well, digital. You don’t just watch a video. You watch, then go into the comments to discuss, possibly even with the actual creator of the video. That’s what people love. The more a host is in the comments, the more people love it.
