108 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 109 SUPP ORTING TALENT Leveraging talent shouldn’t be “take, take, take.” There should be give too. ● Offer to share their content, while being mindful of brand guidelines related to merchandise, brand restrictions and things of that nature. ● Maintain a positive comment section. Read more in Comment Moderation. If you want talent in the comments (and you do), civil comments are key. ● Make things easy on them. Flag comments for them, offer to post on their behalf — don’t ask too much. The easier and more positive you make the experience with talent, the more you’ll both get out of the relationship. Parting Thoughts Talent is one of the most important parts of YouTube, whether talking about editorial, promotion or engagement. Your talent relationship will be successful if you strive to foster a strong relationship, organize clear asks, make their life easy and discuss everything early and often. Now smash that subscribe button. Learn more in the next chapter... Cultivating Audience | Leveraging Talent

# Page 56^page=56
110 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 111 Making Great Vertical Video Now that you have some starting ideas for vertical videos, let’s take a step back and talk about best practices. It may be typical to think of TikTok, Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts as highly distinct types of videos in comparison to broadcast and YouTube/digital video. In reality, many of the fundamentals are the same. Brainstorming a Strong Topic DURING DEVELOPMENT Ideally, vertical video concepts are developed alongside the series and episodes. Waiting will only cause heartache later. Consider platform independence: Each video needs to stand on its own merit and be a complete story with a compelling takeaway. ● Although upselling a longer video is OK, it should not come at the expense of the video itself. The truth is, audiences rarely flip between platforms. ● Assume no familiarity! One should not expect viewers will have seen a long YouTube episode to understand the vertical video. This video should stand on its own. ● Just like YouTube, audiences rarely watch verticals in order, especially if the user doesn’t follow your account. The algorithm can also push older videos at different times, so you want to make sure your videos aren’t framed as a miniseries. Consider platform: Vertical videos can be shared as YouTube Shorts, TikToks or Instagram Reels, or some combination thereof. YouTube Shorts and Instagram Reels have a 3-minute cap, whereas a TikTok video can be up to 60 minutes. Since sharing the same video in all three places is generally a good idea, bear in mind that trends or available library music on one platform may not be relevant or available in others. SCRIPTING BEST PRACTICES The following is a typical format that works in vertical video: Hook: Treat every video like a pitch. On vertical platforms, it’s unlikely a person clicked in to watch. In the first few seconds, you are competing for attention. The hook should be bold, exciting, surprising or all three. Consider opening with your most interesting idea rather than building to it. Examples include the following: c Today I learned that ____ is actually ___. c What is the true story behind _____ ? c ___ and ___: What’s the difference? c Let’s talk about that time when ______. Promise to the viewer: Now that you’ve hooked them in, it’s time to convince them to stay. This is where you tell the viewer the reasons for watching to the end. Step back: This is a chance to reset. You have their attention and can begin making your argument. Just don’t spend too much time here! Interesting things: This is where you place the fun facts, surprising twists or general main argument of your video. Make sure you’re paying off on the promises you made earlier. Outro: A good outro is not as important as a good intro, but if the audience finishes by smiling or wanting to leave a comment or like it, this can lead to repeat viewership or engagement. Both boost your performance! Try to end with something clever. Cultivating Audience Tip! Set time to think and write your episode and vertical concepts at the same time. Oftentimes, what doesn’t make it to your YouTube script can be ideas for your vertical piece! Story templates: Especially for series of vertical videos, repeatable options will make things easier; for example, concepts like “The Most Surprising Thing About [related topic]” or “This Was Too Short for an Episode.” Think of the topic first, format second. It’s easy to fall into the trap of starting with an idea that follows a popular trend or has a particular style but lacks a strong script. The reverse is much easier and better. A strong script can be improved with a creative execution. Pitch multiple ideas and put special emphasis on the takeaway. The strongest takeaway will typically make the best video. For example: h Topic: What were pirates really like? h Takeaway: The popular perception you have of pirates comes from an invention of Hollywood and is probably wrong. h Watch the final video: What Were Pirates Really Like? from “Rogue History” by PBS North Carolina.

# Page 57^page=57
112 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 113 LOOK AND FEEL Let’s explore what video style works on vertical-video platforms. ● Direct to camera: In general, frame closely on the host or subject to help the audience focus on them. No wide shots. ● Tone: Energy and enthusiasm is encouraged, if it feels natural. A light tone is often appropriate, but it’s not a rule. ● Pace: Faster is often better (but not always), and jump-cutting is an accepted practice in a way that it wouldn’t be on other platforms. ● Production value: Lo-fi video is popular on social platforms. Recording in selfie style on a phone can help sell a casual tone, but keep lighting and audio top of mind. However… c High-quality production still works! It’s a myth that all videos need to be lo-fi to work on vertical platforms. High production value can and does work, but it’s still all about the story and takeaway. ● Stay true to your brand: Inauthenticity is the biggest pitfall. Do not try to cater to the platform; instead, focus on what you do best. THINGS TO AVOID What does not work for vertical? ● Slow build-ups: You have only a few seconds to grab the audience’s attention. Tell the best part of the story in the beginning! ● Wide shots: It’s 100 percent mobile viewership. Things will look small. Don’t do this. ● Promotional language: As mentioned before, concepts should be stand-alone. Overtly marketing videos tend to underperform no matter what. ● Dense scripts: Keep the script short, light and easy for the average person to understand. Avoid the use of long sentences and dense jargon. Again, audiences are coming in on auto-play, so don’t assume they’re paying close attention ● Poor audio or grainy video quality: Even if recording on a phone, the video and audio should always be clear. EXAMPLES OF STRONG VERTICAL VIDEOS Review the example vertical videos and their strengths. Cultivating Audience | Making Great Vertical Video REFLECTION Question 1: Did you find anything else particularly engaging about them? Question 2: How could you emulate or ideate these concepts for your own content? Video: Hoodoo vs. Voodoo Series: “Ritual” by Louisiana Public Broadcasting Strengths: Poses a straightforward, compelling question and unpacks it in detail Video: The Real Pokey-Mons Series: “Deep Look” by KQED Strengths: Uses episode footage to great effect, selecting self-contained segments that work great in vertical formats. Note the attention-grabbing “Watch this” opening line, which is very common on these platforms. Video: Cultural Appropriation Series: “Sovereign Innovations” by New Mexico PBS Strengths: Starts with a comedic skit and slightly sarcastic humor to engage audiences and discuss an important conversation, especially for younger viewers Video: Two Spirit Series: “Brave Spaces” by Buffalo Toronto Public Media (WNED) Strengths: Filled host-to-camera dialogue and is highly produced; uses lots of animation, graphics and bright colors that help enhance the story

# Page 58^page=58
114 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 115 Case Study: Eons Video: Why do these dinosaurs look like terrible lizards? Company Name: Complexly Author Name: Seth Radley CASE STUDY “Eons” on TikTok Developing vertical videos for “PBS Eons” was a great chance to meet our audience where they already are. Apps like TikTok had become wildly popular, but there wasn’t a lot of educational content being made yet. Our team considered what our audience loved about our YouTube videos and how we could translate that into shorter content that felt at-home on these vertical platforms, where videos were less polished. We determined that keeping our videos rigorous, accurate and informative was most important. If we maintained those editorial standards, we were comfortable embracing a more informal production style. As a result, our hosts shoot these on their phones while using a teleprompter app. To better connect with the viewers, we’ve deprioritized graphics, and the host is on-screen as much as possible. The great news: We’ve seen success! Our TikTok account has over 360,000 followers, and our YouTube Shorts have been viewed over 190 million times. PRACTICE ACTIVITY Building a Vertical Video Script You are a creative person. Templates are the antithesis of creativity, right? Everything turns out the same! Although this can be partially true, it’s also true that to push creative boundaries, one must first learn and master those boundaries. Following this template will establish the basics. Cultivating Audience | Making Great Vertical Video Activity: Write a vertical-video script for the video concept you identified at the beginning of the chapter. Start by writing down the format and video title. Format: e.g., selfie-style with B-roll, lo-fi green screen, hi-fi green screen, etc. Video Title: Folks are very unlikely to notice your video title because so much of vertical consumption is based on auto-play. However, on YouTube Shorts and Instagram, clicks still happen, so don’t sleep on it! Now, let’s write a script! Remember to read your script draft out loud to make sure it’s readable and casual. Bear in mind your host’s voice, if relevant. The script draft should clock in around 60 seconds. Hook: Be bold. Promise to the Viewer: Why should I watch to the end? Step Back: Establish some context; just don’t spend too much time here. Interesting Thing 1 We’ll leave space for three, but there’s no set number. Interesting Thing 2 (optional) Interesting Thing 3 (optional) Outro: Leave me with a smile and a desire to comment. There’s no single way to make a good vertical video, but by following these general guidelines, you will have a strong, viable and consistent starting point!

116 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 117 Creating Engaging Community Tab Posts Crafting Posts on Your YouTube Channel to Keep Audiences Engaged and Active The YouTube community tab is a great way to engage with your current subscribers and build community. When used effectively, the community tab can give your channel a unique voice while entertaining subscribers. It can also help creators gather feedback, improve channel growth and drive traffic to new videos. This section will break down different types of community tab posts you can use, best practices for crafting an effective post and how to make it work authentically for your channel. Analyzing Community Tab Posts Cultivating Audience | Making Great Vertical Video DIVE DEEPER Congratulations on scripting a vertical video! Don’t forget: Practice makes perfect. It also refines your scriptwriting technique. For inspiration, check out other sample vertical videos on our TikTok or Instagram @PBSDS, @PBSOrigins and @PBSTerra. Research your own favorite creators and their styles. Explore some of PBS Digital Studios’ favorite accounts outside the PBS orbit for inspiration! @WashingtonPost, @PlanetMoney, @NatGeo, @Vox or @AsapSCIENCE. TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y REFLECTION Write down what you noticed and keep it in mind during the next section. Question 1: What kinds of posts are these channels creating? Question 2: Is there anything that you really love or makes you cringe? Question 3: Do any posts inspire? If so, why? Identify two to three of your favorite YouTube channels. Go to their channel, and under the “Subscribe” button, select “Posts.” This is the tab where all written posts created by the channel live. # Page 60^page=60
118 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 119 How to Best Utilize the Community Tab Feature Let’s break down what kinds of community tab posts you can create for your channel. Below are types of posts that work well for many PBS Digital Studios channels. Remember that the less “promotional” the post sounds and feels, the more effective it will be in engaging with your fans. T YPES OF COMMUNIT Y TAB P OSTS You are not limited to this list, but it can give you an idea of what’s working across our network and beyond and what makes it effective. We’ll also cover best practices. Fun facts: Everyone loves a good fun fact! Is there anything interesting about a recent episode published on your channel that your subscribers wouldn’t know just by watching it? Any info that you cut from your script? This would be a good post that you can link back to new episodes. Behind the scenes: Fans love it when you can show content that feels exclusive or special. Do you have any cool photos or clips that show a glimpse of the work behind your series? This is a great way to show authenticity between fans and your host and build community. Check out this example from PBS Origins. Polls/quizzes: This is the most interactive way to engage and probably the most effective in getting immediate action from your subscribers. Have fun with this! You can use images or just text. It’s also a great way to help fans feel involved with the decision-making process of your series, and you can use their responses to better fit their wants and needs. Check out these “Deep Look” examples. Updates/channel news: Do you want to tell your audience about a new series or episode coming soon? Is there any exciting news about special guests, milestones hit or new hosts? Or maybe there are other updates like a delay in uploads or ending a series. Whatever it is, keeping your audience informed and up to date is key to maintaining community, showing transparency and building anticipation for anything coming up! This is a great opportunity to hear directly from your hosts with a special message or blog post. Here’s a nice one from PBS Storied. Other Types of Posts: ● Channel shout-outs: PBSDS uses its network of channels to cross-promote episodes and series. This is one of the main ways we promote our shows and share audiences. Here’s an example on PBS Voices, shouting out “Pan Pals” on PBS Food. ● Throwback episodes: Instead of simply promoting an episode to subscribers, we created a fun poll in which subscribers can vote for their favorite pirate word, and we weaved in a link to the first episode of “Rogue History” for Talk Like a Pirate Day. This was an engaging, less promotional way to get our subscribers’ attention. We received over 1,300 votes! ● Tentpoles/trending topics: Here’s a fun image poll example for Earth Month on PBS Terra! ● Memes: This has been really successful for PBS Digital Studios and many other channels. Memes can help foster community and encourage engagement between the creator and subscriber. BEST PRACTICES FOR COMMUNIT Y TAB P OSTS REFLECTION Questions: Which type would you most like to post? What resonates with you about it or fits your brand best? It’s common to use the community tab as a promotional tool that links to a recent episode. But it’s important to always create posts with value. Engaging posts perform better than promotional posts. Always prioritize publishing valuable or informative content over anything else. Here are some tips to keep in mind when publishing: ● Frequency: It is recommended to post on the community tab at least once per week, with a three-times-per-week max. Use non-upload days as an opportunity to publish a post and keep the channel active. If you publish full episodes multiple times per week, consider fewer community tab posts. If it’s a slower week, engage audiences with posts. ● Engagement: c GIFs/stills: On PBSDS channels, animated GIFs get twice as much engagement as still images. Make sure GIFs/images are square since YouTube automatically crops them. Note: GIFs should be under 16MB. c Memes: This is a favorite of PBSDS and other edutainment channels that performs very well. Adding humor or creating posts that people can resonate with will also boost engagement. Do this while still staying true to your brand and voice. Here’s an example of a PBS Terra meme. c Polls: YouTube allows image polls or text polls. For either option, there is a character limit, so keep it short and sweet! c Quizzes: Write questions up to 80 characters long. For now, images cannot be included on quiz posts. ● Keep in mind: c YouTube cuts off text after four lines, so make sure any links and important information is visible above the “Read more” fold. c Tag any creators you are mentioning in your posts, especially if posting about collabs or shout-outs. Use the @ symbol like on other social platforms. c Schedule posts to lighten the load for your social media manager and team! Cultivating Audience | Creating Engaging Community Tab Posts PBS Origins community tab post

120 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 121 EXAMPLES OF COMMUNIT Y TAB P OSTS Scroll through the most recent community tab posts on these channels for examples. Case Study Series: Deep Look Station Name: KQED Author Name: Sevda Eris CASE STUDY The Community Tab Workflow of “Deep Look” “Deep Look,” KQED’s award-winning wildlife YouTube series about small animals and plants, gives viewers a close-up look at the natural world they rarely get to see or experience. Since launching in October 2014, the series has gained over 2 million subscribers and more than 500 million views. We have been utilizing YouTube’s community tab feature since it was introduced in 2016 to engage with super fans, publishing one to two posts a week. “Deep Look” has experimented with multiple types of community tab posts using GIFs, photos, image polls and quizzes. Animal GIFs outperform those with just photos. GIF posts range from 500 to 900 likes and up to 60 comments, versus photos of animals, which earn about 300 likes and about 10 comments. There are exceptions. Photos of our team or of an award win do really well, with over 1,000 likes and over 20 comments. We saw a huge spike in engagement with the introduction of image polls and quizzes. Image polls can get up to 10,000 votes, over 500 likes and over 40 comments. The visual nature of the polls makes them more interactive and easily captures attention in someone’s feed. Interestingly, the quiz feature also performs well, even though it’s purely a text post. However, there is no option to include photos in the quiz feature. Both features highlight the trend of higher interest in gamified types of content. Cultivating Audience | Creating Engaging Community Tab Posts Channel: “Deep Look” by KQED Strengths: Uses graphics, GIFs, polls and quizzes to engage with subscribers through interactive games and challenges. Channel: PBS Storied by PBS Digital Studios Strengths: Subscribers hear directly from the series hosts through community tab posts. Fans also engage by weighing in on episode topics, participating in Q&As and more. Series: “Sound Field” by TPT — Twin Cities PBS Strengths: Using a light and casual tone, subscribers are highly engaged through polls and GIFs. Subscribers are also actively involved in the episode/topic selection process, making fans feel like a part of the show!

122 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 123 FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY Designing a Community Tab Post TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y Guidebook users will understand the basics of writing an engaging community tab post. Let’s design a community tab post for your channel so you can engage more with your subscribers! 1. Using an existing series or series concept, brainstorm different types of posts you can create to spark more engagement and conversation with fans based on the type of categories in this chapter. 2. Choose two to three types of posts. 3. Write or sketch a draft with any creative assets you may have. This is an exercise, so it doesn’t have to be fancy. 4. Ask your colleagues who like your show for feedback. Do they like the post ideas? Do they laugh or want to share them with other colleagues or friends? If not, ask questions to discern a better direction. 5. Finalize your post’s creative assets with your graphic designer or Photoshop, etc. 6. Once you have drafted a few posts, it’s time to post them! 7. Check in after a day or two to see which posts performed best. This will give you an idea of what your audience enjoys and what you can do more of in the future. This might be an area for trial and error until you find your community’s preferences — that’s okay! Parting Thoughts The YouTube community tab should be a fun and creative space for you and your fans. Ask them questions, share cool information and make them part of your process. The more connected they feel with your host and channel, the better your channel engagement will be. Currently, “Deep Look” creates a GIF challenge for the launch of each new episode, introducing the topic of the new video with a GIF and asking a question related to the animal in the episode. The next day, the answer is provided in the video description and added as a comment in the GIF post. Later in the week, an image poll is posted related to the new episode, asking fans what they find most interesting or surprising about an animal featured in the episode. Each community tab post has at least one link promoting one of the “Deep Look” episodes on YouTube. Overall, we’ve found that it’s good to mix up the types of community tab posts to keep fans engaged and excited about watching our videos. Cultivating Audience | Creating Engaging Community Tab Posts

# Page 63^page=63
124 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 125 TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y Comment Moderation Best Practices for a Lively, Respectful Comment Section Internet comment sections are famous for being full of reasonable people sharing respectful opinions on uncontroversial topics…right?! If only! This section will discuss best practices for moderating comments, wading into gray areas of when to delete comments, when (and how) to reply and when to do nothing at all. Navigating Gray Areas The Power of Comments One of the most powerful and differentiating features of digital content is the two-way conversation between producer and audience. Comment sections are part of what makes digital content great. Not only do they deepen the relationship with audiences by leveling them up from mere consumers to active participants, but algorithms also reward engaged audiences. However, what do we do about the negative things associated with comments? The practice activity was intended to show how the answers are not always going to be clear-cut. Internet commenters are really good at probing boundaries. Although there’s never going to be a perfect black-and-white policy, this section will discuss tips and tricks for dealing with the gray. Although this section will discuss YouTube comments specifically, most best practices can be applied to social platforms. Specific settings options may vary from platform to platform. STEP 1: CHOOSE THE RIGHT SET TINGS First, pick the right comment settings for your comfort level. YouTube has a few options: ● Hold Potentially Inappropriate Comments for Review is the default. Although it can be toggled off, it generally should be on. YouTube automatically filters key words and places comments in a review section, where they remain unpublished until approved by you. c Increase Strictness is an option if the subject is potentially sensitive. c Out of caution, YouTube’s filters will sometimes filter out words that are appropriate when used in context. This is why it’s important to regularly check your Held for Review section. ● Hold All Comments for Review requires every comment to be approved by a human before it shows up publicly. This is a good option if the topic is particularly sensitive and negative comments can be expected in large numbers. Just note that it does require more time. ● Disable Comments will turn off the comment section altogether. Although this can be reversed later, it’s generally only done if a comment section is getting toxic or out of control. Turning off comments can be interpreted as letting the trolls win and draws attention to the negative backlash, even if viewers can’t read the specific comments. Cultivating Audience Guidebook users will be able to assess comment moderation examples and determine when/if to step in. Imagine you just posted a video and are looking at the comments. Consider the following situations and how you would react. 1. People are discussing a topic but being a little rude to each other. 2. A person has commented an animated reaction GIF on your Instagram video and you don’t understand what it means. Someone has made a relevant comment but is using language that’s rated “R.” 3. Someone makes a comment, but the tone doesn’t align with the brand of your video and/or station. 4. A person has left an opinion which you (personally) strongly disagree with and think has negative consequences. But it isn’t technically or legally harassment/hate speech/provoking lawlessness/etc. Keep your answers in mind as you read the rest of this chapter. Notice if your response changes.

# Page 64^page=64
126 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 127 STEP 2: DEDICATE TIME TO REVIEW It’s important to set aside an adequate amount of time to review comments. Depending on the number of comments you get, this can be anywhere from a few minutes per day to several hours per week. ● The first few hours after launch are the most important time to review comments, especially if comments are set to “Hold for Review.” When there are fewer comments, each has more of a spotlight, whether good or bad. ● Check on comments daily for the first week or so. If a comment section is particularly lively, this timeframe should be longer. Then you can generally move to spot-checking. ● The community tends to self-regulate if you’ve cultivated a positive comment section. They will downvote negative replies or respond to criticism with support. This is ideal because it lets the community support itself without needing you to be the arbiter of what people are allowed to say. STEP 3: WHEN TO DELETE COMMENTS The decision of when to delete comments is something that should be discussed with your station’s legal or standards and practices teams. Below are best practices for conditions in which you may consider deleting comments. This list is not comprehensive. New cases, gray areas and edge cases are common and will continue to arise. Consider this a starting point. Personal judgment is still required, and getting station or brand input is critical. ● Slurs: No exceptions. Although YouTube filters these kinds of words automatically, sometimes they slip through with creative grammar or spelling (like the use of ***, symbols, spaces, etc.). PBS Digital Studios not only deletes the comment but also bans and reports the user. ● Hate speech: This can also not contain any filtered words but still convey a hateful message. It can also be implied or include dog-whistle language. c A dog whistle refers to a coded message that appears innocuous to most people but carries secret meaning to others. c The UN defines hate speech as “any kind of communication in speech, writing or behavior that attacks or uses pejorative or discriminatory language with reference to a person or a group on the basis of who they are. In other words, based on their religion, ethnicity, nationality, race, color, descent, gender or other identity factor.” This is the general definition PBSDS follows and uses to differentiate comments aimed at opinions from comments aimed at non-immutable characteristics like political affiliations. ● Threats: These kinds of comments also warrant a report to the platform. ● Criticism aimed at individuals/hosts: PBSDS allows comments criticizing the show, the channel and PBS as a brand but draws the line at criticism directed at individuals. We also delete personal attacks between commenters. ● Invocations of religious beliefs to make an argument: Not everyone shares the same religious foundations, so these arguments are unproductive. ● Spam or off-topic comments: These are a lower priority but try to keep comments clutter-free. ● Vulgarity: This usually gets filtered. PBSDS allows for a PG-13 comment section. ● Comments on appearances: PBSDS deletes these, whether they are directed to the host or anyone else appearing in the video. Comments on appearance often carry underlying meaning that is unproductive. This is especially true of sexualized comments or comments on body characteristics (hair, weight, etc.). This is done regardless of sentiment: A comment intended as a compliment would be deleted because it shifts the focus in a direction we don’t desire. Aside from these instances, PBS Digital Studios does not generally recommend deleting comments. RESP ONDING TO COMMENTS DOS AND DON’ TS What should be part of comment conversations? ● Do allow for criticism, arguments or opinions to be expressed, even those you may not agree with or find distasteful, provided they don’t cross another boundary. Typically, an engaged and loyal audience will self-regulate these comments. ● Do respond to: c Compliments about the show: It is always nice to say thanks, even better to ask what they enjoyed. c Suggestions: If they’re helpful. Don’t be disingenuous. c Clarifications: Chime in either with additional info or to refer them to other sources. c Questions: Answer if you have the answer or can get the answer from experts or crew. c Technical criticism: Acknowledge things such as “The subtitles don’t match,” even if you’re just saying thanks. Cultivating Audience | Comment Moderation

# Page 65^page=65
128 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 129 When commenting: h It’s always best to respond as the channel. YouTube includes a special marker next to the channel, indicating you are officially that person, not a random person. After all, who really notices a commenter’s username? h You’ll typically want to identify yourself if responding as the channel. Signing the comment with your name or initials at the end is a good way to do this. Parting Thoughts Reading and responding to comments is fun and extremely rewarding when everyone loves what you do! Unfortunately, that’s not always going to be the case. When publishing videos on the Internet, you will inevitably deal with trolls, critics and worse. Go into any new series or channel with a clear plan so you can maintain vibrant, productive comment sections and ensure the well-being of your staff and creative collaborators. ● Do not respond to: c Anything from the Delete list: Truly, just delete it. c Low-effort criticism: “This video is bad” does not deserve a reply. ● Tread carefully with: c High-effort criticism: This is a broad category, but it encompasses any comment criticizing our argument, conclusion, the facts presented, etc. Here, it’s advisable to consult as a team or bring in experts as needed. “High effort” is not the same as a good argument. It merely distinguishes that the commenter gave a reason for their critique. “Low effort” criticism like “This video is bad” does not deserve a reply. Tips when responding to comments like these: ● Be brand safe. Comments are a representation of the PBS brand, so civility should be maintained. Use of language should be rated PG. ● Take the higher road. Even if the comment is unpleasant or something you disagree with, still open with “Thanks for watching,” etc., to establish that you are making this comment in good faith and respect people’s rights to leave comments. ● “Once is a response; twice is an argument.” Avoid multiple rounds of responses because they come across as argumentative. Ongoing arguments can be draining and begin to occupy a lot of head space. It’s better to walk away. TIPS FOR HOSTS OR PRODUCERS WANTING TO ENGAGE Getting hosts and producers engaged helps to encourage audience participation and builds community. When doing so, bear in mind: ● Communicate the station’s comment moderation policy, and let the hosts and producers know that responding to comments is not a requirement. ● Comment sections have the potential to be unpleasant experiences. If reading the comments is taking an emotional toll, please stop and seek community or clinical support if necessary. For additional resources on comments and mental health, see the YouTube Creator Safety Page. ● Don’t scroll to the very bottom. That’s where the negativity is generally going to live. Cultivating Audience | Comment Moderation

# Page 66^page=66
130 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 131 Comment Engagement Tactics The Power of Reading and Responding to Audience Comments The comment section is one of the most powerful tools at your disposal. It’s the two-way connection between the producer and consumer as well as the consumer and other consumers, which is a unique and defining characteristic of digital media. This section will teach you how to make the most of your comment section and engagement. Think of a brand or personality who is really engaged with their fans. It could be someone you personally follow and are invested in or someone who is good at audience engagement. This is an exercise, so estimates and impressions are fine! ● Write down why you chose this creator/brand. ● Take stock of their engagement practices. Write down your answers. ● How often do they respond to people? Are they liking posts? How many times per day/week? How many people do they reply to? ● What kinds of things do they respond to? ● What kinds of things do they say to people? ● How do you think this benefits them? ● How do you think this benefits their fans? The Philosophy Behind Comment Engagement The importance of comment engagement can be broken into two key parts: reading and replying. PART 1: WHY YOU NEED TO BE READING COMMENTS Broadcast media develops an idea, films it much later and publishes it even later. Teams wait for Nielsen for viewership metrics and reviews to hear what viewers think, with occasional social media chatter if your show is popular enough. This whole process from idea to feedback takes a very long time, which creates a disconnect between what audiences are saying about your work and what you can realistically change about it next time. In contrast, digital media allows for immediate feedback and iteration. You can have an idea today, film next week and publish a week later. An exaggeration, but you get the gist: Timelines are faster. But that’s not all! Viewership data is available in real time, and comments are flying in fast in a dedicated comment section right below the video. Meanwhile, you are mid-process making your next video, and it won’t be long until it publishes. There is interesting discussion and feedback in those comments, and the timeline means you can do something about it. Reading, engaging and even acting on comments brings unique benefits to your digital series. Step 1: Using Comments for Inspiration and Iteration People have good ideas! Fandoms are an amazing place where the people watching the videos are often incredibly knowledgeable about the subject, sometimes even more than the people making the videos! Comment sections can be used as places to source ideas, whether by asking directly or by making unsolicited suggestions. h For example: A comment from one video was used as a jumping-off point for a new short from “A People’s History of Native America.” Cultivating Audience TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y REFLECTION Guidebook users will be able to identify what is a good use of fan interactivity. Let’s assess basic engagement practices and determine whether your station is implementing them. Question 1: How many of these things is your station doing? Question 2: Could or should you be doing them? (Answer Key: Yes, you should definitely be engaging in the comments!!!) Question 3: Why aren’t you? Evaluating Fan Interactivity

# Page 67^page=67
132 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 133 Step 2: Using Comments to Make Improvements Reading comments also provides technical feedback for your show. Viewers on early episodes of “Far Out” left several comments about the font selection and color palette. These were adjusted in subsequent releases, and the commenters were thanked for their feedback. This not only improved the show but also demonstrated the producers were listening and taking comments seriously. The pilot episode of “Weathered” received many negative comments on the style of the series. The audience felt it too closely mirrored broadcast documentaries, positioning the host as a journalist in the field conducting interviews and utilizing lots of on-location B-roll. Notable adjustments were made in subsequent episodes to make the host into more of a communicator. The series pivoted to utilize more graphics, charts and other visual elements to explain the science, rather than interviews. With these modifications, the series then took off to great success. It is now the most-viewed digital series for “PBS Terra.” Early episodes of “Space Time” were noticeably lighter in tone and covered silly topics like “What Planet Is Super Mario World?” or “Could You Fart Your Way to the Moon?” When more serious topics were covered, the comment sections lit up with advanced physics chatter between fans. Reading through these comments revealed a desire for more serious content, which would become the norm. Host Matt O’Dowd says, “My favorite comments are when people smarter than me start responding to other people’s questions about some pretty hairy physics. That’s when I think my work here is done!” Step 3: Using Comments to Measure Impact Comment sections can be tumultuous at times. But as public media producers, the topics PBS covers are powerful and important, and that is reflected in the comments. For instance, PBS Digital Studios series have heard from: “Ritual” viewers reflecting on their personal history as it relates to the series Series have also leveraged community tab polls as another way of finding inspiration or specific feedback, like when “Insectarium” used a poll to select its next episode topic on the mantis (also known by its Latin name of mantodea). “Brave Spaces” viewers highlighting the importance of the series in the community “Insectarium” viewers asking for — and receiving! — practical advice from the host on how to professionally pursue entomology Reading comments as they come in gives a good sense, anecdotally, of whether you are reaching your target audience or achieving an impact goal. Designing Effective Audience Surveys discussed how polls and surveys can be used to directly quantify comments — namely, by establishing a clear goal and defining the right question to measure it. Combining anecdotal evidence and direct measurement is a great way to build an understanding of your audience. In addition to being incredibly affirming to read, acknowledging and responding to comments like these is one way to be accountable to the viewers who make public media possible. Cultivating Audience | Comment Engagement Tactics

# Page 68^page=68
134 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 135 PART 2: WHY YOU NEED TO RESP OND TO COMMENTS Reading is one thing, but interacting is what takes comment sections to the next level. Making sure your series has the resources — people and time — needed to both moderate and respond to comments is critical, especially because the benefits are too good to ignore. Step 1: Working With the Algorithm The algorithm matters because YouTube makes it matter. YouTube’s algorithm has been adjusted over the years to prioritize different things: clicks, watch time and, nowadays, “satisfaction.” It is not enough to get people in the door and keep them there. YouTube (the company) wants to make sure audiences enjoy what they see. This is not altruistic: A satisfied customer is a repeat customer. Engagement metrics are a signal to the YouTube algorithm that the video is generating interest, which makes it more likely to be recommended. The algorithm also puts emphasis on comments made by the publishing channel. This signals that the channel itself is engaged, which is another positive signal. Thus, generating comments should be a priority. For instance, several episodes of “Sovereign Innovations” saw increased viewership because host Cheyenne Bearfoot personally responded to early comments. This is a tactic explained in Leveraging Talent. Step 2: Building Relationships With Your Fans The ability to directly interact with fans is one of the biggest benefits of digital and social media. In an ideal scenario, the viewer isn’t just watching a video and engaging with other viewers around a topic; they can also talk about it with the creator! This kind of give and take with fans keeps people coming back for more. Commenters also tend to light up when the host shares more personal stories. This reciprocity builds a gratifying relationship with your audience. Step 3: Cultivating Financially Invested Fans Interacting with fans pays off. Making money from a digital property is possible, whether by monetization, sponsorship or direct fundraising through the station or a tool like Patreon. PBS Digital Studios properties that have the most success with fundraising do so primarily by providing access to the hosts and interacting with the fans. “Space Time” has been able to cover a majority of their production costs through Patreon donations, and become profitable when Ad Revenue, Sponsorships, and merchandise sales are factored in. When considering cultivating financially invested fans, understand what offers the best payoff for your overall funding model. Regardless of what you choose, always focus on making a great show that people want to watch. You can’t monetize a show until it has enough viewership! Cultivating Audience | Comment Engagement Tactics Tip! Triple-check the contract for any potential revenue restrictions by a funder or distributor! “Sovereign Innovations” host Cheyenne Bearfoot responds to comments from the PBS Voices YouTube account.

# Page 69^page=69
136 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 137 Case Study Series: Sound Field Station Name: TPT — Twin Cities PBS Author Name: Marissa Blahnik CASE STUDY Managing the Audience Engagement of “Sound Field” “Sound Field” is lucky to have an active community on our channel. It is full of folks who are quick to reply to prompts on our “Sound Field” Community Tab and who tell us what we did well and what we should do next via their comments. We usually drop a prompt at the start of a season, searching for topics or even particular artists that our community thinks we should cover. The unexpected benefit of doing this is we can hype existing episodes when we get suggestions we’ve already covered! To keep the audience engaged, producer Jojo Lorenzo is quick to reply to comments and intersperses memes and polls to keep it light. The audience also indirectly influenced the format of “Sound Field.” In the early days, we closely monitored the view duration graphs and noted that audiences were often clicking away when our experts would be on screen too long. To solve this, producer Jojo Lorenzo adapted by having the hosts summarize some of the expert’s commentary, either on camera or in VO with animations, and brought the expert themselves on camera for only the most vital parts of their commentary. Parting Thoughts Having an audience-first approach is about clearly defining the audience at the outset and continually referring to them throughout the run of your series. Engaging in the comments increases your engagement metrics, builds community, improves your show and makes a big and sustaining impact. Cultivating Audience | Comment Engagement Tactics Learn more in the next chapter...

140 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 141 Pitch proposals come in all shapes and sizes. But there are best practices to showcase your station’s skills and ability to produce a digital series from start to finish. A fully developed pitch proposal provides a clear vision for potential funders and distributors and, ultimately, a path to greenlight your project. STEP 1: MAINTAINING AND LEVERAGING EXECUTIVE BUY-IN Know whether you have the support of your leadership from the get-go. What will leadership support? How does your pitch support strategic goals? What might they have questions about? Identify the strengths and weaknesses and pros and cons of the proposal to help with decision-making. If you don’t have executive support to create the pitch, you won’t have executive support to submit the pitch. Execs also help reinforce or solidify buy-in from other team members, including direct reports, leadership teams and other key stakeholders. If people are excited about the project from all levels, it will make collaborating easier and fulfilling when the project gets off the ground. STEP 2: DRAFT STRONG PROP OSAL DOCUMENTS Always include the following in digital series proposals: PRACTICE ACTIVITY Outlining a Comprehensive Pitch Proposal The centerpiece of a pitch proposal is the creative concept, but the logistical materials are just as important. The clearer the details, the higher your chances of getting noticed — whether you’re submitting to a funder, distributor or executive. Let’s identify those supporting documents. Guidebook users should be able to create a pitch proposal with all essential elements for a successful submission. TAKEAWAY Grab the series concept that you made in Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck. If you haven’t written a formalized concept for your pitch, use a working pitch idea. MATERIALS NEEDED Pitching Potential Funders and Distributors A successful pitch proposal needs more than just an incredible concept and clear audience: You need to instill confidence that the digital series can, in fact, be made. From budgeting to timelines to staffing your creative team, this section provides a breakdown of pitch proposal essentials to help you stand out in a crowded field. Complete the companion chapter Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck first for this chapter to be most effective. Reimagining Workflow Tip: Include at least as many episode ideas as you would produce if the proposal is accepted. Pretend you are pitching PBS a new series. Take five minutes to brainstorm what documents or logistical information you think should be included in the proposal, beyond the series concept. What will indicate your team can successfully produce a digital series? Why did you choose those items? What story do they tell? 1. Series overview: Your series premise should include a detailed description of your goals for the digital series, as well as a description of the desired format, look and feel, along with influence and inspiration examples. If you are using a predetermined prompt from a request for proposals (RFP), how are you addressing the series requirements with your creative concept? What’s something special you’re bringing to the table? Refer to Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck. 2. Potential episode topics/ideas: Each episode idea should include compelling titles and one to three sentence descriptions. Sometimes, funders and distributors look for even more detail such as potential visuals, experts or interviewees and an explanation of why the episodes are important to the target audience. Do initial research! Don’t pitch an episode you can’t follow through on. 3. Social media strategy or vertical video strategy: At PBS, a digital series includes YouTube episodes as well as bespoke vertical videos. These are original digital videos that share a great short story, not clips or promos. Read Making Great Vertical Video for inspiration. Include a brief description in your proposal explaining how the series will reach audiences beyond a single platform. How will you leverage your social (a.k.a. vertical) videos? What will your series’ social media plan be? BRAINSTORM

# Page 72^page=72
142 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 143 Reimagining Workflow | Pitching Potential Funders and Distributors Tip: Keep all three budgets in one spreadsheet on different tabs for ease! Tip: Reach out to potential hosts for introductory interviews to get a sense of who might be the best fit (or not). 4. Potential host(s): Will the series have a host? Know the answer to this question when you’re building your proposal and provide options. Think realistically about who is available and what you can pay them. Propose several options so you have choices. 5. Key creative personnel (digital team + their experience): Show potential distributors and funders you are building a team with the right subject matter and technical expertise. Who will help you achieve the series goals? Confirm they will be available if the project gets greenlit. Consider how the team you choose reflects the needs and interests of diverse audiences. Are they on staff or contractors? Creative personnel in pitch proposals include:  Series director/producer  Director of photography  Editor  Writer(s)/researcher(s)  Animator/motion graphics (as applicable)  Potential subject-matter experts, who could be hosts, writers and/or consultants 6. Proposal budgets: It’s tempting to put together a quick budget to show you have a sense of how much (or little) something will cost to make, but distributors and funders want to make sure you can tell a great story within your budget. Be as accurate as possible. If proposing a brand-new series, distributors and funders might want to separate the pilot budget from the rest of the series episodes. With pilots, distributors want a proof of concept before confirming a full season, and pilot costs typically run higher than the rest of the series episodes. To be safe, draft the following:  A total budget  A separate pilot budget  A remaining series budget for the rest of your episodes (102-end of series) 7. Estimated timeline: How long will the pilot episode take to produce? How long will each subsequent episode take to complete? Consider expected deliverables against the timeline if you know them. Create a visual timeline to demonstrate a realistic understanding of how long you anticipate the project will take to complete. Remember that digital series typically produce and publish on a regular basis, as opposed to delivering the full product all at once. Read Designing a Digital Project Timeline and its companion chapter Building a Digital Editorial Timeline for more. 8. Community engagement goals:  Partners: How will you ensure a skilled production team? How will you support and include your entire team’s ideas, voices and roles during the project?  Audience: How will you learn about the target audience, including their values, preferences, motivations and cultural references?  Storytelling: How will the narratives you are proposing impact the communities being represented in your content? Who will you engage to make sure your content is accurate, relevant and respectful?  Partnerships: What new or existing community partnerships can you develop to support this project?

Parting Thoughts Now you know what workflow elements to include in a strong pitch proposal to convince funders, distributors and executives to support your project. Building a strong pitch proposal is a lot of work, but once you have something solid in place, you can shop it around to different funders and distributors without having to do the heavy lifting each time. Consider creating a pitch proposal template to work off to make sure pitches follow a similar format and process and to help remember all the necessary steps! Reimagining Workflow | Pitching Potential Funders and Distributors Case Study Series: Insectarium Original Pitch Deck: Insectarium pitch deck Company Name: American Museum of Natural History Author Name: Erin Chapman A well-defined workflow shows potential funders and stakeholders that not only do you have a killer idea, but you also have the producing chops to make it happen. You can dream up a fantastic story arc for a star-studded, CGI-driven narrative, but without a clear plan for bringing that to the screen, those dragons aren’t getting off the ground. With any size project, you need to: ● Show feasibility and scalability: How will you produce multiple episodes and set your series up to grow? ● Set clear expectations: What are the roles and responsibilities of your team members? ● Have a plan to mitigate risks: What are potential bottlenecks, and how will you proactively address them? These all enhance the credibility of your pitch and demonstrate a path to a successful final product. You want to convince the people who are reviewing your pitch that this is a great idea and you can make it happen. Don’t underestimate the time it can take for pre-production, filming, and post-production and delivery. In creating your production plan, make sure you consider potential challenges and include contingency buffers, just as you would in drafting a budget. In the case of “Insectarium,” it wasn’t that production took any longer than we’d thought… but at the pitch stage, we hadn’t fully considered the challenges of looking for insects outside in the wintertime. We were able to make it work despite the below-freezing temperatures because we’d built in some wiggle room. No matter the series, a production timeline is dependent on the time of year and the schedules of your talent and team members. Keep in mind that the platonic ideals of a pitch deck don’t always reflect the realities of life and have a backup plan! For more on our process, watch and download materials from our Digital Voltage “Power Pitching” session. CASE STUDY Pitching a Realistic Workflow The “Insectarium” team on location in freezing temps for their cockroaches episode.

146 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 147 Step 1: Understand and Communicate Project Requirements Write a scope of work (SOW) or project brief describing the project requirements. These typically include: How to Set Up a New Digital Project for Success Digital Workflow 101 For a new digital series, you will first want to spend time assessing the bigger picture to identify all the different — but equally important — parts of the puzzle that will help meet your goals. This section provides the key steps and touchpoints to set up, plan and manage your new project and resources to streamline your work. The Importance of Building Digital Infrastructure Just like broadcast, digital production and platforms still require meticulous planning, scripting, filming and editing to maintain high-quality standards. The demand for engaging content on digital platforms necessitates an iterative process with frequent updates and releases. These can influence and change production timelines and staffing needs. Meanwhile, digital workflow expertise (or lack thereof) can impact the bottom line and how much (or how little) you can do within a given project scope. Follow these steps as you sketch out your latest project to ensure your project is on the right track. 6 STEPS FOR STRONG DIGITAL INFRASTRUCTURE Reimagining Workflow  A creative vision plan  Contract obligations  Deliverables  Funding requirements/restrictions  The total budget  Team members and hiring needs  A timeline Check out the Kickoff Meeting Agenda template to prep for your project kickoff. Check out the Digital Team Roles and Responsibilities template from PBS Digital Studios to start and read Digital Production Team Structure for more. Step 2: Build a Strong Digital Timeline Building a strong timeline accounts for project needs and anticipates potential variables by building in buffer time. Digital timelines are ordered differently than broadcast, so it’s important to know the similarities and differences — more on that later. Whether it’s contracting, pilot development or iterative publishing, you want to fold into a digital workflow while still leaving space to work out first-time kinks. Step 3: People Power — Identify the “Who’s Who” After absorbing project requirements, start thinking about who will need to be on your team to complete the work. The pitch likely identified potential digital team members, but now it’s time to confirm those individuals and their roles and responsibilities. Having a strong sense of who you need on your team will help you solidify your budget and timeline. For your digital team, consider each person’s skills, expertise and experience and how they align with the project’s specific requirements. Digital series production teams can vary in size. Smaller, lean teams can consist of only a few people, or they can accordion to dozens, depending on the size of the production and staffing architecture at your station. Again, items like budget and timeline often impact what a team ultimately looks like. Step 4: Communicate to Your Team What They Are Responsible For Having a clear understanding of the roles and responsibilities of your team is critical in a fast-moving production environment. This is especially true if members on your team are wearing multiple hats. If their roles aren’t clear, it creates confusion, frustration and further setbacks that will affect your timeline. At the beginning of the project, create a document that delineates each role needed for the project, who will fill the role and what their responsibilities include.

Parting Thoughts Executing a great idea takes careful planning and usually several people. Knowing how to set up a new digital project before the creative work begins will always help you set up yourself and your team for success. Step 5: Budget and Scope, a.k.a. “What Makes Digital ‘Digital’ and Not Broadcast?” There is still a misconception in our industry that digital productions are faster and cheaper and don’t take as many resources as broadcast productions. Although some digital productions are faster and cheaper, the majority are not. More often, digital productions are competitive with broadcast standards, resources, budgets and quality. Digital production is becoming more ubiquitous, and although digital production offers opportunities for innovation and flexibility, it requires careful planning and investment to achieve efficient and cost-effective results. When you are drafting your budget, consider the following: ● Gather: Collect project needs, all associated costs and wish-list items. ● Research: Who is available? What are their rates? What can you afford? ● Limits: What is your budget or funding limitations? Step 6: Tools and Databases Keeping track of all the ongoing logistics for your team requires tools that will successfully support your operation. Below are examples of complex project areas where the tools you use will help anchor your project management. ● Communication: A communication hub provides a centralized platform for conversations, discussions and shared information. Tools like Slack or Teams can provide a streamlined method for communicating updates, files and feedback in real time. This enhances collaboration and ensures that everyone is on the same page regarding project progress and deadlines. Having one place where all production discussions take place creates transparency among team members. ● Project management software: Databases and project management software like Microsoft Excel, Google Spreadsheets, Airtable, Smartsheet, ClickUp, Monday.com and others can help you keep track of deliverables, timelines and series information. Consider your database or project management tool as the “one source of truth” that everyone can refer to for any project information. For example, PBS Digital Studios uses Airtable as an editorial calendar and to keep track of all metadata for every single series PBSDS distributes across platforms. ● Regular check-ins: Establishing cadences for regular meetings is important to ensure a digital project stays on track. Regular brainstorming meetings, check-ins and daily stand- ups are good ways to check on how the team is working toward final project goals. These types of check-ins are imperative for collaborating and making sure everyone is heard and included. Be sure check-ins extend to your creative freelancers too! Take notes or post quick updates so everyone has access to what was discussed and decided. ● File management: In addition to a project management tool, create a system for file organization and management. Cloud-based tools like BOX, Google Drive, Microsoft OneDrive and others can make it easier for you and your team to know where important assets are being stored. 150 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 151 3. Limits: After completing the research, start identifying your limitations. ● What are your budget or funding limitations? What can you afford? ● Where can you advocate for pay equity? ● What items could go over budget (e.g. editor time, shooting days, etc.)? 4. Iteration: Draft your budget. Review and revise until approved. 5. Be flexible: Unknowns will arise. Be calm, logical and thoughtful in assessing opportunity and risk. Now it’s time to draft your budget! 1. Gather: Collect all project needs, must-haves and nice-to-haves. Certain line items may fluctuate depending on how long you need to employ certain people or secure resources, so be sure to consult your production timeline. 2. Research: Information gathering in the beginning can identify cost-saving areas. ● What resources do you have access to? How much will they cost? Bring in internal collaborators as early as you can, alongside your usual partners. Ensure all needs are considered early and the proper resources can be reserved for your project priorities. While many consult development, marketing, graphic design, community engagement or PBS LearningMedia teams later in a broadcast production, it’s digital best practice to loop them in at the beginning. ● Who is available? What are their rates? Know industry rates for common roles including producers, writers, hosts, editors, directors of photography, etc. ● Are there necessary expenses you must factor in (e.g., taxes, fringe benefits, legal, insurance, clearances, etc.)? ● How can you create new opportunities for growth, play, authenticity and quality? Who is at the table? Who needs to be there? THE ART OF A DIGITAL BUDGET There are two main budget philosophies: scarcity and abundance. Much of the public media world often starts from a place of scarcity: “We only have X; what will we do with it?” Instead, it’s better to practice starting from a place of abundance. Ask, “Ideally, how would we make this?” and scale back from there. This ensures the must-haves and helps plan for surprises or wants. When building a budget, it’s important to remember that finance is all about perspective. Finances are incredibly creative and the first place you get to say “Yes, and…!” and play with any new idea. Money is powerful as well as practical. When creating new budgets, aim to get what you need plus one or two things that push the dial forward. FIVE STEPS TO WRANGLE A BUDGET Tip: PBS Digital Studios provides producers with this digital budget template. Building a Digital Budget Optimize Creative Freedom and Understand Resource Limits The principles for building a digital budget are the same whether you’re pitching or already greenlit. Executing a digital project requires careful planning and investment to achieve efficient and cost-effective results. This section will dive into key action steps for building a solid digital budget and provide recommendations for essential items to include in your budget scope. Analyzing a Prior Budget Guidebook users should be able to identify common budget limitations or overages. TAKEAWAY Reflect on a recent digital project’s budget. If you haven’t worked on a digital project before, reflect on a final financial report from a recent production you worked on. ● Where did line items run over? ● What resources would have made the project smoother or more successful? ● How many hours or dollars do you think you would have needed for those items or people? Did you know the answers to these questions? If not, who can you ask at your station? ACTIVITY Reimagining Workflow

154 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 155 Notice how Episode 2 starts with pre-production at the star, advancing clockwise through Research & Script Outline to Research & Scripting to Script Approval, through to the end of the episode. Halfway around the circle, Episode 3 starts the same cycle. Now Episode 2 is finishing as Episode 3 is advancing. The cycle repeats for Episodes 3, 4, 5 and onward. When producing iteratively, staffing is the hardest part because instead of everything taking place in concrete linear windows, the team works cyclically, one to two episodes at a time, to maintain publishing cadence and retain audiences. Make sure your team has enough bandwidth to take on a cyclical volume of work. This might mean moving them off certain projects, adjusting their portfolio to digital-only projects, increasing their hours on the project or adding a freelancer to the mix to help balance workload and quality output. THREE KEY FUNDER BUDGET FORMATS Every funder has their own way of doing things. Therefore, it is important to make sure your budget is in the right format. When proposing a brand-new series, distributors and funders might want to separate the pilot budget (101) from the rest of the series episodes (102-end of series). With pilots, distributors often want a proof of concept before confirming a full season. It’s easiest to have three different budget tabs in the same document containing the following: ● A total budget ● A separate pilot budget ● The remaining series budget for the rest of your episodes (102-end of series) Tip: The costs to produce a pilot episode typically run higher than remaining series episodes. A CYCLICAL STAFFING RHY THM Remember, digital workflow is cyclical, not linear. Because digital workflow is cyclical and overlapping, you must plan for continuous coverage! This is especially important when budgeting for a digital timeline. Check out this helpful graphic for what a continuous digital producing schedule can look like: Reimagining Workflow | Building a Digital Budget

156 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 157 Step 1: Think of a digital series you are working on or dreaming up. Write down the title and logline. Step 2: Make a list of all the “must haves” and “nice to haves” for your project (e.g. staff members, host, field and studio shoots, editors, etc.). Aim within reason. Tip: Don’t bring your executives a $5 million studio budget if you’re applying for public media grant dollars. Step 3: Estimate costs for each “must have” and “nice to have.” Standard rates vary for different digital line items. If you’re unsure, check with your production manager or use a cost-of-living calculator that helps estimate cost comparisons across different cities. Account for reasonable rate increases year over year. Just like for internal staff, you should be estimating a standard percentage of cost-of-living raise for each year of work or new project. Similarly, make sure you have factored in any inflation or market-relevant rate increases. Step 4: Add up all items to get a total number. This is your ideal budget number. FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY Now It’s Your Turn! Let’s practice making a digital series budget. REFLECTION ● Now that you have your ideal budget total, reflect on its feasibility. ● Is it above or below your actual budget cap? ● Is it similar to or higher than the funds you usually have access to? ● Does it seem realistic? If not, go through your list again and assess what could be trimmed. Tip! It is very common for production budgets to go through several drafts before meeting approval. Don’t be discouraged if a supervisor asks you to revise. Practice makes perfect! Based on your list, start plugging in items and estimates in either your station budget template or the PBS Digital Studios budget template. You can refer to these sample digital budgets for a realistic idea of what certain line items can cost. If needed, recruit other members from your team to help with the gathering and research components. Congratulations! You’re ready to present or discuss your digital draft budget. CASE STUDY Learned Lessons from “Ritual” If we were to produce “Ritual” again, we would allocate funds differently. Our ambitions for the show exceeded the $150,000 for eight episodes, but that did not deter us from going big. We originally invested significantly in three areas: ● Talent: We invested about a quarter of our budget in a star-power host who was completely worth it but spent much more than the average PBSDS show. ● Equity: We were especially committed to paying equitable wages to all collaborators, even with the limitations our project scope presented. ● Travel: Our show depicted rituals from the American South, and we wanted to see our beautiful region on screen. We budgeted $48,000 for lodging and travel for four large-scale, out-of-state episodes and two regional episodes. The first two areas paid off nicely: We had a talented, well-known host and dedicated collaborators. The third shifted almost immediately. Doing digital for the first time means learning as you go and iterating. Early on, we learned we needed to staff our archivist across all eight episodes — she saved producers time and asset money! We also needed to hire a colorist, hair and makeup for set and a more expensive director of photography. Our business office also instructed us to support internal staff costs (producers, editor, graphic design, crew). So we needed to revise the budget. Travel was the most flexible category. We decided to fly interviewees to New Orleans for filming instead of sending our sizable crew out of state. This saved tens of thousands of dollars, which went to other needs! Scale back on the instinct to go big in multiple areas. Know what your team can realistically achieve within a set budget. Allow wiggle room to iterate staffing to make the production process less stressful. And coordinate closely with your finance department to establish standard protocol that ensures alignment and ease in expense tracking, billing and reporting! Case Study Series: Ritual Station Name: Louisiana Public Broadcasting Author Names: Avery White and Emma Reid Reimagining Workflow | Building a Digital Budget “Ritual” host Tarriona “Tank” Ball on set.
