158 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 159 The Ins and Outs of Contracting Let’s say you’ve developed an idea for a digital project, but you are looking for funding and a distributor. After a lot of shopping around, you confirm a funder who will also distribute your series to make your digital project a reality. STEP 1: UNDERSTANDING THE CONTRACTING PROCESS Entering a contract negotiation process is the first big step to fully secure the deal. Contract negotiation will define and cement all partnership expectations and help establish comfortable working terms that protect both parties. Before signing anything, it is critical to understand the distributor and funder’s workflow and requirements. Review the following best practices to ensure you’ve got things covered. This is the time to negotiate! Consider what makes a good deal for you and what you are willing to compromise on. It’s not a one-way street. Contracting With a Distributor or Funder Best Practices and Key Terminology in Legal Agreements Negotiating a contract to secure funding and distribution of a digital series requires careful evaluation and an essential understanding of legal terms and obligations. When contracting with a distributor, funder or distributor who is also a funder (like PBS), there will be legally binding expectations. As a result, the scope of your digital series or project will differ from a station-funded project. Reading the fine print of a contractual agreement cannot be an afterthought! It is best practice to work with a lawyer or your legal department, who can help you fully understand what your company is responsible for and contractually obligated to do. Disclaimer: Consult your legal department for your station’s standard protocols and legal preferences when negotiating a contract. Parting Thoughts Investing time and research in building your digital budget always pays off in the end. A great story requires a great production team who has the time to focus on the work. Invest in getting the right people in the right roles who can work on this project continuously. Hire roles that support and strengthen the authenticity of your story. One of our mentors liked to say, “A budget is a moral document.” What are the values and priorities at your station? Do they align with your budgets? Food for thought! Additional Resources For more information, watch these two event recordings about building effective digital budgets. • Why Authentic Storytelling Begins With Your Budget, Digital Voltage Workshop, 6/20/24 (recording) • Digital Workflows, Budgets and Process (PBSDS), 1/17/24 (recording) Refer to these examples and resources when crafting your digital budget. • Example pilot and series budgets • PBSDS financial cost report • Roles and responsibilities template • Planning for accessible audio and video • Described and Captioned Media Program RESOURCES Reimagining Workflow | Building a Digital Budget Reimagining Workflow

# Page 81^page=81
160 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 161 REFLECTION ● Clearances — music and rights licensing: Typically, producers must obtain permission to use assets owned by third parties because those materials may be protected by trademark, copyright, privacy or other laws. The first step in the clearance process is to determine what creative works were used or will be used in the production. c Will you use assets fully created by the producer, or will you secure third-party materials? What are the parameters and pricing for distributing those materials on digital platforms? ● Insurances: Producers should consider the different types of insurances a distributor or funder might require. Some of the most common are production insurance and errors and omissions insurance. ● Talent agreements: If employing a host/talent/creator as part of your digital series, their agreement must align with the contractual terms in your distributor agreement. Typically, a talent agreement is between you and the creator/host. Therefore, you will need to consult with your legal counsel to draft and negotiate the talent agreement. Distributors will not provide talent agreement templates if they are not directly negotiating with talent. ● Deliverable expectations: Contracts can include a variety of attachments that provide additional context and detail about terms in the agreement in the form of schedules, exhibits and straightforward attachments. Deliverable expectations are often included in schedule or exhibit attachments. Schedules are where all contractual deliverables, timelines and milestone payments live. Review all the materials in your schedules to determine if the established timelines you will need to abide by align with your ideal project timeline. h Ask: Is the timeline and payment milestone realistic? Can we cover expenses through the payment schedule? Read more in Preparing for Series Deliverables. ● Determine credits expectations: Production credits refer to the acknowledgment and listing of individuals or entities involved in the creation and production of a show. They provide transparency about the collaborative nature of media creation and ensure that individuals and teams receive proper credit and recognition for their contributions. Credits may vary by distributor, but the contract usually includes specific requirements or guidelines. File this under another deliverable expectation! ● E&O insurance: Errors and omissions (E&O) insurance is a common type of liability insurance that covers claims against a business for mistakes you made or services you failed to provide. This is often a requirement in many types of distributor contracts. ● Indemnification: This is a common standard term in a contract by which one party promises to save the other from loss. ● How do you ensure you’re making a deal that works for your company? ● What are the contractual obligations you must adhere to? ● Who will own the rights to your digital project? ● Hire a lawyer, or engage someone within your legal department: First, learn how contracts flow through your legal department. This will give you an advantage when setting timeline expectations and communicating terms with the distributor. Your counsel can help you understand the types of contracts and legal paperwork needed to execute a digital series. They can also tell you if there are existing station templates or if new documents need to be created. If you need to hire outside counsel, don’t skip the due diligence. Confirm the lawyer you hire has reasonable rates that fit your budget and expertise in the type of funding and distribution contract you are negotiating. ● Review legal terms: Terms are the conditions you set with a business partner that define your working relationship, and they are legally binding. When reviewing agreement terms, determine what type of agreement the company is entering and what you want out of the deal. c Project commission: If the distributor funds 100% of your digital project, they might want full rights to the content. This means they could own the copyright and intellectual property of the series and have the exclusive right to distribute your project across all territories (e.g., not just limiting distribution to the USA or on specific platforms like YouTube). c Partial funding: If a distributor is partially funding the project, there may be more flexibility with terms and splitting rights. Think about the long game. How will this contract affect the ability to distribute the content in other ways in the future? c Distribution only: Sometimes, a producer doesn’t need funding but seeks a wide-reaching distributor. Here, you might consider licensing the content for a certain amount of time in exchange for certain rights and terms. Occasionally, when applying for a grant opportunity, certain terms are allowed for preview. Read these closely in advance to confirm you can comply! ● Redlining and working with lawyers: Sifting through agreements and negotiating terms takes time. Often, there will be a back-and-forth period of providing feedback and edits to your contract until both parties are satisfied with the terms. It’s best practice to include your comments and edits in a redlined document instead of email lists or other forms of communication. Be mindful of how long this process can take and include that buffer in your overall production timeline. STEP 2: UNDERSTANDING KEY TERMINOLOGY Here is a short list of common terms you may encounter in distributor and funder agreements. ● Work-for-hire agreement: This is an agreement for a commissioned project in which the party commissioning the project typically owns most or all rights to a project. ● License agreement: This is a type of contract in which one party (the licensor) grants another party (the licensee) the right to produce, use, sell and/or display the licensor’s protected material. ● Exclusivity: The right to have or do something is given to a single person or entity. Often, distributors negotiate exclusivity rights to ensure limited competition. Reimagining Workflow | Contracting With a Distributor or Funder

# Page 82^page=82
162 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 163 ARE THERE ADDITIONAL EXPECTATIONS FOR UNIVERSIT Y, SCHOOL DISTRICT OR STATE LICENSEES? Public media uniquely features different types of station licensees. University, school district and state licensees can have additional required parameters or processes to follow when negotiating or approving new agreements. Set realistic timeline expectations for your company and potential distribution partners when assessing how long it may take to both negotiate contracts and produce your series. More internal steps of review may be needed due to your status. Communicate that clearly to your external partners and work closely with your colleagues to keep things moving internally. Parting Thoughts Working with a funder and distributor has many advantages for the reach and scope of your digital project. A lot of the hard work of realizing your series will be reflected in the contracts you establish. Prepare in advance. Understand what tools you have at your disposal and what you might need. When contracting, slow down and take the time to comprehend the terms and rights you’re establishing to hold both your station and your funder/distributor accountable. This is a comprehensive list of deliverables required for many PBS Digital Studios series. STEP 3: LEARN STANDARDS AND PRACTICES Media organizations typically have some form of standards and practices (S&P). For PBS, standards and practices are the cornerstone of our commitment to serving the public interest and preserving trust. Standards and practices are different based on who is distributing your content and where. Check your agreement to determine if part of your obligation as a producer will be to adhere to specific standards. Below are PBS’s editorial and funding standards. ● PBS Editorial Standards and Practices ● PBS Funding Standards and Practices ● PBS Standards website Additional Questions to Consider: ● What are the standards and practices at your station? How are you thinking about how these standards apply to the media you are distributing? ● What restrictions do you need to consider at your stations (e.g., state and local laws)? ● What are the policies that guide a distributor’s standards and practices? ● How do these policies impact the production and distribution of your content? ● When do you check to ensure you’re adhering to standards and practices, and what happens if an issue is raised? STEP 4: ALIGN CONTRACTOR AGREEMENTS AS NEEDED Apart from a distributor or funder contract, consider any contractor agreements needed to establish and execute for freelancers and other work for the project. Your station will be responsible for creating and negotiating any third-party contracts for station projects. Consult with your legal team to ensure the station is protected and ready to proceed. Reimagining Workflow | Contracting With a Distributor or Funder

164 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 165 DIGITAL TEAMS CONSIST OF THESE KEY ROLES: There are a lot of similar roles between broadcast and digital work, but the frequency and timing of when the role is engaged is different. More on this in Building a Digital Editorial Timeline. Use these questions to guide your staffing process. Be intentional when assembling your team. STEP 1: WHO IS YOUR TEAM? At the beginning of your project, create a document that delineates each essential project role. Write down who will fill those roles and confirm what their responsibilities include. Use this roles and responsibilities template from PBS Digital Studios. If certain team members wear multiple hats and their roles aren’t clear, it can create confusion, frustration and further setbacks that will affect your timeline. As you’re defining roles, think about these additional questions: PROJECT STAFF FIELDWORK AND POST-PRODUCTION ● Executive producer ● Director ● Producer ● Assistant producer ● Production manager ● Researcher/scriptwriter ● Fact checker ● Graphic designer ● Marketing staff ● Episodic advisor ● Director of photography ● Field crew ● Talent/host ● Editor ● Assistant editor/asset manager ● Audio mixer ● Motion graphics/animator ● Who will call the shots and make final decisions? Who will be the deal-breaker? Who gives approval? ● What are everyone’s strengths? ● Are there people who are media veterans but need more digital training? Are they willing to expand their scope? ● Who is attuned to digital platform trends? ● Who is nimble and quick to solve problems on their feet? ● Who has strong attention to detail? ● Who is good at wrangling people? ● Who is the team diplomat? Assembling a Digital Team Staffing Appropriately to Cover All Critical Areas of Digital Work Every project needs a customized team. Consider what your series idea needs, what digital production and distribution requires and who you can afford to bring on. Be sure to read the companion storytelling chapter Digital Production Team Structure for a deeper dive into staffing teams with clear roles and responsibilities. Staffing Is the Key to a Successful Digital Project Deciding how to identify team members and assign roles for your digital project involves assessing skills, expertise and experience, as well as the project’s specific requirements. Remember, one person cannot wear 20 different hats, and they shouldn’t have to. To that end, responsibilities must be effectively delegated, and leadership must be clear, so people know who is calling the shots. Teams for a digital series can vary in size, from smaller teams with only a few people to larger teams with dozens, depending on the size of the production. The size of your team will greatly depend on your budget, scope and timeline. Reimagining Workflow

166 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 167 STEP 4: HOW CAN YOU INVOLVE OTHER DEPARTMENTS? Recruiting help from other departments can foster excitement and collaboration opportunities. It can also help fill in skills and resource gaps internally as opposed to externally. Communicate your goals early and clearly if working with other departments (e.g., marketing, design, development, etc.). Make sure they understand what will need their time and attention and when. Although departments may want to be supportive, they also may face limited time and resources. For example, does your design team have the bandwidth to take on a digital project with multiple touchpoints? Establishing relationships and conversations with other departments can help you determine where they are realistically able to step in and help. STEP 5: WHAT GROWTH OPP ORTUNITIES ARE THERE? It is important to identify which roles require strong digital experience, but it’s also important for other roles to try new things and upskill during the project. Consider giving team members opportunities to take on roles that align with their career goals and interests, even if they may not have previous experience in that specific role. Make sure they have appropriate oversight and support if doing something for the first or second time. For instance, are new skills task-based or relationship-based? Encouraging growth helps foster professional development and motivation within the team. STEP 6: HOW WILL YOU CONSIDER TEAM DYNAMICS? Having assessed the project and your team needs, take into account the dynamics of the team and how individuals work together. Consider factors such as communication style, collaboration preferences and past experiences working together to ensure a cohesive and productive team environment. Parting Thoughts Ultimately, the team you choose should be able to tell the best version of your stories. Take the time and care to pick the right people and build a team culture that fosters enthusiasm and flexibility for the work. STEP 2: WHO WILL BE INTERNAL STAFF AND WHO WILL BE FREEL ANCE? Certain project needs may require hiring people outside your station or organization because of skills and availability. What skills are missing, and does it make sense to hire freelancers or contractors? For example, there might be a writer on staff who could be a good fit for your project, but they don’t have the writing experience you need for digital scripts. ● What are the pros and cons of outsourcing? Finding suitable candidates takes time, but having the right people who bring the experience you need makes managing and sustaining your project much easier. Be mindful of how long it will take to contract outside help. If the project experiences delays, the people you hire may not be available to stay on longer than their original time. ● What do I need to know about hiring new or freelance series staff? c If you can hire new staff for your project, spend time crafting a job description that specifically outlines your expectations. Check out this digital series executive producer job description from PBS Digital Studios. c Understand what makes for a qualified selection and current market-rate offers. Include an hours estimate and timeline for the work so candidates know how much time they’ll need to commit and how frequently as part of their role. c Think ahead about how you will introduce freelancers to the project and manage them throughout. How and when will you include them in project team meetings, communications and extended company culture? What processes are in place to address any concerns they have? How will you communicate these to them? c A by-product of external collaboration is that freelancers can be unintentionally excluded and left out of the loop. Fostering a sense of belonging and ensuring your production is an inclusive, communicative space will allow for a healthy, collaborative team experience. STEP 3: HOW WILL EACH ROLE SPEND THEIR TIME? Know what percentage of time each role will be able to commit to the project, whether internal staff or contractors. How many hours does each role need to be successful? Will they be available full-time for a specific window of time? Will they be part-time or only work certain hours because of other commitments? Do they need to be moved off other projects to make room for this one? Some of your team members may not need to jump into the project until later in the production. Reimagining Workflow | Assembling a Digital Team

168 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 169 Guidebook users will be able to identify key milestones and necessary touchpoints to draft a digital project timeline. Let’s Get Started PRACTICE ACTIVITY The right questions at the beginning of a new project can help you learn how to take that first step in organizing your timeline. Reflect on your last project’s timeline. If it was a digital product, even better! ● Where did you need more or less time? ● What caused delays or were immovable deadlines? Now, take a few minutes to reflect on the following questions about a series you’re thinking about developing or plan to develop and produce. ● How much time do you have to complete the project? ● What tools do you need to successfully complete the project on time and on budget? ● Are there roadblocks you can predict, or items that will take longer that you should add buffer time for? ● What is the list of deliverables you need to complete for the project? TAKEAWAY Designing a Digital Project Timeline Making a Timeline That Works for Digital Platforms and You Digital project timelines include many of the same essential stages of a broadcast series, such as pre-production, production and post-production, but the cadence and order in which certain elements within each stage occur can vary. Digital project timelines must account for real-time audience feedback and performance, which will require touchpoints for additional planning, adjusting and pivoting to optimize performance. This section will help you anticipate what you need in your digital project timeline and provide useful steps, examples and templates to build and execute your timeline with confidence. Create a timeline for the logistics, production and distribution of a new digital series. TASK Houston Public Media (HPM) produced two series for PBS Digital Studios channels: “Dead & Buried” and “Roots of Resistance.” Given limited station capacity, we decided early on to hire external contractors for director, producer, writer and research roles because: ● We wanted to deepen our bench and connect with local creators. ● Our series required unique expertise on death and dying and, later, resistance movements. These experts are often external to our station but can bring tremendous value to the work as writers, researchers or SMEs — and therefore can help create a smoother production process. ● Outside vendors could provide more flexible scheduling that aligned with project needs, particularly following COVID-19-related staff reductions. ● Adding digital media experts to the team could help cross-train our own staff. Both series and subsequent contractor hirings were illuminating. We had an overall positive experience but learned valuable lessons along the way: ● Discuss contractor availability and working hours. Contractor schedules are different from full-time staff, so there needed to be clear and fair expectations regarding the amount of time they spent on the project and when they were online. ● Understand where freelancers fold into project workflow. Scoping contractor duties is so important! Freelancers have competing projects, so be very clear and realistic about task handoff, turnaround time, due dates and revisions. Our original timelines couldn’t accommodate multiple rounds of feedback; this oversight caused several delays because our freelancers did not have unlimited availability. ● Learn your contracting process. As a university licensee, HPM must contract through the university system. This can easily impact your production timeline. To avoid condensing your work windows too tightly, talk to your legal department to establish digital-friendly contracting workflows and timelines. Leverage senior leadership to fast-track these agreements when you can! Now we are thinking about how we can integrate positive changes into future projects. Our three key action items are: 1. Use development time to identify team roles and responsibilities. 2. Craft better workflows based on our real-world experience. 3. Add time on the front end to select and hire the right vendors for staff and support roles. Case Study Series: Dead & Buried and Roots of Resistance Station Name: Houston Public Media Author Names: Don Geraci and Tamar Price CASE STUDY Balancing Act: Series Staffing at Houston Public Media Reimagining Workflow | Assembling a Digital Team Reimagining Workflow

170 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 171 PHASE 2: PRE-PRODUCTION The pre-production phase of a pilot episode for a new digital series will involve some trial and error and experimentation, especially around format, topics, style and securing all the details to help you stay on course. ● Don’t underestimate how long contracting can take. Whether you are working to execute a distribution contract or hiring freelancers for your digital team, the process of drafting and negotiating contracts takes a lot of time. Work with your legal team to secure deals that work for both you and your partners. When signing a distributor contract, make sure requirements and terms for contractors in that agreement are carried through to freelancer contracts. Add several more weeks than you think to finalize contracts — negotiating takes time! Contracts can take two to six months for digital series. All contracts should be finalized before production begins, but some contracting can be staggered. For example, some members of the team need to start earlier than others. Consider what is needed for the entire series versus what is needed for episodes and could be topic-dependent. Digital Project Timeline Phases PHASE 1: PL ANNING AND DEVELOPING This phase can be short or long depending on your distribution plan. Keep the following in mind: ● The life cycle of a new digital series is typically a full year from development to completion. ● If you are working with a distributor like PBS, identify how much time is needed to plan and secure your timeline before pre-production starts. Development time will vary, but this phase could take anywhere between one and four months and sometimes longer. Understand what your distributor’s expectations will be. Development begins when contracting is completed. ● Revisit your pitch deck during this phase. Go back to your original concept and address any changes you or your distributor would like to make, and confirm your digital project format (explainer, docuseries, video essay, vertical-only, etc.). Allocate a few weeks to refine your pitch if need be. ● If funding for your project is coming from a distributor or third-party, understand how often funds will be released based on the deliverable milestones in your payment schedule. ● Start thinking about the visual style and branding of your series including fonts, graphic backgrounds and whether you will need to acquire a lot of third-party content. What’s Different About a Digital Timeline? Digital series milestones are ordered differently than broadcast. They often have truncated timelines and faster turnaround times because there are many episodes with shorter run times. Digital production is an active process that requires several feedback loops, regular staff commitment and having multiple production windows, which means you should avoid shooting and producing an entire digital project all at once. Digital publishing and distribution involves a two-way conversation between the creator and the audience, so you must build space in your timeline to react and iterate. In a digital timeline, you will publish your first episodes before you produce your final ones. It is a fundamentally different approach than broadcast production. Digital is a cyclical process, not linear. Look at the graphic below. Here, you begin at the top star with pre-production of Episode 2, but halfway through, you will start that same cycle for episode 3. Then, the cycle repeats itself. Instead of everything taking place in concrete windows, your team is working cyclically, 1-2 episodes at a time. Digital workflow is cyclical and overlapping. Establishing a strong timeline will not only help you understand your project needs but also help you anticipate potential variables by building in appropriate buffer time. The more planning you can do up front, the fewer disruptions you will experience. Reimagining Workflow | Designing a Digital Project Timeline

# Page 87^page=87
172 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 173 PHASES 3,4,5: BATCH AND REPEAT PRODUCTION, P OST-PRODUCTION AND PUBLISHING Once the production schedule is set, work with your team to establish a deliverables timeline for the pilot episode. The PBS Digital Studios pilot schedule template can help you keep track of deadlines. After the pilot, adopt the same framework and deliverables timeline for future episodes. Production: Build in time for feedback in each stage of the production process. Including feedback and review time periods for various stakeholders including your executive producer can be cumbersome, so build in two to three days for these types of approvals, if you need them. This phase of production can last between four and six weeks for your first episode. Budget time for the following deliverables and milestones: ● Episode concept development ● Episode concept reviews and approvals ● Researching and scripting: For digital explainers, this element can be very time-intensive and could require multiple revisions in the beginning. ● Script reviews and approvals: Build in time for writing multiple versions of your scripts for review. ● Concept and script reviews and approvals for vertical video ● Fact checking once your script is approved ● Branding and graphics package development Post-Production: Depending on how your pilot episode is received, you may be able to move through this phase quickly. However, if there are issues with your pilot, build in time to adjust and re-edit. ● Rough cut edit ● Rough cut reviews and approvals ● Final cut edit ● Final cut review ● Footage and image rights clearances ● Fine cut review ● Rough and final cut reviews and approvals for vertical video ● Finalizing branding and motion graphics ● Credits reviews and approvals ● Solidify your budget and establish financial reporting protocols. Review the required deliverables and confirm they align with your budget scope. This typically occurs in tandem with contracting. ● Write and finalize a Pre-Production Report that illustrates how you will ensure your content is relevant, respectful and accurate. ● Create a publishing schedule. Confirm how many episodes and accompanying bespoke social video pieces you need to produce and the publishing rhythm. Establish a premiere date for your last episode, and work backward to fill in your timeline. For YouTube, expect at least an eight-episode order to be published either weekly, biweekly or monthly at most. Waiting longer between episodes can negatively affect performance. If budget allows, start with 10 or more episodes to build a stronger audience base. ● Identify multiple fieldwork windows in your production schedule. Expect to produce at least the first and second episode one at a time. Advance to batching episodes in sets of two or three only after your show has found its audience. Shooting and producing all at once limits flexibility and hinders the ability to incorporate feedback into future episodes. By shooting in smaller batches, creators can adapt to audience response and adjust subsequent episodes accordingly, leading to more relevant and engaging content. ● Finalize your team, including talent. Will you have a host? How are you incorporating best practices into your budget, team and production? Build in several weeks to solidify your team and confirm the availability and workload of team members when scheduling tasks and allocating hours. Identify any expected time off, holidays or potential leave so your timeline accounts for likely delays in staff coverage. ● Identify acquired media and clearance parameters. What types of assets do you need, and where are those sourced from? What is your station’s philosophy on fair-use defenses, and does it align with your distributor? Does the stock library you’re using have the license you’ll need to transfer rights to your distributor? What music library are you using and do your rights extend to third-party digital platforms? Always make sure music has been cleared legally by either using a music library with digital rights built into your agreement or having an artist sign a music licensing agreement that specifies rights for digital platforms. Follow the licensing process at your station. Be sure to complete music cue sheets for any media materials. These may be required by your licensor(s) or distributor(s). Licensing agreements ensure your station can respond appropriately to copyright claims. If YouTube flags a copyright claim, reach out to your music licensor with the relevant information and the licensor can usually work directly with YouTube to resolve any issues. Reimagining Workflow | Designing a Digital Project Timeline

It is helpful for visually identifying bottlenecks for both the project team and the station. At the station level, it helps us define what shared station resources we want to utilize for this project specifically so we can identify conflicts early and hopefully resolve them. With our digital projects, we have found it is hard to build a schedule based on specific calendar dates because there’s almost a constant stream of micro adjustments. DIVE DEEPER A timeline is meant to be clear but nimble. After that specific production timeline was approved and the contract was signed, we went on to drop an episode and later add two episodes and six verticals. Regardless, we were tied to the 8/31 end date due to grant funding; thus, the overall footprint of the series couldn’t change. Instead, we kept sprinting towards the end date, making deliverable adjustments in real time. This made communication an essential part of the process. We were working with PBS Digital Studios as our distributor, so we had an established rhythm of weekly internal station meetings, plus regular external meetings with PBS. We also have constant Slack (PBS) and Teams (TPT) threads to keep everyone in the loop. Now in Season 5, we recommend anyone pay special attention to the Contract Signing and Legal Approval milestones; if you’re working with PBS, bear in mind the PBS Approval milestone, too. These touchpoints are crucial and have the most potential for impacting your timeline if they’re not met. Publishing: You will receive feedback immediately from your audience on digital. Add buffer time to revisit the steps for iterative storytelling in Pivoting and Piloting to determine what you would need to stay on course, when to pivot and when to tweak. The digital space requires creators to be agile, responsive and iterative in a space that is ever-changing in terms of audience preference, algorithm decisions and trends. ● All assets for your first episode should be finalized and saved in a safe place. ● After the pilot, establish a rhythm to complete milestones for each episode. Your team should know when they can start on the production of the second, third, fourth episodes, etc. Think about when they can start on research for the next episode if a final cut of the current episode is approved. Don’t underestimate the time you’ll need to support each episode’s launch. Even if you’ve delivered everything well ahead of time, when an episode premieres, that is often the start of a new flurry of activity. Once it goes live, the first 24–120 hours can require quick communication and collaboration and additional work. 4 MA JOR IMPACTS TO A TIMELINE (FOR THE BET TER) ● Keep your production manager informed of changes or possible red flags. They can work ahead and advise you on any domino effects, or brainstorm ideas with you to get the series back on track. For example, does something feel like a conflict of interest? Is the interviewee being flaky? Does something feel too good to be true? Why is that line item so expensive? Do you need something you don’t currently have? ● Plan for surprises. Think in advance about all the obstacles that can come and plan to plan for them, not around them. For smaller teams, it can mean less redundancies. For instance, how will you adjust if someone gets sick? ● Build in buffer time. Without being too extravagant, try to incorporate one or two weeks of buffer time per quarter to create cushion for project ebb and flow, holidays, time off or surprises. ● Take the time to communicate. Clear communication and collaboration among team members is key to managing multiple episodes concurrently. Producers should establish regular check-ins, share progress updates and address any issues or concerns that arise along the way. Mapping everyone’s time effectively will aid in reducing bottlenecks and burnout. Reimagining Workflow | Designing a Digital Project Timeline

180 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 181 Parting Thoughts Breaking down the total number of deliverables for a series can be overwhelming, but good planning and organization provide a strong foundation for a smooth delivery schedule that gives you room to pivot if obstacles arise. Chapter Resources • PBSDS pilot deliverables timeline • PBSDS sample deliverables list RESOURCES Case Study Series: Women of the Earth Company Name: Summer Moon Productions Author Name: Chiara Hollender What was the most challenging part(s) about managing deliverables with PBS Digital Studios during the first season of your show? The most challenging part for my team was not considering the full scope of deliverables from the very beginning. Before working with PBSDS, I had little experience with non-video deliverables (such as scripts, social media, thumbnails, etc.), and it would have been immensely helpful to have created a comprehensive list of all the deliverables for the series upfront. Another challenge was not having team members from the outset who could own and handle each deliverable individually, which led to my coproducer and I taking on many of them ourselves at the last minute. This added significant stress to our already full plates, which is an outcome I don’t recommend! How did you change or adjust your process to accommodate what you needed to contractually? The biggest adjustment to my process was creating an in-depth deliverable timeline and calendar to accompany the episode deliverable timeline. Once I could visualize what was due and when, it helped streamline the process because I wasn’t having to do unanticipated work at the last minute. When possible, I would give your production lead a heads-up of when you will be submitting cuts, scripts, etc., so they can anticipate needing time in their busy schedules to review. What specific advice would you share with new producers who are less familiar with the cadence and amount of deliverables required for a successful digital series? The best advice I can give is to stay extremely organized! Creating a shared calendar that all team members and freelancers can access is a game changer, as it ensures everyone is on the same page about the priorities on any given day. With constantly moving parts in development, production, post-production and delivery happening simultaneously, knowing what’s due when is critical to help you stay on top of the workload. 182 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 183 STEP 2: UNDERSTAND KEY ART DELIVERABLES Basic key art deliverables: These depend on the platform you’re publishing to, but most platforms require a set of standard assets to set the tone of your show: ● Poster ● Mezzanine/series thumbnail ● Full-color logo ● Full black/full white logo ● Style guide h Check out branding examples here. In-video key art elements: These elements inform the key art deliverables and round out the brand package overall: ● Typeface ● Color palette ● Lower-third templates ● Maps/timeline templates ● Various icons and graphics ● Credits template ● YouTube end card template STEP 3: CONCEPTUALIZE THE SERIES BRAND An idea for a series turns into a fully realized visual brand through a comprehensive process encompassing concept development, design and strategic branding elements. Remember, art and design help create visual solutions to a problem. When considering how to transform your idea into a visual, work through the following questions and prompts: Is the show title cleared by your legal department? If the answer is yes, then that can inform your design. If your answer is no, don’t panic! You can still create wonderful branding without a final title. Try to incorporate at least a working title that reflects the essence of your series concept. Don’t forget to receive legal clearance on a final title! Are you working with an external or internal designer/team? Branding and key art are some of the most important deliverables that bring your show together. Ideating and executing the concept and design takes a lot of time and patience. Invest in a strong designer or design team with experience in accessibility, and create enough buffer time in your timeline for multiple reviews, versioning and exports. Designing Standout Visuals for Digital Series Effective key art encapsulates the essence of the series, conveying its themes and tone, and plays a vital role in standing out in a crowded marketplace like YouTube. Sometimes creating the branding and key art for your show can feel like an impossible task, but these steps will keep you marching in the right direction! STEP 1: KNOW THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN BRANDING AND KEY ART Branding is the tactical development and promotion of a consistent visual and thematic identity of your show. This establishes a distinct and recognizable image in a viewer’s mind. Key art is the associated deliverables that make your series’ brand come to life. Key art deliverables for a digital series serve as the primary visual representation of your brand that attracts and retains an audience. A striking visual can spark curiosity and entice potential viewers to click and explore further, making it an essential tool for initial engagement. Elements like logos, color palettes and typography create a cohesive look that reflects the series’ personality. Together, strong key art and branding enhance visibility, facilitate marketing efforts and contribute to the overall success of the digital series in attracting and maintaining a dedicated viewer base. What is the difference between broadcast show branding and digital series branding? Branding is branding! Effective branding transcends broadcast and digital lines. What it comes down to is utilizing branding and visual design to help find your show’s voice and identity. Creating Series Branding and Key Art The Creative Deliverables That Bring Your Project to Life Creating the visual representation of your series is an exciting part of the process but takes time and careful methodology. This chapter delves into the key steps necessary to develop the deliverables that will help you hone a holistic creative vision for your digital series. Reimagining Workflow

# Page 93^page=93
184 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 185 STEP 6: DETERMINE L AYOUT AND COMP OSITION Balance: Arrange elements harmoniously. Hierarchy: Ensure the title is prominent, and any taglines or credits are secondary. Reflect: Make sure the poster and thumbnail follow basic design principles. Analyze the subtext of the design. Ask yourself: c What is the hierarchy of information? c What information is required that you need to design around? c Can you tell what the show is about by looking at the poster or thumbnail? c If featuring your host, are they floating over a background or do they feel part of the environment? Are they represented realistically or illustratively? c Have you written alt text for key art that will appear online? c Do your file names accurately depict the contents (e.g. RenegadesSeriesPoster.png)? How will you define your design concept? Identify the core themes and messages of your series (e.g., nature, identity, time period, community, nostalgia, etc.). Understand who your target audience is and what appeals to them visually. Ask yourself: c What is your show about? c What’s the visual vibe of the show? c What is the tone of your show? c What associations do you make when thinking about the show concept and themes? Align those associations across the key creative roles. STEP 4: GATHER VISUAL INSPIRATION Research: Look at key art from similar genres or successful series. Mood boards: Create a visual collection of colors, styles and imagery that resonate with your series. See these sample mood boards. Reflect: Consider current design trends. Will they seem dated in a few years, and does that matter? Are your initial ideas cliché? Challenge yourself to push beyond clichés. What colors and typeface support the tone and vibe of the show? STEP 5: CREATE VISUAL ELEMENTS Title and typography: Select legible, accessible and striking fonts that reflect the tone of your series. Imagery: Use high-quality images, such as original photography, illustrations or digital art. Focus on main characters or significant symbols. Color palette: Choose colors that convey the mood (e.g., dark and moody versus bright and vibrant). Make sure your colors have sufficient contrast to meet accessibility standards. Reflect: What is working and what isn’t in the design mock-up? Ask yourself: c Do your elements and color connections meet accessibility standards? c Do you have too many colors? Have two to four core colors, and add more secondary or tertiary colors as accents. c How many typefaces are you using? Best practice is to use a maximum of three typefaces, but two is better. Use one typeface for the wordmark or logo and another for in-video text with various weights. c Does this show lend itself more to be fully illustrative or to have simple and naturalistic elements like photographs? c Will your graphic feature the host? Is it important to do so? Reimagining Workflow | Creating Series Branding and Key Art STEP 7: BE CONSISTENT Brand cohesion: Key art must align with the overall branding and marketing strategy of your series. Supplementary design elements: Do you have enough supplementary design elements to be consistent, and do they all connect? Do your social media assets and episode thumbnails feel consistent with the brand? Templates make your editing processes smoother and faster. Are you overthinking it? We’ve all been there! Not every design element needs a “concept.” It’s important to take a step back and ask a colleague for their thoughts as a fresh set of eyes.

186 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 187 Production and Publishing Checklist The challenge of managing a digital project is adapting to a new workflow that requires an agile leadership mindset and team. Use this checklist to stay on top of things when the digital series is in full production and publishing mode. PRODUCTION  Roles and responsibilities: Establish these for the team early, and don’t stray too far. If roles and responsibilities need to change, communicate the changes clearly, early and often.  Identifying audience: Regularly revisit the original series goals to determine if you are reaching the intended audience. If not, how can you pivot? Or what can change to engage the audience you discovered?  Concept development: Engage the right people and teams to transform your idea into a full-fledged brand and production. Include development, marketing, design and production management staff too. Take the time to develop a strong concept and create an organizational system for tracking deadlines and deliverables.  Managing staff: Digital production team members are often working at the same time instead of in linear stages. Make sure the team has a regular touchpoint and communication channel to stay connected and informed about changes, recommendations, findings and wins. Managing Production and Publishing You’ve set up your digital project, locked in the budget, built the timeline and team and prepped the deliverables, and you are now ready to manage the day-to-day needs of production and publishing. Complete the companion chapters, Building a Digital Editorial Timeline and Pivoting and Piloting, for more on an effective iterative digital series process. Maintaining a Steady Workflow Rhythm Across a Digital Series Parting Thoughts One of the most exciting parts of creating a digital series is the visual design, but like other deliverables, it requires specific planning and oversight to ensure a successful execution. Don’t leave this part to the last minute! 188 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 189  Scheduling crew: Regularly review your timeline to know the best schedule windows to book crews for your shoots. Scheduling is a juggling act, so leave yourself enough time to understand when people are available or have conflicts. Never film before the script is approved.  Filming and media management: Whether filming in the field or studio or remotely, ensure the right people are on your crew and that someone is taking ownership of the media captured on the shoot. Know how long it takes your station staff to ingest footage and integrate this into your timeline accordingly. P OST-PRODUCTION  Post-production bookings and quality control: c Many of the same post-production standards in broadcast production apply to digital series. Consider all the time and people needed to meet these standards: edit, mix, color, revisions, design and export, quality control (QC). c When producing in a cyclical fashion, maximizing the time between episode publish dates matters a lot. Know how often and when you need to employ certain members of the team to complete different parts of the production process. Make sure that if another round of revisions or shoot is required, the right staff can return to it.  Media management/metadata: Post-production media management is critical to the longevity of a project. Assign someone to manage this process and track all the assets with an organizational tool. Revisit publishing guidelines to understand what metadata you will need for distribution (e.g., title, description, tags, etc.). PUBLISHING  Publish: Have a clear understanding of what the expectation is to manage publishing across the series and who will be responsible for which task. c Who is publishing the videos to YouTube or social media platforms? c Who will manage it once published? Who will update titles and thumbnails, if necessary? c Who will respond to audience comments and elevate the good or bad? c Who will keep watch over episode performance and analytics in real time?  Iteration: What needs to change or be improved for future episode(s)? If your series is not performing as expected on certain distribution platforms, it is important to be open to changing certain things and iterating in the moment. Changing titles and thumbnails on YouTube is a common way to iterate to see if different audiences can be engaged. Tweaking the editorial format of your project can sometimes make a drastic difference. Learn more in Creating Titles and Thumbnails. BILLING Billing typically stretches across the entire project. Here’s how to keep your finances smooth along the way:  Contractor invoices: Communicate invoice expectations and send reminders about submitting invoices on a regular basis. Getting everything at once can back up the payment process and delay when people can get paid.  Contractual payment milestones: Distributors and funders operate on payment schedules. This means they won’t issue another pay installment until a certain amount of work is done. This can be hard for smaller stations and production companies. Understanding when funds will be released can help manage payment expectations.  Projections and financial analysis: Track spending along the way to better anticipate the future and see what has and hasn’t been paid yet. Where are you spending more or faster or slower? Depending on the scale of your project and tools available, this can be done monthly or quarterly.  Expense reports and final cost reports: Track every penny that is spent on the production, including in- kind, to understand the real cost of the project. , DIVE DEEPER • Project brief template • Kickoff template • Roles and Responsibilities template • PBS Digital Studios pilot schedule template • Sample deliverables template • Sample payment schedule • PBSDS financial cost report • Example pilot and series budgets Parting Thoughts Being a project leader for a digital series involves a lot of moving parts, but the right organization systems and team support will set up any producer for success! 190 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 191 Financial reporting: Final cost reports are often required to summarize all program expenses compared to the proposal budget for the duration of the grant period. Work closely with the finance department to keep a detailed record of expenses and include a brief narrative explanation for line items that were over or under budget. Understand and be able to explain any variances in spending, if requested. You can use the PBSDS Financial Cost Report template if the funder or distributor has not provided one. STEP 3: REFLECT ON COMMON PAIN P OINTS Make time for you and your team to reflect on every area of work tackled throughout the project. Normalize talking about pain points as they come up with your team, but especially dedicate space for it during a debrief. Every conversation should be about problem-solving and creating solutions for better results in the future. For example, the START/STOP/CONTINUE debrief structure can help keep conversation constructive. Read Closing the Loop for more best practices on feedback models. STEP 4: DEBRIEF ABOUT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNED Talking about failures is never easy, but framing failures as learning opportunities is essential when you’re trying something new. Writing down and explaining the lessons you learned along the way can help you argue for better support in the future. What simply didn’t work this time and why? What could go better next time? If you have difficult feedback to share, work with your manager or a trusted colleague to determine the most productive path for that conversation and how to share it — in full or a portion of it — during the debrief. If you are leading the debrief, provide multiple avenues for team members to share feedback. Sometimes colleagues or partners will feel more comfortable sharing honest feedback through a survey or in a one-on-one meeting. Tip! Express genuine gratitude to everyone for trying something new. Emphasize each person’s positive outcomes and learnings. This will make team members feel seen and recognize their hard work and professional development across the project, garnering support for future work! Production and Publishing Checklist Closing a Project and Looking Ahead Analyzing Project Wins and Challenges and Crafting Next Steps You’ve set up your digital project, locked in the budget, built the timeline and team and prepped the deliverables, and you are now ready to manage the day-to-day needs of production and publishing. Congratulations! You’ve completed your digital series (or the first season)! When a project comes to an end, take stock of all the learnings. Dedicate time to debrief with your team and leadership on what was successful, what could be different next time and how the project can evolve. Be sure to read the companion chapters, Closing the Loop in Leadership Strategies and Conducting a Show Evaluation in Digital Storytelling. Reimagining Workflow STEP 1: MANAGE UP ALONG THE WAY AND AFTER Throughout the production, make note of accomplishments and communicate them to managers and leadership on a regular basis. The more they hear about what is going well, the more buy-in you might receive for the project. Plus, the more you track along the way, the easier your final reporting will be. Once the project is done, create an executive summary that showcases tangible results and what could have gone even better if you had the resources and more support. STEP 2: COMPLETE WRIT TEN AND FINANCIAL REP ORTING Written reporting: No project is complete until every deliverable is done — this includes final reporting! If there was a distributor or funder, revisit your contract to determine what final reporting is required. Be candid, thorough and clear. Final reports typically require project summaries, key performance indicators (KPIs) and insights on successes and challenges. Make sure a final report is reviewed and approved by the relevant senior leadership before sharing externally, because a final report reflects not only on the project team but on the station overall.

# Page 97^page=97
192 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 193 STEP 5: PL AN FOR SUSTAINABILIT Y It’s important to consider how to incorporate learnings for another season or iterate on the first version of your project, but don’t forget to reflect on potential business models for the long-term sustainability of your project. ● If season 1 had a funder, are they interested in continuing their support? What are they hoping to see evolve? ● Look into different methods of monetization for your content, including creator revenue on YouTube, digital sponsorships, licensing or crowdfunding platforms like Patreon. ● Discuss opportunities to leverage your project in front of prospective funders with your development team. Learn what they need to close a deal and give them those materials. ● Be realistic about how much future funding feels possible to secure. Reimagining Workflow | Closing a Project and Looking Ahead Parting Thoughts Wrapping up a digital project is a rewarding process that provides both closure to hard work and renewed enthusiasm for the potential continuation of a project. Don’t move on too quickly once everything is finished! Take the time to debrief with everyone who was involved and plan for an even better future. Is this something the station wants to and can support with core funding? 4. How can we keep ideating and iterating in the meantime? 200 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 201 Assessing Tone in YouTube Videos PRACTICE ACTIVITY Understanding the YouTube Landscape Starting at the 30,000-foot view, let’s take a deep dive into YouTube and learn its atmosphere, where it fits in the media landscape, key demographics and why it’s worth investing in. This foundational knowledge will set you up for success for what comes next. Digital Storytelling Watch a popular YouTube video to better understand its style, appeal and search query. 1. Think of a topic you enjoy. It could be gardening, DIY, video games, books, etc. 2. Search for a specific query on YouTube. A specific query will yield better and more realistic results than “gardening” or “books”. For example, search: c Best books this year c Zelda walkthrough c How to make raised garden beds 3. Select a popular video (something with at least 100K views), ideally published within the last three years, and watch it. TAKEAWAY ACTIVIT Y REFLECTION Question 1: What did you enjoy about this video? Was it the host, delivery, content, writing, style or something else? Question 2: What made you click on that specific video? Was there an intriguing title, thumbnail, number of views or something else? Guidebook users will be able to describe the tone of YouTube videos they enjoy watching. Understanding the Current YouTube Landscape YouTube is a massive media landscape, 9 hosting billions of monthly active users. Users on average watch over 48 minutes of YouTube videos every day, a trend that has increased every year. WHO IS WATCHING YOUTUBE? In December 2024, streaming accounted for over 43% of overall TV usage, while cable and broadcast accounted for 23.8% and 22.4%, respectively. Of the streamers, YouTube captured a record share of TV usage — 11.1%, higher than any other platform. 10 18-24 25-34 35-44 45-54 55-64 65+ 0% 10% 20% YouTube Age Demographics, 2023 15.5% 21.3% 17.5% 12.5% 9.2% 9.2% AgesPercent See the most current YouTube Age Demographics 12 ● Overall, 54.5% of YouTube users are men and 45.6% are women. ● But that doesn’t mean that every video attracts the same audiences. Some videos and channels can skew up to 90% of one gender. ● The largest age demographic on YouTube is users 25-34, but all age brackets, and a growing number of kids, are also watching YouTube. ● Viewers can watch YouTube on desktop, mobile or on a smart TV. As of March 2025, smart TV devices officially eclipsed all other devices 11 and accounted for the largest share of US viewership.

# Page 102^page=102
202 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 203 WHAT ARE THEY WATCHING? ● YouTube, like other social media sites, is completely personalized to you and your data. An algorithm will suggest videos to you based on your interests and previous watch history. What you and your friend watch on YouTube and get suggested might be completely different. c There has been widespread criticism of YouTube’s algorithm perpetuating conspiracy theories and sending users down “rabbit holes” to promote watch time and prevent people from leaving the website. ● Many people use YouTube like a search engine — after all, it is the second most popular search engine in the world, behind only Google, who owns YouTube. For example, if you want to learn how to change your tire, you might search on YouTube for an explanation video that walks you through the steps. ● Most people use YouTube for entertainment purposes, whether they are tuning into the latest video from their favorite creator, watching a movie or consuming content in order to relax and unwind. ● By and large, most YouTube channels are run by “creators” — individuals who are both creating the content and functioning as the front-of-camera personality. ● To date, YouTube best practice says videos should be a minimum of 8 minutes. Some creators publish videos that are several hours long. ● YouTube also recently released new features, including YouTube Shorts (their answer to TikTok), Courses (a LinkedIn Learning-esque destination) and Podcasts. ● As for types of videos, nearly anything will have a YouTube community. Editorial/educational videos, makeup tutorials, vlogs, reaction videos, news, video game streams, music channels, unboxings and reviews, video podcasts, the list goes on. According to YouTube, the top four content categories are comedy, music, entertainment/pop culture and how-to. Digital Storytelling | Understanding the YouTube Landscape HOW TO THINK LIKE A YOUTUBER ● YouTube is not a marketing platform — it is a content creation platform. YouTube videos should be a stand-alone story, with a beginning, middle and end. ● The best YouTube videos are evergreen — they can be watched by someone when it first publishes, and someone a year later, without losing any value. ● On YouTube, titles and thumbnails are just as — if not more — important than the actual content in your video. ● Think about what is sustainable for you and your organization. The YouTube algorithm craves consistency, whether that means you’re publishing once a week or once a month. Try to create a publishing cadence and stick to it. ● YouTube is a two-way conversation. Listen to what your audience is saying in the comments, and pay attention to the analytics to make more informed editorial decisions. More about this in Creating Titles and Thumbnails. ● Don’t paint yourself into a corner. Think about content horizontally rather than vertically. What’s a theme or an angle that can touch multiple topics, spaces or communities and that give you enough room to try things out and pivot? ● But…if you’ve found a niche that works, dive deep! The rule of thumb on YouTube is, if something is working, replicate and double down. Now It’s Your Turn! PRACTICE ACTIVITY TAKEAWAY Guidebook users will be able to identify the major differences between storytelling on YouTube vs. broadcast.

204 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 205 REFLECTION Question 1: Which video do you think was more appropriate for YouTube? Question 2: Which video did you find more engaging? More informative? More attuned to a younger audience? Why? TASK Below you will find two different videos on the same topic. The only difference is the original publishing platform. Step 1: Watch these two videos. ● You’re Not Wrong, Turbulence is Worse | WIRED and ● Why turbulence on flights is more common and expected to worsen from TODAY. ● Both videos cover the same topics but in different ways. The video from TODAY is a clip from their broadcast program, whereas the video from WIRED is a bespoke piece made for YouTube. Please note, this is not an exercise in determining which outlet did a better or worse job, but an exercise to notice different stylistic and editorial decisions on the platform and how that can impact viewership. Step 2: Write down three storytelling/stylistic differences between how TODAY and WIRED decided to cover the same topic. Digital Storytelling | Understanding the YouTube Landscape Parting Thoughts This section is only scratching the surface of everything there is to know about YouTube. The best way you can really understand the platform is by consuming it on a regular basis. Just like practicing a sport or reading about a topic that interests you, it’s hard to be in the know unless you’re regularly reading/watching/listening to others around you. Try to take time once a week to watch YouTube. Find new creators, explore topics you like, peruse the trending and home pages to find new suggestions, and keep an open mind. Learn more in the next chapter...

206 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 207 Setting Series Goals PRACTICE ACTIVITY Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck Set Your Goals and Decide What You Want to Say — and Why So you have a great idea for a series and understand the YouTube landscape — that’s amazing! This section will focus on how to make your idea become a reality with goal setting and creating a powerful, effective pitch deck. TAKEAWAYS TASK Guidebook users should be able to do the following: 1. Define commonly used goals of a digital series. 2. Summarize the importance of choosing goals wisely. 3. Recommend editorial and operations decisions based on set goals. Let’s set the goal of your series. This could be an existing series you’re developing or a hypothetical concept. MATERIALS NEEDED An idea for a series (real or hypothetical) and a place to chronicle your answers. What do you want your series to achieve? Identifying and differentiating your goals is the key to success and measuring impact. 1. Review the list of potential goals below. Check the boxes that apply to your series. 2. Use the space provided to write specifics about each goal you check off. We recommend checking a maximum of three goals. Too many goals indicates your team won’t know what the “real” priority is, and your vision will be muddied. Note that some of these goals might be in direct opposition with one another. Digital Storytelling What are the goals of your series?  To reach and resonate with our current audience. h Who is your current audience? Why will this series resonate with them? Be specific.  To reach a new audience. h Who do you want to reach and why? Why will this series resonate with them? How can your current audience connect with this content? How will you get the word out about this series, in spaces where your desired audience already is? What role will partnerships play?  To get as many views as possible. h Why are views important? How will you get them? How many views per video will you aim for?  To generate income (monetization). h What’s your financing plan? Are you exploring sponsorships? Has your channel met YouTube’s ad criteria already?  To support the PBS mission (for example, a topic or community that is underrepresented or a PBS tentpole like civics and democracy). h What part of the mission are you supporting? Why is this important right now? What might you sacrifice in order to create mission-driven content?  To support digital-first learning (for example, a new-to-YouTube creator, a rising talent or a new-to-PBS creator). h How will you set them up for success? How will you measure this goal? Question 1: Which goals did you pick and why? If you had to pick only one, which would you choose? Why? Question 2: What two goals might be inherently in direct opposition to one another? Why? REFLECTION

208 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 209 Communicating a Series Concept With your series goals in hand, it’s time to flesh out your idea. As you learned in the previous chapter, YouTube is a massive space, but it does have a defined “vibe” that drives audience expectations and viewership. The best way to communicate your idea to stakeholders and potential funders is a pitch deck. A pitch deck is a visually rich document that encapsulates your series idea and direction. It contains all the key information to pitch stakeholders, whether they are outside funders or internal leadership. WHY IS A PITCH DECK IMP ORTANT ? A pitch deck explains the who, what and why of your series and gives the reader an idea of how it will all come together. Because it’s a PDF or Google Slides, it’s easily shareable. A pitch deck is considered industry standard for film, television and digital-media projects, so it’s important to understand how to make one that is effective and captivating. Consider yourself a salesperson. This is not only your first impression but also your big presentation to sell your product! WHAT SHOULD BE IN A PITCH DECK? The main components of a pitch deck are: ● A title page. ● A logline. ● A short synopsis of your series: What is it about? Is this a YouTube series, a TikTok series, a podcast? Give all the basics. ● Your creative approach: What’s the look and feel, the style, the format? In this section, filmmakers typically utilize these tools: c Comparables (“comps”) or other links to your inspiration. That might be similar series, whether it be in tone, style or something else, that gives the reader a better idea of what you’re trying to achieve. c A moodboard of images, videos or GIFs. c A selection of example episode topics. c If applicable, different tiers or options for creative decisions that can scale up or down depending on the budget. Beware, though; this might muddy your vision. Digital Storytelling | Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck ● Creative team and potential talent, including if they are formally attached or not. ● The why behind the what: Why is this series important, timely or necessary? This might be where you infuse some of your goals into your deck. ● Audience: Who is the audience? Why are you trying to reach them, and why will this resonate with them? ● Representation: What individuals and communities are being portrayed in your content? How will these communities be involved in the process? How will you make sure your content is accurate, relevant and respectful? ● A high-level timeline: You can keep the deck evergreen by using numbers of months or weeks instead of actual dates. Learn more about production timelines by reading Building a Digital Editorial Timeline and companion chapter Designing a Digital Project Timeline. Optional elements can include the following: ● A community engagement plan: Is there a way your member station plans to utilize this content on the local level for in-person events? ● Impact: Is there an action you’re trying to get the audience to take or an impact plan you’re going to carry out? ● Platform/Funder: Include a tailored slide that speaks directly to the platform and/or funder you are pitching that showcases why it or they are the right fit. ● A director’s statement: Why are you making this series? This is a chance to showcase your personal dedication, journey and passion. ● A landscape analysis or additional research: Are there trends or research that indicate this series has a high chance of success? How is it different from what already exists? ● A basic price-package slide: You could also include “tiers” of pricing. Learn more in Building a Digital Budget. GET TING THE GREEN LIGHT The best part is that when you’ve secured funding, your pitch deck can easily turn into your series’ North Star. This can anchor your creative and strategic decision-making and ensure you achieve your goals.

hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 211210 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook Case Study Series: Sovereign Innovations Station Name: New Mexico PBS Author Name: Benjamin C. Yazza CASE STUDY We always wanted to center Native and Indigenous creatives throughout this series. Our first pitch deck for “Indigi-Genius (w.t.)” had a lot of academic language and didn’t fully encapsulate the context of the Native audience we wanted served. PBS delivered feedback encouraging us to more clearly flesh out the angle of the show. Because our team comprised largely Native and Indigenous creatives, we hadn’t realized we needed to break down historical contexts or insights from our worldviews. Although we were targeting a Native audience, this would be important to non-Native audiences who would also watch this series on YouTube. In the revised deck, we further explained topic scenarios and advocated why certain themes were being explored. We kept a lot of this same language within the final deck for what became “Sovereign Innovations,” but we also broke down our audience backgrounds, what the current Native media landscape looks like and why our series mattered in this space. We clearly defined our series logline, episode topics and how our episodes would incorporate guests in the topic. We also added a slide that described the team and their respective roles. The pitch deck was a great space to identify the context of the series and help navigate us when we got lost along the way. We went back to the pitch deck again and again to evaluate what might be missing in the episodes or how better to tell the stories in a new, fresh way. “Sovereign Innovations” Pitch Deck Creation Design a Pitch Deck Now it’s time for you to design a pitch deck for your series! Take a look through these example pitch decks from previous PBS Digital Studios series. You can also search for other pitch decks online. FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY Digital Storytelling | Building a Series Concept and Pitch Deck BRAINSTORM Congratulations on creating an amazing pitch deck! Send the final pitch deck to a friend or colleague to get feedback. Ask them to tell you what they think the series is about to check if your ideas are conveyed clearly. Pitch decks often go through several rounds of edits! If you don’t have the time, resources or skills to confidently create a pitch deck, you can always hire a professional freelancer to put your ideas into a slick presentation. With your pitch deck in hand, proceed to the Pitching Potential Funders and Distributors chapter to finish drafting your proposal. In this activity, create a pitch deck that includes:  A working title  A synopsis  The creative direction, with either a mood board or links to inspiration  Your target audience (if you haven’t already, make sure to read Crafting an Audience Strategy)  A high-level timeline (key pre-production milestones, tentative due dates and a hypothetical publishing schedule) Create a pitch deck that conveys the tone and creative style of your series visually. As you may notice, the visual design (graphics, color palette, fonts, etc.) make a huge impact on the content of your deck. If your series is for kids, it should look like a fun, bright series that kids would watch! If this is a serious series with somber topics, the deck should reflect that. Canva and SlidesGo are two great (free!) places to find plug-and-play deck templates. Later on, you will learn more about structuring a production team and choosing a host. After completing those chapters, revisit this deck and add those slides if you wish. Tip: Don’t think of pitch decks the same way you think of a formal presentation. Presentation decks utilize a minimum amount of text because you will be talking alongside them. Pitch decks are the opposite — paragraphs of text are fine as long as it’s not overwhelming, hard to read or poorly designed. Your pitch deck should be able to pitch itself with or without you in the room.
