hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 213212 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook Understanding Digital Roles and Responsibilities Digital Production Team Structure Organize Roles and Responsibilities for a Smooth Production Process Establishing roles and responsibilities for each stage of video production ensures a smooth process in a fast-paced environment! In this section, you will learn how to structure your team and understand the functions of each role from pre- to post-production. Read companion chapter Assembling a Digital Team for a deeper dive into setting up teams for success, hiring considerations and sustainable staffing. Guidebook users should be able to: 1. Define the roles and responsibilities of a digital team, including how a team can be structured and how many people you will need to staff. Are they local or national, staff workers or freelancers? 2. Understand what roles are necessary for a team to create a successful digital series. 3. Assign and delegate responsibilities to begin pre-production on your digital series. Read and analyze the Roles and Responsibilities template. Estimated read time: 15 minutes PRACTICE ACTIVITY TAKEAWAYS TASK REFLECTION Question 1: What were your first impressions of the suggested team structure? Question 2: Which roles are already on staff at the station? Question 3: Which roles would need to be hired? Question 4: Are there any role(s) that are unfamiliar? Digital Storytelling Building an Experienced Digital Production Team Every team member plays a critical role in bringing a digital video to fruition. Teams are composed of professionals with individual strengths who contribute to the team effort. Everyone will be constantly collaborating. Each role brings creative and technical skill to the production. Identifying roles and how they will work — together and separately — builds the foundation for seamless workflow and video execution. TEAM LEADERS The three crucial staff roles to start off your series are your executive producer, creative director and writer (or writer/researcher). ● The executive producer will lead your team and be the final decision-maker in all areas — editorial direction, finances, legal and team operations — and will act as the primary liaison with any outside distributors or creative partners. ● The creative director will lead the vision and development of all episodes, from ideation through the writing, editing and publishing stages of production. ● The writer will be responsible for writing all long-form and vertical scripts and will collaborate with directors and producers to incorporate any editorial feedback. Together, they will shape the tone, structure and direction of both your team and the videos. They will also manage others on your team and develop your content from pre-production through post-production. These roles build the proverbial ship before your production can set sail. All roles are important, and each role will contribute creatively and technically. These three roles, however, will set the course of your episodes and your series — without them, you will be lost at sea. Tip: Familiarize yourself with basic roles and responsibilities on a digital production team.

# Page 108^page=108
hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 215214 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook TEAM SIZE AND SCALEABILIT Y Generally, teams can vary in size, anywhere from six to 15 people. Sometimes, one person will fill multiple roles. For example, an editor might also be an animator and a graphic designer. A host might also be a writer, or a writer might also be a researcher. Some staff may multitask on multiple projects, or some may work full-time on one project at a time. Variations in schedule are fine as long as you agree at the outset what responsibilities, deliverables and communication are expected. SUB JECT-MAT TER EXPERTS AND EPISODE ADVISORS You will likely need to consult a subject-matter expert(s) or advisor(s) to support your series in scripting, research and fact-checking. This person provides key knowledge and/or lived experience that might fill blind spots on your team. They can weigh in on story ideas and scripts and guide you to other experts or community members in the space. This person will most likely not be full-time staff, but you should think about integrating them into your pre-production as early as possible. Scheduling and communicating with advisors can take time, and digital production moves fast! In addition to hiring your subject-matter expert, writer and researcher, make sure there is a staff fact-checker who can support your project. Read PBS Standards & Practices: Compliance and Fact-Checking for more detail on why this is important. TO HIRE OR NOT TO HIRE? THAT IS THE QUESTION. Consider which roles you can staff internally and which roles you will need to hire as freelancers or contractors. Are those freelancers local, or can they work with your team remotely? There are always pros and cons, but in the digital world, remote production is common. Considering certain roles for remote freelancers — like writers, researchers, editors, animators and even hosts — can expand your network to find the very best candidate for your project. More on finding the right host for your team in Choosing a Host. Outlining roles at the start of your process means your team can communicate well and hit the ground running in pre-production. Making sure team members understand what they’re responsible for and know who to rely on for what will ensure the ship keeps moving. If your staff availability changes, then you will know what responsibilities you need to fulfill. Digital Storytelling | Digital Production Team Structure Case Study Series: In the Margins Station Name: WHYY (Philadelphia) Author Name: Tom Grahsler CASE STUDY WHYY Digital Studios staffs five full-time video employees: one animator and four producers who are also competent in writing, shooting and editing. WHYY Digital Studios producers hold different roles (editor, camera operator, etc.) throughout various projects. To work with PBS Digital Studios, we had fewer people than the roles outlined in the PBSDS roles and responsibilities template, so filling it out up front helped define the roles, working timeline and process. Our biggest takeaways were: ● Build your team around specialists: Lean on specialists, like writers, to focus on specific, time-intensive tasks, which frees up the staff producers to focus on time-intensive archival research and video editing. c Key role: archival researcher. Having the archival researcher also be the editor was extremely helpful, since they were setting themselves up for their own edit. c Key role: writer. A dedicated writer as a subject-matter specialist is invaluable since the production schedule is short, and research and voice for the series is paramount. ● The benefits and challenges of swapping roles: Roles for producers would switch for each video between writer and archival producer, which was challenging on a condensed timeline. However, archival and video-editor roles can safely rotate between producers, because those timelines align to optimize the production calendar. ● The director: The director role was also particularly difficult to define, because the host was remote and filming herself. Typically, a director would be working with the talent, asking for other takes or approaches on delivery. ● Staffing: One producer left the company at the beginning of Episode 1, and a second left on medical leave soon after publishing Episode 2. We had to backfill those roles and adjust responsibilities accordingly. We leaned on freelance help with scripting and research and reduced the number of other active WHYY DS projects outside of “In the Margins.” ● Roles and visuals: Some episode topics didn’t have a lot of available archival footage. So we needed to pivot to more animations created by a freelance illustrator and animated by our in-house animator. Staffing “In the Margins” at WHYY

FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY TASK Building Your Own Team Now you know what digital team roles and responsibilities can consist of and have learned about one station example. Let’s decide which roles you will need on your team to produce your digital series! Digital Storytelling | Digital Production Team Structure Question 1: What roles are essential to pre-production? Production? Post-production? Question 2: Which roles could be filled by staff members at your station? What roles would need to be filled by freelance hires? Could freelance workers be remote or local? Question 3: Are there any roles that might overlap on your team? Are there any roles you think might be missing from the template? Next, sit down with your team and review each role line by line. Ensure that everyone understands their position and their peers’ roles. Overcommunication is key! Now is the time to ask questions and voice any suggestions to be added to internal or external staff role expectations or statements of work. This document should be revisited as often as necessary, but certainly as a team, as you pivot and reflect from video to video or series to series. DIVE DEEPER

218 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 219 Fostering Belonging Across Production This section will review strategic tactics to thoughtfully and intentionally foster a sense of belonging on your production teams and with project participants to effectively tell compelling and accurate digital stories. CONTENT ● Belonging starts in pre-production! Consider how your project reflects a variety of stories, voices or perspectives. If it only reflects one point of view, that might be an indication to widen your lens. ● Consider how your team sources stories and what steps you’re taking to ensure that your stories are authentic and accurate. ● Consider also how people from the communities you’re reporting on are included in the development and pre-production process. ● Analyze how you’re thinking about the audience. Are you making assumptions about what might interest them based on stereotypes? ● Ensure your script has been reviewed by not only a fact-checker but also a relevant episodic advisor and/or subject-matter expert for accuracy. BEHIND THE CAMERA ● Consider the psychological safety of your set and writers’ room. Do people have the power to speak up? Do people feel safe speaking up? ● Does the crew, especially those in key editorial and creative roles, have the expertise and perspective to effectively tell the story your program highlights? ● Look at your hiring sources for freelancers. Are you relying on the same people all the time? How can you widen your talent pool? ● Is pay equity reflected across your production? IN FRONT OF THE CAMERA ● Authenticity must be built in from stage one in story selection and pre-production, with continuous reflection and improvement across production and post-production. ● Analyze your use of stock footage and illustrations to ensure there is an accurate reflection of the American public and representations are free of stereotypes. This counts for music cues, too! ● Consider the psychological safety of your talent and team post-publishing. How will you handle negative or hateful comments, especially those aimed at your talent? Read more in Comment Moderation. ACCESSIBILIT Y One in four Americans has a disability, making accessible content essential for fulfilling our mission to serve all audiences with content that educates, entertains and inspires. ● To be considered fully accessible in American English, videos must have readable on-screen text with proper contrast, low background noise, sensory-safe lighting, high quality captions, audio descriptions, descriptive transcripts and on-screen American Sign Language (ASL) as well as the ability to turn added accessibility features on and off. ● Currently, the PBS web player has the most accessibility options (provided producers include the required captions, audio tracks and ASL versions when uploading to Media Manager of the platforms where PBS video is available. ● Due to the limitations of many commercial media players, many producers create multiple versions (e.g., a video with and without audio description) and links to alternate versions in online descriptions and social copy. Take five minutes to journal about when you have felt welcomed in a production setting. ● What did people do to make you feel that way? ● How does your team or newsroom make the environment feel safe and foster a sense of belonging? ● Is there is a leader you admire who does this well? ● What is something you do to create this sense of welcome? If completing this guidebook with a team, have a candid conversation or solicit anonymous responses. Use a circle format or conversation agreements to create a safe space for all team members to share their thoughts and feelings. REFLECTION Co-Creating Content for Your Communities Let’s look at how your production can advance the PBS mission by partnering with and reaching local communities through the power of media. Digital Storytelling

220 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 221 Case Study Series: Rogue History Station Name: PBS North Carolina Author Name: Greg Jeske Challenge: When PBS North Carolina’s “Rogue History” started hiring for a producer–writer in their first season, they didn’t find the talent they were looking for. The preference was for a multi-hyphenate role, like a producer–editor. These can be helpful roles to include in your team because having people with several production skill sets can improve workflow and be more cost-effective. Solution: A consultant recommended the station hire two separate roles: a producer and a writer. This ended up expanding their talent pool and allowed them to hire a team that aligned with the station’s strategy. Although hiring two people for a role that had been intended for one put a strain on their first season’s budget, they were able to compensate by bringing on a mix of early career and more experienced team members. By setting the expectation that the more experienced staff would coach newer staff, the producers stayed within budget, developed new talent and created a high-quality digital series. In their second season, several team members were then experienced enough to take on multi-hyphenate roles. Hiring and Supporting New Talent on “Rogue History” CASE STUDY Rogue History production team on location Pre-Production Case Study Series: Ritual Station Name: Louisiana Public Broadcasting Author Names: Avery White and Emma Reid Challenge: The producers of Louisiana Public Broadcasting’s “Ritual” wanted to create a welcoming environment for their talent on set. A common struggle for Black talent is receiving subpar and unqualified hair and makeup services. Because of historical segregation and inequitable standards of beauty, many cosmetologists in the United States are not trained to provide the same quality of service to people with darker skin tones and/or textured hair. When producers are unaware of this issue, it can result in negative on-set experiences for talent from communities of color. The team hired Tank Ball (of the award-winning music group Tank and the Bangas) to host their digital series “Ritual.” It was a priority that Tank, a Black woman, should feel respected on set. Solution: Prior to filming, the series producers asked Tank what she needed to feel comfortable on set, and she requested a hair and makeup team that specializes in working with people with darker skin tones and who could create the elaborate natural hairstyles Tank is known for. By honoring Tank’s needs, LPB contributed to a welcoming filming experience for their talent. To make your set more welcoming, make sure you consider the following in advance:  How can I make sure my talent feels comfortable on set?  Have I asked them what they need?  Have I considered their hair, makeup, dietary restrictions and accessibility needs and factored this into my budget and communications? Creating a Welcoming Environment on Set CASE STUDY Production Tank Ball on location filming “Ritual.” Digital Storytelling | Fostering Belonging Across Production

222 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 223 Case Study Series: Sovereign Innovations Station Name: New Mexico PBS Author Name: Benjamin C. Yazza Challenge: Initially, the “Sovereign Innovations” team wanted to build community with their Native and Indigenous audience by addressing comments — whether they were negative or positive. Sadly, there were a slew of negative hate comments aimed directly at their host about her complexion and tribal affiliations on early episodes. This was discouraging and hurtful and affected the team’s ability to move forward in series development. However, they also noticed some audience members actively combating the hateful comments and providing support! Solution: To address the team’s own distress, they reached out to PBSDS to work together to figure out how to handle negative comments in the future. One outcome was that PBSDS would a) default to more restrictive comment settings on their shows and b) clarify their comment policy with a social media post across their channels, which was positively received. These changes meant fewer harmful comments could be posted and engaged viewers could participate in online conversations without feeding the trolls. Although negative comments do happen and sometimes require adapting policy for the well-being of all, it’s important to remember there’s a whole other viewership that eagerly awaits your content who wants to discuss the importance of the video’s topic. Dealing With Online Trolls CASE STUDY DIVE DEEPER Post-Production Read Comment Moderation and Comment Engagement Tactics for a deeper dive on how to manage comment policies and trolls and empower loyal fans. Tip: The PBS Digital Studios Comment Moderation Policy provides comprehensive guidance on how to create an active and respectful comment section. Learn more in the next chapter... Digital Storytelling | Fostering Belonging Across Production

hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 225224 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook Analyzing Video Styles Developing a Strong Video Concept Learn How to Craft an Excellent Episode At this point, you have created a strong series concept and pitch deck and are ready to make a video! But what is your video going to be about…and how will the story unfold? In this section, we will define some of the most common styles of video that are successful on YouTube and discuss the tools you can use to identify and develop an episode topic that will stand out from the pack. 1. Gain exposure to different video styles. 2. Develop the ability to identify and analyze those styles and their pros and cons. Watch the first five minutes of the two videos. Consider how their presentation style impacts the storytelling. 1. “Drowning in Entertainment: The Age of Distraction” 2. “How Smartphones Sabotage Your Brain’s Ability to Focus | WSJ” PRACTICE ACTIVITY TAKEAWAYS TASK REFLECTION Question 1: What differences do you notice between the videos’ presentation styles? Consider how the videos are filmed, speaking tone, graphics, editing, etc. Question 2: How would you describe the hosts’ interaction with the audience? Write down two to three descriptors for each video. Question 3: Who was the “main character” of each video? Did both videos have equally strong main characters? Question 4: Which video focused more on factual information? Question 5: Did you find one of the videos more engaging than the other? If so, which one and why? Digital Storytelling Understanding “Edutainment” on YouTube YouTube is a massive platform with videos that vary infinitely in look, sound and topic. But a few styles of video have consistently resonated the most with audiences. As a result, common characteristics appear across genres and channels, particularly on the educational side of YouTube. STEP 1: CHOOSING A ST YLE The style of video you choose will influence the pacing, scripting, cinematography and editing of your video. Just as importantly, being clear and consistent with your style makes the audience comfortable and gives them hints of what they’re getting into. For example, if your video opens with long, slow nature shots that set a mood or lightly edited on-the-street action, the audience will rightfully suspect that they’ve started watching a documentary and will set their expectations accordingly. There are three main types of factual videos on YouTube: 1. Explainer: Like the name suggests, this is a video where a concept, question or fact is explained to the viewer. These predominantly feature a host, either on screen or through voiceover (VO), talking directly to the viewer, and they generally involve fast-paced editing. h “How A Kitchen Mishap Changed Science Forever” h “Why Do These Words Get Mispronounced So Much? | Otherwords” 2. Video essay: Video essays usually argue a perspective, point of view or opinion and mirror the structure of a traditional written essay: a thesis statement, supporting evidence and a conclusion. They often share similarities in hosting style with explainers. h “How Fictional Pandemics Reflect The Real Thing (Feat. Lindsay Ellis and Dr. Z) | It’s Lit” h “Do You Pronounce it GIF or GIF?” 3. Documentary: Documentaries have very few limitations on style, length or tone. But in general, they document a certain subject by featuring the people, places and things that comprise that subject. Any to-viewer narration is generally used to stitch together story beats rather than deliver the bulk of the information. These frequently feature interviews. Remember, even though a documentary is more filmic, the rules of YouTube still apply. There still needs to be a strong hook, and the length and pacing must be appropriate for the platform. h “What Happens When a Queer Muslim Meets a Lesbian Reverend?” h “How This Aquaponics Farmer is Reinventing Urban Agriculture” Guidebook users will be able to:

# Page 114^page=114
226 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 227 Digital Storytelling | Developing a Strong Video Concept As you developed your series idea, you likely already had an intuitive sense of what style of storytelling would fit your video best. Your video does not need to adhere exactly to any one of these categories and its characteristics. In practice, all these styles borrow elements from each other when beneficial. However, before you start breaking the rules, it’s best to master the basics. STEP 2: EPISODE IDEA DEVELOPMENT When concepting your series, you probably came up with a few potential episode topics. But how do you choose one to work on, and how do you develop it into a ready-to-produce episode idea? Beyond the basics of feasibility (access, budget, timeline, etc.), consider this: ● Is it interesting? First, ask yourself whether the idea is interesting. Be honest. That sounds simple, but it is the core of a successful YouTube video. What emotion do you expect your video to evoke in your audience? Is the topic surprising, intriguing, inspiring, moving, infuriating? Could you imagine yourself or your viewer telling their friends about it or sharing it? What is the unique offer that will make viewers have to stop and watch? ● Simplicity: Your episode idea should be simple enough to explain in one sentence. This does not mean that the video needs to be simple or you need to avoid nuance. But you do need to be able to communicate the key concept of the video quickly. Try writing one brief sentence that explains what your topic is about. Is it still interesting? Was it too hard to include all the necessary information in one sentence? If it was, Tip! Bear in mind that strong framing can make almost any topic interesting! A video about the evolution of anesthesia applications in medicine might sound a bit dry, but a video on how anesthesia started out as a Victorian-era party drug is much more intriguing. consider how you can narrow your topical focus or adjust the framing to have one clear angle. ● Will your audience be into this? By now, you have identified the target audience for your series. Will the topic be interesting to them? If not, what new audience do you expect to reach that is worth potentially not serving your primary viewer base? Have a very good justification for making a video you expect your main audience to be lukewarm about. c Evaluate the landscape: Open a private browser window and search your potential topic on YouTube. This helps you get search results that are not influenced by your personal browsing history or preferences. Has anyone already made a video on the topic you want to cover? Do videos on this topic get a lot of views? It’s not disqualifying if there are already videos on your chosen topic. In fact, a popular topic is a good sign that your video will be interesting to a certain audience. c Think about how you are going to differentiate your videos from the others: Are you approaching the topic from a new angle? Did everyone else get it wrong? Does your video offer superior production value? Be open to a topic simply being saturated with little left to be added. c Are there no videos on your topic? Try to assess why. Is it a story no one else has access to? Just a particularly unique topic on a niche interest? Videos on a brand-new topic can be a big advantage for standing out from the pack. But make sure it’s a subject that people are actually interested in. ● What would the title and thumbnail be? One of the most vital aspects of creating a successful YouTube video is crafting a strong title and thumbnail. Start that planning now. Write down a few ideas for a title and thumbnail. Similar to your one-sentence episode description, if you struggle to create an eye-catching title, this may not be the best topic for a YouTube video. Alternatively, consider your framing. ● Visual strength: Why is this topic well suited to visual storytelling, rather than a podcast or a blog? How does creating a video on this topic enhance the information you’re trying to share? Is it visually interesting? Do you have enough visuals to tell the story, such as access to archives or a budget for illustrations? WHERE CAN EPISODE IDEAS COME FROM? ● Your creative team. This includes freelance filmmakers, writers, producers. The most obvious answer is the team who is working on the series. But accepting pitches for episodes from your freelance team is a great way to not only widen your idea pool but also find episodes that the team is really passionate about. ● Your audience. Mine your audience for episode ideas by creating a poll on your community tab or sifting through your audience’s own suggestions for episodes they’d like to watch and learn about. ● Your newsroom. Is there a journalist or producer who’s working on a fascinating story that can be reimagined for YouTube? A social media manager seeing a trend online that fits the theme of your series? A staff member who’s really into a niche hobby that could align with your audience? Ask around and make your pitch process open to all. ● The news/social media. It’s possible to be inspired by ideas that are in the news or trending on social media, but ensuring they remain evergreen and that you can turn them around in time to catch the traffic wave is the trick to YouTube success. ● Holidays, heritage months or other high-traffic moments. There will always be traffic and special promotion around highly searched holidays, so consider situating an episode around one of those moments in a way that is organic and nuanced. Tip! Read Creating Titles and Thumbnails for more.

228 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 229 Digital Storytelling | Developing a Strong Video Concept CASE STUDY “Ritual” is a series dedicated to exploring the long-held sacred practices and traditions of the South. Because the show was meant to feature vulnerable communities, the producers began by establishing a core mission and moral compass, even before choosing individual episode topics. In the case of “Ritual,” this mission was to tell stories that 1) would be enjoyed by the communities featured in the piece as much as by a wider audience and 2) offer a deeper understanding of lesser-known topics through the lens of their subjects. The episode “Uncovering the Power of Hoodoo: An Ancestral Journey” achieved this balance. By looking at existing content on YouTube, the team knew a rigorous deep dive on the topic would be highly popular and benefit general audiences’ understanding of the subject. However, they identified significant challenges to producing the episode: ● Very little archival imagery to craft a visually engaging episode. ● A lack of strong guests to interview. ● As an often misunderstood practice, highlighting hoodoo, especially in a compressed run time, without playing into harmful stereotypes would take significant additional research and review. Ultimately, the team determined these challenges were surmountable. The producers: ● Staged and shot their own evocative imagery to overcome the lack of archival. ● Conducted additional rounds of factual and cultural review. ● Executed a robust character search that resulted in compelling and authentic interview subjects. But working through these issues added significantly to the overall production time of the episode, which would not be sustainable over a full season. Ultimately, the episode was a huge success, becoming the most popular episode of “Ritual” with over 400,000 views as of September 2024, and was praised for its accuracy and nuance. Although the producers were able to find a balance between their core mission and YouTube popularity, topics with weak archival imagery and characters proved a significant challenge, and they would encourage others to plan for additional production resources or time to devote to such topics. Brainstorming Episode Topics for “Ritual” Case Study Series: Ritual Station Name: Louisiana Public Broadcasting Author Names: Avery White, Emma Reid and John Campbell FINAL PRACTICE ACTIVITY Crafting an Episode Concept Let’s put all these concepts into practice. Make sure to answer the following questions in your explanation: c Will the episode have an on-screen host? c Will it be fully scripted? c Will there be interview guests? ● Write a one-sentence description of your episode that is no more than 90 characters. ● Describe the emotions your audience should feel while watching your video. ● Look up your episode topic on YouTube. c How many videos already exist on the topic? Copy the links to the top three videos by views, and add them to your document. c Watch the most popular video on your topic (if there is one). Describe what will make your video different from theirs and why someone would want to watch yours instead. ● Write down the target audience for your episode. Compare it to the target audience you identified for your series in Crafting an Audience Strategy. ● Write three title ideas for the episode. c Compare your draft to this finished “Sound Box” episode concept example. Is there anything you want to revise or anything that is missing? Parting Thoughts Nicely done! With a strong episode topic, you can progress to developing an episode outline, which will be further detailed in YouTube Story Structure.

Ready to choose a host for your series? Let’s dive in. KEY CHARACTERISTICS OF A GREAT HOST  Engaging personality  Articulate and emotive  Knowledgeable (or a strong researcher) in the subject matter: This can look like having an advanced degree in the subject, like Erica Brozovski, host of “Otherwords,” who has a Ph.D. in English sociolinguistics, or Matt O’Dowd, host of “Space Time,” who has a Ph.D. in astronomy and astrophysics. Or it can mean having extensive lived experience, like Taietsarón:sere ‘Tai’ Leclaire, host of “A People’s History of Native America,” who is a Native American writer, actor and comedian; or Curly Velasquez, host of “Dead & Buried,” who had recently chronicled the death of his grandmother.  Has a significant digital presence/following to leverage or is willing to be social-savvy  Can connect with your member station audience (so get to know your member station audience well!)  Has experience in remote or on-set filming (whatever best suits your needs)  Adds a unique perspective or experience to your existing roster of on-screen talent Nice-to-Haves c Comfortable and skilled at reading scripts on teleprompters c A strong vision for storytelling (if choosing a host that will also contribute to the writing process) c Interested in building relationships and engagement with audiences, like reading and responding to comments, producing community tab posts on YouTube or promoting videos on social media c Reflective and interested in ways to improve performance

232 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 233 IMP ORTANT TO KEEP IN MIND… ● How does your host fit into your production plan? Consider whether your host will be expected to share their personal lived experiences on camera. How will you make your set and writing room a supportive environment? How will you support them if negative comments targeting them personally are posted? ● What is your talent budget? Because this person will be representing you online, it’s best practice to vet your top candidates and ensure their online presence matches your company requirements. Choosing a host who aligns with the PBS brand is essential for a successful project. ● If your host is a contractor or part-time employee, remember that they may be working on multiple projects. Digital Storytelling | Choosing a Host CASE STUDY In 2024, PBS Digital Studios (PBSDS) sought an on-camera talent to host a new history anthology series, “In the Margins.” The new series needed a host who: ● Would collaborate in producing videos with multiple stations in different time zones ● Had experience with remote production and self-taping ● Had history expertise and/or could connect with history fans ● Had a keen eye for storytelling PBSDS conducted an extensive search for potential talent that met these criteria by: ● Reaching out to the PBSDS and PBS networks and academic and media communities for recommendations ● Researching history-related creators on social media platforms who fit the criteria ● Reaching out to the preferred candidates or their management via email Ultimately, PBSDS hired Harini Bhat, Ph.D., as host based on meeting those criteria and exhibiting the following strengths: ● A successful interview, in which Harini communicated her excitement and understanding of role responsibilities ● Harini’s engaging and personable delivery in her self-tape, where Harini read a section of a prior episode script ● Harini’s experience researching and producing short history-related videos for her own channel, “Today I Learned Science” ● Eagerness to communicate with multiple stakeholders and openness to work with a tight production schedule ● Strong experience producing both longform and short-form videos remotely, building her own simple “sets” and familiarity with camera, lighting and video-format techniques Hiring a Host for “In the Margins” Case Study Series: In the Margins Station Name: PBS Digital Studios Author Name: Hannah Rothblatt Thumbnail of Harini Bhat hosting “In the Margins.”

hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 237236 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook YouTube Story Structure How to Write and Produce an Effective Digital Script In this jam-packed section, we’re talking everything story: outline, script, production and post-production best practices and how to effectively utilize interviews. This is where the magic happens — but a good structure, like a tried-and-true recipe, ensures that everything works together in harmony. Hooking the Audience PRACTICE ACTIVITY ACTIVIT Y Guidebook users will be able to identify and write a strong YouTube hook and opening. REFLECTION TAKEAWAY 1. Which video do you think had a clearer hook and why? 2. Rewriting existing hooks is a great practice exercise. If you rewrote the FRONTLINE video hook, what would you mention in the first ten seconds to capture viewers’ attention? The “hook” — or the first ten seconds of a YouTube video — is crucial. Let’s learn how to spot the difference between a great hook and a weaker one. Watch the first ten seconds of both videos and answer the following questions. 1. “What If We Detonated All Nuclear Bombs At Once?” by Kurzgesagt — In a Nutshell 2. “Life on New York’s Upper East Side During the Great Recession (full documentary)” by “FRONTLINE” Digital Storytelling Adapting From Broadcast to Digital Although many broadcast production skills can translate to digital, creating a digital video for YouTube differs in a few specific ways revolving around story. Shifting mindset from broadcast methods to digital methods around story structure will strengthen your video’s performance. YOUTUBE STORY STRUCTURE Take a look at the two story arcs below. On the left is a traditional story arc, with a beginning, middle and end and a high climax in the middle. This story arc is seen in films and broadcast programs. On the right is the emerging story arc, 13 which is more commonly utilized in digital-first storytelling. Traditional Story Arc Emerging Story Arc Lead in OfferBuild Climax/Big reveal Branding Subtle brand cues Start high Unexpected shift Multiple peaks More story for those who want it The major difference in these styles can be seen in the introduction. For a linear program like a broadcast show, many viewers turn on a program halfway through or miss the first five minutes. Less emphasis is placed on grabbing viewers from the very first minute. That said, some modern shows have begun to adopt a more front-heavy approach as well thanks to the continued growth of streaming. ABCD: A playbook for building effective creative on YouTube (2018). 238 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 239 On YouTube, your video is fighting during the first five seconds to keep viewers from clicking away to a new video. The best way to achieve this is to “frontload the awesome.” Pick the most exciting, intriguing or confusing element of your video and lead with that. This could be a cool experiment, an animated map with cryptic narration or a bombastic montage of upcoming moments. A great hint of what your video’s most interesting part is can be found by looking at your title and thumbnail. These videos show three different approaches to crafting a strong introduction: “How Cults Use Language to Control” “Redwoods Shouldn’t Be So Tall. Here’s Why They Are” “What Is Blaccent And Why Do People Keep Using It?” Digital Storytelling | YouTube Story Structure Title and Thumbnail Set expectations for the video. The purpose of your introduction: To meet and exceed the expectations set by your title and thumbnail. h You can’t do that if you don’t have a title and thumbnail in the first place. First 5 Seconds of the Introduction Meet the expectations from the title and thumbnail. 1. The first sentence matches the title 2. The first shot matches the thumbnail h It doesn’t need to be word for word or pixel for pixel, but it should be enough to assure viewers your title and thumbnail were not clickbait. The Rest of the Introduction Exceed the expectations from the title and thumbnail. 1. Create a Curiosity Gap h Open a loop to incentivize viewers to stick around and get answers. 2. Provide Context h What do viewers need to know for the video to make sense? 3. Leverage Input Bias h Show the effort (time, energy, money) that went into the video. Final Construction Take it from “Meh” to “Wow.” 1. Show, Don’t Tell h Make your intro engaging even without sound. 2. Frontload Stimulus h Increase the pace vs. the rest of the video. Final Check: Watch it 10 times. If you look away or get bored, others will too. Stunning videography selling the topic’s importance. Featuring famous figures paired with a twist. Uses a quick montage to build interest and appeal to a viewer’s personal experience. How to make a killer YouTube intro (to blow up your channel 14), Aprilynne Alter (2024). YouTube.

# Page 121^page=121
240 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 241 STEP 1: FROM IDEA TO OUTLINE Previously, you completed Developing a Strong Video Concept. Now it’s time to turn that idea into a story outline. An outline is a bare-bones skeleton of your story with the main points arranged in bullets. Creating an outline allows you to break your story into building blocks and more easily arrange them. This nails your story structure before committing time to writing the actual script. Keep the outline simple so you can make changes quickly. How to create an effective outline: ● To start, think of the outline like a traditional written essay, with a beginning, middle and end. What’s the point and how do you get there clearly? ● Then, rearrange these sections to match a more digital-friendly story structure. This is how you will keep your audience along for the ride and deliver on the promise of your story. ● Label the segments of your outline so you know you’re hitting all the important elements: Intro, hook, unexpected shift, second peak, etc. ● Review successful outlines from real PBS Digital Studios videos if it feels difficult to visualize an outline. STEP 2: FROM OUTLINE TO SCRIPT Once you have an outline that provides the bones of your story, it’s time to add some meat. Scripts are exactly what they sound like — everything that will be said in your video. This is a moment to add all the connective tissue, do fact checking for particular phrasing and start thinking about the specific visuals to carry your story. Tips to Get Started: ● A digital script may share a lot of similarities with scripts for traditional media but differs most significantly in its tone. YouTube in general is a conversational space. Even when discussing complex topics, your host should speak like they’re explaining something to a friend. For example, check out “How Moldy Bread Can Change Your Brain” by “Fascinating Fails.” ● Always do a table-read with your host. This reduces clunky or overly complex phrases and allows the host to put the script into their own voice. ● Strong visuals are crucial for success on YouTube. Best practice is to plan visuals alongside the script using an audio/visual template. ● Scripts take many different forms and formats. For some inspiration, check out these sample PBS Digital Studios scripts. Include footnotes or comments sourcing any factual claims, adhering to PBS editorial standards and practices. Digital Storytelling | YouTube Story Structure Dive deeper on how to develop a strong outline and script with this recorded “Scripting for Digital Storytelling” workshop led by Andrew Matthews, who directs and writes for “Otherwords” and “Two Cents.” STEP 3: PREPARING AND UTILIZING INTERVIEWS Interviews with subject-matter experts, firsthand witnesses and primary sources can be an integral part of a digital video’s narrative. However, interviews are also one of the most challenging elements to incorporate into digital content successfully. Here’s how to make it work: ● Understand common challenges. c You have less control over the wording and pacing of interviews when compared to scripted sections that you’ve finely tuned for YouTube’s style. There’s a risk they will slow the pace drastically and people will click away. c Your interviewees are unlikely to be as camera-ready as your hand-picked host. ● Do include interviews when … c The interviewee can speak to something that the host cannot, like personal experiences, different cultural insights, unique experiments and opinions. c The interviewee is a well-known public figure who will draw attention to your video. c The interviewee is a uniquely engaging speaker or otherwise enhances the entertainment, as well as the substance, of your video. DIVE DEEPER Tip! Writing for digital is a very specific skill! Hiring writers with digital experience whenever possible is the best bet. If it’s a producer’s first time directing digital, trying to learn digital scripting at the same time will likely be overly difficult.

# Page 122^page=122
242 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 243 ● Always pre-interview your guest! c Always research your potential guest. Ask yourself: Is this someone who should be associated with the PBS brand? Is this a voice that should be amplified? Do they post problematic content or content that does not align with PBS that could damage our audience’s trust? c Meet with your guest prior to your shoot date virtually or in person to discuss the topic you’d like them to cover. Ask them the questions you will ask during the filmed interview. c This allows you to begin incorporating the general ideas your interview will cover into your script to make the integration more seamless and avoid planning for something your guest isn’t going to say. c It also gives the interviewee a chance to consider their answers, improving the quality of their take on camera. ● Make the most of interviews. c Think of the interview as another set of building blocks to help construct your final script. c Avoid long chunks (10 or more seconds) of unedited or lightly edited interview segments. Work to maintain the same level of pacing and visual interest as your scripted sections, be it graphics, animations, B-roll, etc. c Unless this is a specific goal of your series, avoid the feeling of a sit-down Q&A or a video podcast. h Example: “Why We All Need Subtitles Now” from “Vox.” Notice how the expert guest rarely talks for more than 30 seconds without the host interjecting with additional color or picking up the narrative himself. Even when the guest is talking, visually, the episode continues its trend of frequent cuts, interesting graphical representations and clips from films. c Remember to adhere to the PBS editorial guidelines for interviewees too! STEP 4: PRODUCTION BEST PRACTICES FOR DIGITAL Working in digital spaces can allow for nimbler productions that happen faster with a smaller crew and simpler setup. How to capitalize on the simplicity of this format: ● On set: As discussed in previous chapters, it is possible to film multiple episodes in one day, also known as “batching.” However, the set or location must still be well lit, in focus and on brand, and the audio must be crisp and clear. Unfortunately, it’s true that investing too much more in digital production rarely pays off with more views. Be thoughtful about how you spend your production dollars. c Gear: A recent PBS Digital Studios survey found that most producers are currently using the Sony FX6, a much less expensive camera than its broadcast or film cousins. c Host setup: Most hosted YouTube videos are just the host or creator sitting in front of an entry-level digital camera in their home office. To the right are a sample of simple host-to- camera setups, all of which were filmed inside the host’s home or at a rented home. These are often filmed in 4K to allow you to “pop” in and out, resembling a two-camera setup with only one camera. c B-roll and visualizations: Although most YouTube videos will spend a lot of time in the to-camera shot to build a relationship between the audience and the viewer, digital is also an opportunity to get creative with B-roll. Remember, this is a fast-paced environment, and viewers will expect lots of visuals to support your storytelling. Think beyond news-style B-roll and stock, and explore hands-on experiments or visual metaphors, illustrations and animation. Remember, claiming “fair use” on copyrighted visuals or music “just because it’s the Internet” is not a thing. The same rules apply as on broadcast. Make sure you have legitimate fair use claims for your project and are prepared to pay for assets. Brush up on PBS Standards and Practices. Digital Storytelling | YouTube Story Structure

# Page 123^page=123
244 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 245 Digital Storytelling | YouTube Story Structure STEP 5: P OST-PRODUCTION BEST PRACTICES FOR DIGITAL Like scripted works of fiction, post-production for digital series should follow the straightforward script you wrote. “Finding the story” in the edit should never be the default and is highly discouraged. Prepare to spend your time in post-production optimizing for YouTube. What to focus on in post-production: ● Keep the story visually interesting. During the edit, focus on optimal length, pacing and visuals. Follow YouTube’s recommended settings for encoding. ● Don’t be afraid to cut! Keep the intro snappy, short and interesting. Eliminate moments where the audience might get bored and click away. If a section you loved in the script is dragging on in the edit, delete it. Credits should be only 15-30 seconds maximum; longer will hurt average view-duration stats. ● Music is a great way to set the tone and pace. Choose music that won’t distract the viewer from your host. Be thoughtful about where music comes in and out — don’t have wall-to-wall background music! Plus, all music should be licensed appropriately. ● Quality control: Ask another team member or your fact checker to do a final quality-control check to catch any last-minute mistakes, like typos or dropped frames. ● Ensure that your set is a space where everyone feels welcomed, included and heard. Put in the extra work to ensure that your host is comfortable on set by conducting table reads, investing in training, mentoring and listening to their needs. If your host is uncomfortable, the camera (and the audience) will be able to tell. ● Be thoughtful, and don’t reinforce stereotypes. B-roll, illustrations and music are great ways to be intentional on-screen. 248 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 249 Creating a Pre-Production Report Pre-production reports help producers explain how their projects support the principles that PBS has outlined and illustrate goals for audience reach and community engagement. Setting goals for your production helps ensure you stay focused on the target audience and relevant content theme(s). Think about a time you connected with a character or subject while watching video content. This could be a movie, TV show, social video, and so on. ACTIVIT Y Question 1: Who did you choose? What were the points of connection you found with the character or subject? h If you couldn’t think of any one character, reflect on what it might be like to see someone on screen that you feel a personal connection to. Question 2: How did having a personal connection with someone on screen make you feel? Were you more or less engaged? Question 3: How does representing your community on-screen connect to your station’s mission? REFLECTION The Power of Representation CRAFTING A PRE-PRODUCTION REP ORT A Pre-Production Report is developed before production begins and outlines how the production goals will meet the PBS mission and values and goals for audience reach as well as community engagement. Creating a plan before production begins helps producers and PBS ensure their series are serving a broad audience and the network is creating a welcoming environment for all team members. Fostering belonging from the beginning makes sure all are included and encourages thoughtful processes to assess any challenges or opportunities that may arise. Review the Pre-Production Report Guidelines to get started! When creating your own production plan, bear in mind these PBS editorial best practices: ● The PBS approach to on- and off-screen representation is expansive and inclusive. No one can be excluded from consideration for involvement in your series. ● In all PBS series, producers should seek out as many perspectives as possible to ensure fair and accurate portrayals of the subject matter. ● Producers should collect both qualitative and quantitative data about audiences, which may include demographic data from YouTube analytics, lived experiences and feedback surveys for audiences or other forms of assessment. ● Every episode or series has a theme or focus. Sometimes this focus is a specific community. To ensure accuracy, stations should seek out subject-matter experts on this community. Often, although not always, these will be people from the community itself. Learn more by watching the “Meet the Advisor-Powered Production Workflow” webinar by PBS North Carolina and New Mexico PBS. ● When the main focus of an episode or series is a specific community, producers should include people who are positioned to tell authentic stories about that community. ● Do targeted outreach to specific audiences who might be interested in the relevant content. Tip: The most important aspect of digital storytelling is that it is a constant journey, not a destination. Continuously seek new and interesting perspectives, voices and ideas. Digital Storytelling

252 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 253 PBS Standards and Practices: Compliance and Fact-Checking Building fact-checking and standards and practices (S&P) compliance into your pre-production avoids massive headaches and heartbreak in post-production. PBS’s standards and practices are at the core of everything PBS does and how we maintain the American public’s trust — and these policies extend to all digital work. Upholding the public’s trust means always understanding what is editorially permissible or not. Write down your answers to the following prompts: 1. What common S&P concerns do you encounter in your work? 2. What is the current process for flagging and correcting S&P concerns? 3. How could it be improved or standardized? 4. Do you know who to go to when an S&P issue arises? Digital Storytelling Editorial S&P is the Cornerstone of Preserving Public Trust Unfortunately, there is a pervasive myth that digital video or social media is a more relaxed environment and is not held to the same standards as broadcast or print journalism. That is simply not true. All PBS producers, regardless of medium, are responsible for following the PBS editorial standards and practices. These editorial standards live alongside the funding standards and the co-production policy, all of which are posted on the PBS Standards website. This section will focus on the editorial standards specifically, with a spotlight on fact-checking. PBS EDITORIAL STANDARDS AND PRACTICES: A BRIEF OVERVIEW The PBS editorial standards are the cornerstone of our commitment to serving the public interest and preserving trust. They consist of six core principles: 1. Independence: Content distributed by PBS must be free of undue influence from third-party funders, political interests and other outside forces. 2. Accuracy: Accuracy includes more than verifying whether information is correct; facts must be placed in sufficient context so the public is not misled. 3. Fairness: Producers must consider all relevant facts and perspectives and present information in a responsible manner, without favoritism or discrimination. 4. Transparency: Content should be produced in a way that allows the audience to evaluate the credibility of the work and determine whether it is trustworthy. 5. Inclusiveness: PBS strives to contribute to informed debate by presenting, over time, content that addresses a broad range of ideas and perspectives. 6. Accountability: Producers must respond to relevant inquiries about their work. They also must adhere to the highest professional standards of conduct. REFLECTION PRACTICE ACTIVITY

254 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 255 ACCURACY AND THE IMP ORTANCE OF FACT-CHECKING Per the PBS standards, “Producers must implement rigorous fact-checking procedures to verify the accuracy of all factual assertions. As part of these procedures, producers should be able to identify the source for each asserted fact and why that source is reliable. As a general rule, at least two authoritative, independent sources should be on the record before controversial assertions are included in content for distribution by PBS. The accuracy of assertions by expert and non-expert interviewees should never be assumed, particularly if such assertions are contentious or otherwise questionable. Producers should also pay careful attention to the accuracy of names and titles, dates, places, statistics, and foreign language translations.” Working with an independent fact-checker is best practice, in addition to having a producer fact-check their work. This double-layer system reduces errors before moving into production (which can cost time and money) and creates accountability on both sides. If working with producers who are new to the editorial or journalism space, it’s important to educate them on S&P best practice. 3 TIPS TO MAKE S&P EASY 1. Be informed: It is imperative that all staff — including freelancers — are well versed in the PBS Editorial Standards. PBS employees should also be knowledgeable regarding our funding standards and coproduction policies. Make sure your team completes the self-paced PBS S&P course. Invite an open dialogue after completing the course to discuss any questions or feedback. 2. Speak up: In an ideal world, everyone on your team, regardless of position, should be on the lookout for potential S&P conflicts and feel they can voice concerns. Foster a safe working environment in which people are encouraged to ask questions early to prevent problems before they happen. 3. Have a process: If an S&P issue occurs, what happens next at your station? Make sure teams understand who to approach with concerns and what steps should be taken to correct the issue. Build these processes into your development, scripting and post-production stages. S&P concerns can manifest in different ways in different situations. For example, an interview can look great on paper but have issues (like a prominent logo or URL) in post-production. Digital Storytelling | PBS Standards and Practices POP QUIZ! Spot the S&P Problem PRACTICE ACTIVITY Question 1: True or False? Because you are producing a series that will publish only digitally on a social media platform, the PBS editorial standards are more lenient or don’t apply to your work. Question 2: You are working on a piece that includes an on-camera interview with a community member. They are nervous about how they sounded and request a rough cut before publishing to approve their sound bites and how they are used. Is this:  A. Totally fine! Send the rough cut.  B. Okay, just pay attention to only the notes you agree with.  C. It is never acceptable to send an unpublished piece to an interviewee, except in limited circumstances pertaining to sensitive information or national security. Question 3: You are working on a piece about a very niche historical subject. There are not many experts in the entire world on this subject, but you have managed to find one! You have paid them to consult on your script to ensure it is accurate and nuanced. Your producer gets a great idea to interview them on camera because it would be such an exclusive piece. Should you…  A. Do an on-camera interview, but not credit them as a script consultant.  B. Do an on-camera interview, and credit them as a script consultant in the end credits.  C. Do an on-camera interview, and credit them as a paid script consultant in the lower third.  D. Not interview them. TASK TAKEAWAY Guidebook users will be able to identify basic S&P compliance issues and know what to do. Below are several examples of scripts or visuals that may or may not have an editorial S&P issue. Review the examples and select the correct answer. Tip: Answers are on page 257. No cheating!

256 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 257 Question 4: You just got a rough cut from your team that includes an interview with an expert throughout the whole piece. You notice a book placed prominently in the background of the interview (screenshot right), and after searching it, you realize it’s written by the interviewee. What do you do?  A. Blur the cover of the book.  B. Reshoot the interview.  C. Nothing; it’s useful information for the audience.  D. Either A or B. Question 5: An episode of a show includes an incredible statistic — so incredible that commenters are asking for the source material so they can learn more. Upon asking the producer for the source, they send you a study. But you discover that the statistic in the video is not in the study’s raw data. It seems to have originated through an interview with the organization who funded the study, which multiple blogs and small news outlets re-quoted. You reach out to the organization and the interviewee but do not receive a response. What do you do?  A. Nothing — if we admit that we made a mistake, it could lead viewers to distrust us.  B. Wait until you hear back from the interviewee and/or organization to see if they have the data, even if it takes days or weeks.  C. Immediately take the video down so the misinformation does not spread.  D. Issue a correction as soon as possible where the video is posted that explains the error clearly and concisely and provides correct information. Parting Thoughts Now you have all the right tools to feel confident, informed and empowered to stay within PBS standards and practices compliance in a digital environment. Remember to always consult your own organization’s standards and practices for additional guidance. When in doubt, ask! Interview with geophysicist Michael Mann, Ph.D. “Weathered.” Digital Storytelling | PBS Standards and Practices ANSWER KEY Question 1: The answer is FALSE. The PBS editorial standards apply to all our work, regardless of publishing medium. Question 2: The answer is C. Generally, work-in-progress materials (including rough cuts, fine cuts and segments) should not be pre-screened for funders, interviewees or outside parties. The editorial process must remain free from any attempt to influence or interfere with the professional editorial judgments of producers and PBS. The executive producer, in consultation with PBS, may pre-screen content in limited circumstances — for example, to ensure the accuracy of sensitive information or to comply with national security concerns. The pre-screening of content for media critics or for other publicity purposes is a common and acceptable practice subject to appropriate embargoes and provided that no undue influence occurs. Question 3: The answer is D. Paying someone as part of the video team and interviewing them on camera creates a conflict of interest, and a reasonable audience member may think that you paid this person for their interview, which is against PBS policy. Question 4: The correct answer is D. Either blurring the book cover or reshooting the interview are acceptable ways forward in this circumstance. Prominent book, product, logo or URL placement can be perceived as an endorsement or product placement by a viewer. It is best to avoid these situations. This is a great example of why your crew should be knowledgeable of S&P issues. Question 5: The correct answer is D. Errors are inevitable, and it’s important to catch them before they happen. But when they do happen, it’s important to take accountability and be transparent. C, although tempting, is not correct because it does not address the correction and can leave viewers with more questions, leading to distrust. The PBS editorial standards state that producers are responsible for correcting errors in a way “that is abundantly clear to the audience.” As stated in the PBS standards memo concerning corrections, for videos with errors: “At a minimum, a correction should appear on the webpages where the video is posted (e.g., PBS.org, YouTube) and as close to the applicable video as possible (ideally, in a prominent caption underneath the video where the audience can view the program).” Tip: Turn this page upside-down for the answers!

258 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 259 Building a Digital Editorial Timeline Preparing the When, Where and How of Getting It Done Digital production timelines are very different from documentary film or broadcast timelines. How does everything get done on a rolling basis in such a short amount of time? This section will teach you what to look out for in an editorial timeline, using real-life examples from digital-first productions. For the full picture, be sure to read the companion chapter Designing a Digital Project Timeline. Analyzing a Sample Timeline Guidebook users will be able to: ● Comprehend the cyclical nature of digital timelines. ● Compare and contrast the differences between digital and broadcast timelines. ● Identify the “biggest lift” areas of digital productions based on time and effort spent. PRACTICE ACTIVITY Digital Storytelling Consistency Is Key You likely noticed that digital-first timelines are much faster than broadcast. They are also rolling, meaning that episodes are constantly in various stages of production to accommodate a constant, consistent publishing schedule. Digital-first productions rarely complete the entire series production and publish afterward. Typically, series produce one to two episodes at a time, all the time, and publish every two to four weeks, depending on capacity. If producing bespoke vertical content alongside YouTube episodes, they should follow the same production timeline for maximum efficiency. Let’s break down the common pitfalls in production timelines and learn how to build an efficient and sustainable production schedule. PRE-PRODUCTION The biggest task in pre-production is episode research and development (R&D). This can take anywhere from days to years until a concept is ready to pursue due to timeliness/publishing strategy, scientific research, cost to produce or several other factors. That’s why it’s important to constantly be developing and researching episode ideas and have a “stable” of potential ideas that are ready to produce. If your goal is to publish once a month, it will pay off to have either 1) a fully dedicated researcher and writer on your team or 2) multiple researchers and writers on your team to ensure that while one episode is being filmed or edited, another is being researched. Scripting, background research and scheduling interviews (on camera or pre-interviews) take the most time but are worth the investment to ensure that filming and editing are a breeze. Most episodes that rush this step fall apart later on or have to re-edit or re-film segments that had factual errors or didn’t fit the flow of the story. PRODUCTION Production is where digital and broadcast differ the most. Because digital-first series are typically short (8-12 minutes each) and more streamlined, several episodes can often be filmed in one day and even in one location. Doing a table-read before production can make things even smoother. DO NOT FILM ALL EPISODES AT ONCE. This is extremely important in digital-first work! Filming all at once does not let you be present or responsive to the target audience. Instead, best practice is to develop two to three ideas and select the pilot from that group of ready-to-go episodes. Then write and film the pilot, release it and analyze the response before marching full steam ahead on the rest of the series. That often means an aggressive timeline between the pilot and Episode 2. But it also means you have an accurate gauge of your audience’s response and know what to tweak. TASK TAKEAWAYS REFLECTION Pull up one of your recent broadcast-production timelines. Review the production timeline from “Overview: Season 1,” a show that reveals the natural phenomena and human forces shaping our planet. Compare the two and answer the reflection questions. Question 1: What major differences are there between this digital production and your broadcast timeline? Question 2: “Overview” published a video every four weeks, a standard digital publishing rhythm. How does their schedule support a four-week turnaround?

# Page 131^page=131
260 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 261 P OST-PRODUCTION Because digital-first productions at PBS are scripted, editing time is mostly dedicated to graphics and illustrations. Typically, production teams budget time for an internal rough cut, an external rough cut, a fine cut and an unlisted review (a.k.a. a final cut that is uploaded to YouTube with completed metadata). Post-production for one episode rarely exceeds three to four weeks, and multiple episodes are in different editing stages at once. Read more about what to focus on in YouTube Story Structure. AVOIDING COMMON PITFALLS ● Do not rush pre-production, especially scripting! Scripting is the foundation of the success of your series, and most of your time should be spent researching and scripting. ● Engage a fact-checker, and budget enough time for them to work. Ensuring your script is fully fact-checked before filming saves heartache and money down the road. Learn more in PBS Standards and Practices: Compliance and Fact-Checking. ● Do not spend too much time (or money) on production. Digital-first productions are filmed on simpler, less expensive cameras and commonly use inexpensive backgrounds and settings. Time spent designing a complex set, doing a fancy lighting setup or capturing intricate B-roll might not pay off to your average digital viewer and would be better allocated elsewhere. ● Do not wait to start the next episode until after the previous one publishes. ● Do not film everything at once. Neither of these last options are best practice. A consistent publishing cadence that meets the audience’s expectations for a digital platform requires multiple episodes to be in production or pre-production at once. However, filming everything at once does not allow you to pivot or be iterative. The goal is to design a timeline that keeps things moving while not getting ahead of yourself. Digital Storytelling | Building a Digital Editorial Timeline CASE STUDY “Be Smart” has grown and evolved a lot since 2012 as its producers have sought to improve the show, adapt to changes in digital media and grow the brand while being faithful to the highest of educational standards. How the show is made has changed a lot over the years too. The “Be Smart” production timeline reflects the producers’ current logistical approach to making the show. The basic timeline structure is fairly straightforward when considering it in the context of a single episode. Once an idea is green-lit, two weeks are allowed to write it, one day to shoot it and two weeks to cut it. What may be less evident is how individual episode timelines overlap. “Be Smart” producers always endeavor to have at least one episode in pre-production while the previous one is in post-production. But having multiple episodes in pre-production requires maintaining a sharp focus on development. Ongoing and robust story development is vital to enabling “Be Smart” to maintain its brisk, three-week publishing cadence. And it is likely where producers and writers/researchers spend most of their time and energy, working to maintain a steady flow of incoming story ideas and coordinating with all parties to ensure that pitches are reviewed and green-lit (or passed on) in a timely fashion. Producing a digital show is always a bit of an adventure, as it should be. Unexpected twists and turns are where a lot of the fun lies. But they can often cost days out of a production timeline. It is important to build a timeline with generous allotments for the production stages so that producers can seize surprise opportunities or navigate bumps in the road without compromising their publishing calendar. And it can’t be overstated how important ongoing story development is. Fresh story ideas are the fuel for a production’s engine. Keep the tank full! Maintaining a Brisk Publishing Cadence Case Study Series: Be Smart Company Name: Spotzen Author Name: Thomas Fernandes
