264 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 265 Creating Titles and Thumbnails Over three million videos are uploaded to YouTube every day. So how do you get people to watch yours? This section will teach you how to excite and convince viewers to click on your videos by creating effective titles and thumbnails. Reach Your Audience with the Right Title and Thumbnail Analyzing Titles and Thumbnails PRACTICE ACTIVITY Question 1: Which title and thumbnail combination would you be most likely to click on? Question 2: What makes you want to click on that title and thumbnail? Question 3: Why are you less interested in clicking on the other titles and thumbnails? REFLECTION ACTIVIT Y ● Develop a sense of what makes a strong title and thumbnail. ● Think critically about how episode packaging impacts desire to click and view. TAKEAWAYS Review these three potential titles and thumbnails for a video and consider which is most effective. Digital Storytelling How To Make a Great Title and Thumbnail Combo You’ve spent weeks or even months producing an excellent video for your YouTube channel. Now your job is done! Here come millions of views, right? Not so fast. YouTube is a highly competitive space where every video is vying for the attention of potential viewers. Unfortunately, it doesn’t matter how good your video is if nobody clicks to watch it! But you have two main tools to convince them to pick your work: Your title, a line of text about 50 to 60 characters long, and your thumbnail, a small image that appears above your title. CREATING AN EFFECTIVE TITLE Your goal when crafting a title and thumbnail for your video is to make viewers curious enough to click. This begins with your title. You have about 60 characters to accurately describe what your video is about. But accuracy isn’t enough. You need to highlight the most interesting element of your episode. Consider the basic practice examples from earlier. What’s more interesting, a video about “earthquakes” or “surviving a megaquake”? Examples of various YouTube titles and thumbnails styles. Guidebook users will be able to:

# Page 134^page=134
hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 267266 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook They say a picture is worth a thousand words, but on YouTube, it’s more like a million. Your thumbnail will be the first thing to draw a potential viewer’s eye, even before the title. So your thumbnail needs to be big, bold and simple to understand at a glance. You only have a second to grab someone’s attention. Keep it simple. Amazing archival footage, beautiful imagery, a dramatic graph or an emotive face are all simple and effective thumbnail options that can be processed in the blink of an eye. Packing too many visual elements into your thumbnail makes it hard for the most important information to stand out. Digital Storytelling | Creating Titles and Thumbnails Tip: During your first shoot, take high-quality still photographs of your host, capturing a range of emotions, to potentially use for thumbnails later. Be concise! The fewer words someone needs to read to get hooked, the less likely they are to move on to another title. Try to keep your title under 60 characters. Shorter is better. Don’t be afraid to go BIG. Make your title as sensational and intriguing as you can: superlatives; grabby adjectives like “shocking,” “surprising” and “unbelievable”; strategic use of ALL CAPS. It’s all fair game, but … CREATING AN EFFECTIVE TITLE CONTINUED Don’t misrepresent your video. If viewers click expecting one thing but get another, they’ll stop watching. This is what’s commonly known as “clickbait” and will hurt your video’s performance in the long-term. Include major keywords in your title. This helps YouTube serve your video to the right audience. This is particularly important if your video is on a highly searchable topic, like a how-to guide or a common school curriculum subject. Tools like vidIQ or TubeBuddy can evaluate your search engine optimization (SEO) strength. Questions are a powerful tool to generate curiosity. But be careful with questions with yes-or- no answers. If someone feels like they can intuit the answer without watching, they won’t be motivated to click. Consider how to rephrase as a “how” or “why” question instead. Think about potential titles in pre-production. If you can’t create a concise, clear and catchy title when planning your episode, consider whether this is an appropriate topic for a YouTube video or how you could reframe the topic. Never leave title planning for post-production! CREATING AN EFFECTIVE THUMBNAIL Think small. Assume your image is being viewed on a phone. Always downsize your thumbnail to ensure your key elements can still be easily understood at a smaller size. Use text sparingly. Text on thumbnails can be an excellent tool to complement your title. But if you use too much, viewers will move on to something more visually interesting. Consider brightness and contrast. Look at the thumbnails at the top of this section. Are your eyes drawn to the brightest colors? So are everyone else’s. Use eye-catching colors to get noticed, but make sure the most important elements of your thumbnail stand out with high contrast. Don’t be afraid to really pump the saturation.

# Page 135^page=135
268 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 269 CHANGING YOUR TITLE AND THUMBNAIL Titles and thumbnails do not end once your video goes live. It is possible to change either or both elements at any time, even after release. Monitor your video’s performance post-launch, and make changes if you are unhappy with early results. A great title and thumbnail make all the difference in reaching viewers. Read more on assessing your video’s performance in Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics. Wait a few hours before making changes so YouTube has time to find the right audience for your content. Watch your CTR! Impressions click-through rate (CTR), or what percentage of people who saw your video clicked on it, is a key metric for judging how effective your title and thumbnail are. Draft multiple titles and thumbnails before release so you can make changes swiftly without extra work. Make your changes meaningful. Try a totally new base image. If your first thumbnail was a stock photo, try a more graphical approach. Pick the second-most interesting thing about your video and focus on that. Big changes will give you more information about what is working and what isn’t. Let analytics tools do the heavy lifting. YouTube’s A/B testing feature allows you to test multiple thumbnails at the same time. Use this as often as possible. Many creators also use third-party services like vidIQ and 1OF10 to aggregate data from millions of videos to help identify trends. Check out these YouTube Title and Thumbnail Tips. DIVE DEEPER Digital Storytelling | Creating Titles and Thumbnails CASE STUDY “Sound Field,” a PBS music-education show produced by Twin Cities PBS, created an episode highlighting a Mexican regional genre known as Sierreño, now titled “Why This Mexican Genre Is Growing As Fast As K-Pop.” At publication, the team went with a thumbnail featuring various Sierreño artists. Early performance for the episode was slow, with a click-through-rate (CTR) around 3 percent, which was low for the channel. The team decided to make a change. After brainstorming, they landed on a stat mentioned in the episode: Mexican regional music was the second-fastest-growing genre behind K-Pop. They swapped the title and thumbnail to “Why This Mexican Genre Is Growing as Fast as K-Pop.” Sierreño Thumbnails in “Sound Field” Case Study Series: Sound Field Station Name: TPT — Twin Cities PBS Author Names: John Campbell and Marissa Blahnik CTR immediately increased to nearly 7 percent, a very high number for the channel. The change worked because the old thumbnail featured artists and a genre that most people would not recognize. It was also visually cluttered. The new thumbnail emphasized the connection to K-Pop, a well known genre, and featured simple graphics with a clear, intriguing message. The team learned it’s better to design a thumbnail with one clear visual point of interest that builds on the title in an interesting way. When working with unfamiliar topics, it is smart to consider how to give audiences a point of reference, like with K-Pop in this example. Don’t be duplicative. Your thumbnail works together with your title to create curiosity. It should rarely repeat the same information as your title. Think of your thumbnail as being in conversation with your title. It’s not a great conversation if they both say the same thing, right? Look for trends. Research what viewers of your type of content like. Are you a weather channel? Open an incognito browser window for objective search results. Search for what other successful videos on your topic have for thumbnails and create your own spin on them. For example, PBS Digital Studios has identified that the “PBS Terra” YouTube channel audience responds well to thumbnails with maps on them, so we lean into that design when appropriate. If you’ve created a strong title and thumbnail, you’ll have successfully created a curiosity gap: the difference between the information your potential viewer has right now and the remaining information the episode promises.

# Page 136^page=136
270 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 271 CASE STUDY “Monstrum,” a PBS series about monster folklore produced by Spotzen, released an episode on the popular children’s series “Pokémon.” The original title for the video was “Gotta Catch ‘Em All,” the tagline of the “Pokémon” series. Early performance was disappointing after release, with the video ranked sixth out of the 10 most recent videos. Given the popularity of the topic, there was potential to do better. The team brainstormed a new title, “The Untold Secrets of Pokémon’s Monster Mythology,” but did not change the thumbnail, feeling they had landed on a strong option to begin with. “Pokémon” Titles in “Monstrum” Case Study Series: Monstrum Company Name: Spotzen Author Name: John Campbell The episode saw immediate improvements in viewership, eventually ranking first out of the 10 most recent videos. The change worked because the original title failed to communicate the promise of the video. A person looking at the title and thumbnail would know only that it was a video generally about Pokémon. The new title changed this by adding an element of the unknown. Promising “untold secrets” of a familiar topic created a strong curiosity gap and encouraged viewers to click to learn more. The team learned to search for a stronger title hook and consider what knowledge potential viewers already have on the topic when writing a title. Do they want to click on your idea? Why or why not? How could it be improved? ACTIVIT Y Topic 2: Humanities This is an episode about the philosophy of happiness. It is part theory, part history and a drop of science. It starts by discussing perspectives on happiness from different parts of the world and across different time periods. It also explores the impact of these attitudes over time and how they continue to evolve and be questioned today. The story ends in the present day, when researchers show how psychology and neuroscience merge with social sciences to explore what makes us happy and how we can be happier. Topic 3: History This video explores the Harlem Renaissance’s influence on the New Deal. The Harlem Renaissance saw an outpouring in music, literature and art in the early 20th century. But it wasn’t just an artistic movement. Intellectuals like W.E.B. Du Bois and Alain Locke put forward writing and philosophies that influenced several of the New Deal’s civil rights policies, including Executive Order 8802, the “Black Cabinet’’ and the PWA. This episode explores the story of this powerful yet underappreciated connection. Parting Thoughts Now you know how to make your own strong title and thumbnail. Remember, even the most popular and well-established YouTube channels aggressively tinker with their titles and thumbnails. YouTube is all about experimentation, so keep an open mind and don’t be afraid to tinker and take big swings! Topic 1: Science New studies reveal that far more animals than previously thought likely have complex consciousness, including fish, lobsters and octopus. Meanwhile, zebrafish show signs of curiosity, and crayfish respond to stress by using anti-anxiety medication. But what does all this mean? This video explores the research into animal neuroscience and ponders what these revelations mean for our relationships with animals.

# Page 137^page=137
272 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 273 Pivoting and Piloting Reflecting on the Pilot Episode to Make Necessary Storytelling Changes YouTube is a two-way conversation. After publishing an episode, you will immediately know what your audience thinks of it. In this section, we will explore how to process qualitative and quantitative audience feedback to make your next episode even better. Why Iteration Is Mandatory in Digital Digital requires creators to be agile, responsive and iterative in a space that is ever-changing. Audience preference, algorithm decisions and trends can change quickly, which can be daunting. It can also be exhilarating and freeing! You can change up your approach, your style, your topic — almost anything! — in the name of experimentation. However, you want to make changes with intention and purpose. MAKE INTENTIONAL, DATA-INFORMED CHANGES WITH 3 KEY ITEMS: 1. Performance analytics 2. Overall comment sentiment 3. Series goals WHEN TO STAY THE COURSE It’s full speed ahead for a pilot or series that has a thoughtful, positive and engaged comment section, is reaching a new audience and is performing well or above average in terms of views. Remember, a new show will take some time, so don’t stress about views too much. The episode might pick up a “second wave” of views later on, as is common on YouTube. Digital Storytelling WHEN TO TWEAK Most often, shows will tweak certain elements of the series for optimal performance and audience reception. These can be deduced through analytics (most replayed, retention) as well as through a qualitative comment analysis. Are people in love with your host? Maybe try showing them more often or infusing more of their personality into the episodes. Do they find your jokes insanely funny? Write more. Try your best to lean into what people are liking. Do they suggest what kinds of episodes they’d love to see you do? If an episode suggestion is appropriate and within scope, make the episode and tell them it was based on their suggestion! Conversely, lean away from what’s not working — for instance, if viewers are dropping off during interviews. Read more in Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics. WHEN TO PIVOT Sometimes it is difficult to discern if the series is just having a slow start or if it’s really hurting. Doing new things in real time can be uncertain. Here are some common reasons to consider a full pivot after a pilot or mid-season: If the content is not resonating with your audience Remember, this doesn’t mean the show is “bad,” but it isn’t finding your people. This typically looks like … ● Your viewers just don’t seem into it, the content is performing poorer episode over episode and it fails to break out of the channel’s subscriber base into the broader audience of YouTube. ● There are no strong negative comments, but the audience just doesn’t find it interesting. Maybe it’ll perform better on another channel or platform. This usually takes a very long time (more than five episodes) to surmise. ● If your pilot episode does “just okay,” then stay the course and see if it finds its audience. 6 QUESTIONS TO ASK YOURSELF BEFORE A FULL PIVOT ● Should we try another episode and give the audience another chance to show up for it? ● How long do we want to wait and see? Remember, YouTube is not about instant success; it takes time to build an audience, and viral videos are rare. ● Are our expectations for performance realistic? ● Should we try additional marketing efforts to reach our desired audience? ● Is the problem with the episode topic or the show as a whole? ● Is there a way we can pivot the format or other elements of the show to address what audiences are not liking?

# Page 138^page=138
274 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 275 CASE STUDY We originally developed the series “Hip-Hop and the Metaverse” as a look into hip-hop’s impact in today’s emerging technologies. Watch the pilot episode, “Hold Up, How Did Hip-Hop Become a Trailblazer in VR?” to get a feel for the early show. As the first episodes published, viewership was low, and the series’ Web3, AI and Metaverse themes often led to negative comments. To be responsive to audience feedback while pursuing the mission to provide an educational perspective on hip-hop and its communities’ impact, we first leaned away from Web3 and AI themes and reflected on hip-hop’s impact on technology more broadly. Ultimately, when that proved ineffective, we paused production after our fifth episode and devised an entirely new series. “Outside the Lyrics” was inspired by the research we had gathered during the production of “Hip-Hop and the Metaverse.” We shifted our focus to the culture and community of hip-hop beyond its music (fashion, dance and photography). Tonally, the show leaned into a more cool, stylish and minimal documentary-style approach (versus HHMV’s maximalist explainer style). We also prioritized title and thumbnail development. Upon release, “Outside the Lyrics” had a significant increase in views and positive engagement. Watch “The Story Behind These Iconic Hip-Hop Photos” to see how the show shifted. If we did it again, we would develop a leaner production approach, allowing more resources to be allocated to research and development. Fully scripting each episode would allow us to control each story without having to build it in the edit through interviews. This was something we found effective in our series “Stop Saving the Planet,” which relied entirely on a host-to-camera setup accompanied by archival material and motion graphics. If more episodes were green-lit, we would also lean toward batching as many episodes as possible, once initial feedback and analytics were collected on the first few episodes. A Full-Show Pivot by PBS SoCal Case Study Series: Hip-Hop and the Metaverse and Outside the Lyrics Station Name: PBS SoCal/KCET Author Name: Jaime Morgan Muñoz Comparing “Weathered” Before and After a Pivot PRACTICE ACTIVITY “Weathered,” a show about extreme weather and natural disasters, undertook a full pivot after its first episode. STEP 1 Compare and contrast the first episode with a more recent one. What feels different about these two episodes to you? 1. Watch the first episode: “We Can’t Stop Wildfires — But Here’s How We Live With Them” 2. Watch a recent episode: “Is Earth’s Largest Heat Transfer Really Shutting Down?” STEP 2 Now, watch Balance Media explain how their team approached this pivot and the lessons they learned in this recorded Zoom seminar about pivoting. Estimated watch time: 35 minutes “Weathered” went on to become an anchor show for PBS Terra. ACTIVIT Y Parting Thoughts After a successful pilot launch, keep iteration in mind. It’s not a one-and-done. Analyze every episode to see what worked and what didn’t to better inform your next episode…and the next…and the next! The more episodes you publish, the better you get at analyzing meta-trends about certain topics, formats or sequences. Look for more comments and views episode over episode — this is a sign of a very healthy show. And don’t be afraid to lean in if something is working, or to try something new! Digital Storytelling | Pivoting and Piloting

hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 277276 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook REFLECTION ACTIVIT Y Conducting a Show Evaluation What to Look for After Wrapping Season One Congratulations on finishing the first season of your new show! This section will explore how to conduct a debrief after a season of a series is completed. • What were the biggest successes? • What could have been better? • What trends do we see in analytics? It’s crucial to reflect and capture these findings so the second season or next show can be even better. PRACTICE ACTIVITY Getting to Know YouTube Analytics Before diving into the full components of a debrief, it’s important to start with the data. Data is an unbiased metric of performance and a good place to start for a basic understanding of how your show performed in terms of views. Although other metrics of success can also be important, how this series played out in attracting your audience (and how they felt about it!) will reveal a lot. Digital Storytelling Reflect on the video and name three data points that YouTube Analytics can show you and why they might be helpful when doing an analysis on a completed show. The first step is getting to know YouTube Analytics — the data dashboard for a YouTube channel. First, let’s get familiar with everything YouTube Analytics can do (because it’s a lot!). Watch the video Analytics in YouTube Studio (approximately six minutes) for a brief and thorough overview of this powerful tool. Why Debriefs Should Be Part of Your Process A show debrief is critical to the long-term success of your show and allows you to reflect on key questions at the end of your series. WHY ARE SHOW DEBRIEFS IMP ORTANT ? 1. Show debriefs provide a dedicated space to honestly reflect on the highs, lows and in-betweens after finishing the wild ride of making the actual project. 2. Process reflection and data allow you to iterate and improve. How will you know where you’re going if you don’t know where you are? Think of your series like a business. If you don’t take a moment to analyze each quarter — what’s selling and what’s not — how will you be able to order the right inventory for your customers? 3. Debriefs encourage you to document wins, impact and opportunities, which can be effectively packaged to your leadership team to advocate for changes and/or future funding. WHAT SHOULD DEBRIEFS INCLUDE? The basic pillars of a show debrief should include the following: An analytics review: A look purely at the numbers and performance. This often utilizes YouTube Analytics but can also encompass social media performance analytics like Instagram Reels and TikTok. Editorial approach and workflows: A closer look at the wins and growth opportunities as they pertain to the creative vision, editorial direction and execution. Did these align strategically with current station work and goals? Operations workflow and deliverables: A closer look at the wins and growth opportunities as they pertain to the deliverables, budgeting, sponsorship and contracts. Mission advancement and community engagement: A holistic review of any goals set forth at the beginning of the series’ pre-production. Did you meet the goals set at the beginning of the series’ pre-production? What were the wins or challenges encountered along the way? These categories are not exhaustive. Add any others that make sense! WHEN SHOULD WE DEBRIEF? Typically, a debrief should happen approximately 30 days after the final episodes of a series are published. This way, the process highs and lows are still fresh in people’s minds, but the team has had time to reflect on how the project went for them individually and overall. This also gives time for any “long tail” viewership to trickle in, providing a more even playing field for later episodes’ analytics. Try to take at least an hour with the team to hold a debrief. You will be surprised how fast the time goes!

Complete the series debrief below, ideally as a team with anyone who was involved in key creative or strategy decisions. TASK Choose your series. This should be a series with at least three episodes whose last episode was published at least 30 days ago. While conducting this debrief, try to answer these questions: ● Would you renew this series for another season? Why or why not? ● How would you convince your leadership of your argument? TAKEAWAYS 1. How to conduct a show debrief 2. A basic understanding of advanced YouTube Analytics, including knowing what key indicators to look for when conducting a show analysis, purely in terms of performance 3. How to use analytics to influence future editorial decisions MATERIALS NEEDED ● A recently completed series or miniseries ● Access to YouTube Studio (viewer permission or higher) c Psst! If you don’t have access to YouTube Studio, no worries. You can complete Part 1 below using these screenshots from “Rogue History: Season 2.” PART 1: ANALY TICS REVIEW Sign into YouTube Studio, go to Analytics, and go to Advanced Mode. Create a group for the series. This will make tracking analytics easier and will allow you to isolate the series you’re analyzing. Still in Advanced Mode, confirm you’re viewing the group you just created. Set the time period to the first episode publish date through the current date. Your line chart should be set to “Views by: Content.” Time to analyze: ● Which videos had the most views? The least? ● Which videos had the highest impressions click-through rate (CTR)? The lowest? ● Which videos brought in the most subscribers? ● Click on “Viewer Age.” What was the largest age group that watched your series? ● Click on “Viewer Gender.” Did your series have more men watching or more women? Reflect and discuss: ● What insights can you deduce from the videos that had the most views? Are they similar in some way or different from the lowest-viewed videos? ● Did the audience demographics surprise you? Are they at all different from the overall channel’s average age and gender? PART 2: EDITORIAL REVIEW Review key wins and missed opportunities in the editorial direction and creative vision of the show. Discuss as a team: ● What did you really like about working on this project and with this team? ● If you could do this project over knowing what you know now, what would you change? ● Did you complete any collabs with other creators, make quick turns on timely topics or produce episodes that were significant to advancing the PBS mission? ● What were the editorial or creative goals for this season? Did you meet those goals? ● What significant snags did you encounter in your production, if any? How did you overcome them? ● How were the team dynamics throughout the series? Were roles and responsibilities clearly defined? Was communication clear and thorough? ● What tools and/or resources do you wish you had throughout the production? ● What’s the overall sentiment in the comments? How might that influence future discussions? You can dive even deeper in the Understanding the Basics of YouTube Analytics section of this guidebook! 284 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 285 Creating Impactful Classroom Content PBS LearningMedia, a partnership of PBS and GBH, is a dynamic website that offers pre-K–12 educators free access to resources that enrich classroom instruction and bring lessons to life with engaging content from PBS member stations and partners. This section will cover how to create high-quality, classroom-ready content, including principles of content production, and discuss best practices for PBS LearningMedia. Make your process conducive to a multiplatform project and its associated deliverables by following these tips during each key production phase. Expanding Impact Question 1: Which video(s) did you pick and why? Question 2: What video subject is most relevant to classroom learning? Question 3: Do you think the video(s) will align with classroom learning or state standards? Research local subject-area state standards online and record the standards that align most with the topic(s) in your video. Question 4: Is there anything about the video that you would adjust, knowing that it will be used in the classroom? Be specific. REFLECTION ACTIVIT Y Let’s think about how to use our video in the classroom. Write down the last three videos you produced or will produce this year. ● Write down the video topic(s) and simple description(s). ● Which videos do you think would work in a classroom to enhance student learning? Producing for PBS LearningMedia PRACTICE ACTIVITY Guidebook users will be able to identify which videos are best suited for classroom use. TAKEAWAY PBS LearningMedia Overview PBS LearningMedia offers free, media-based content intentionally designed to complement classroom instruction for pre-K–12 educators and students. Our goal is to enhance educator practice and equitable student learning through educational resources. ● Available to teachers and students at no cost for educational use ● Relevant, accessible and trustworthy ● Intentionally designed to supplement classroom instruction ● Representative of a wide range of perspectives representing many communities ● Designed to promote and support effective practices in teaching and learning with digital media Now, let’s learn more about the PBS LearningMedia platform and content development best practices to help you create better media for the classroom. EDUCATE AND INSPIRE TEACHERS AND STUDENTS USING CONTENT The first step is to engage with current classroom teachers. Involve teachers in every step of your production process, from brainstorming topics for classroom exploration to choosing the media clips. Educators are critical partners in the creation, review and evaluation of materials to ensure content is relevant, appropriate for classroom use and aligned to academic standards. ● Teachers should be directly informing, reviewing, testing or writing the classroom materials you develop to ensure those materials are grade-level-appropriate and aligned to the core curriculum. ● Use subject-matter experts to review all content and make sure your materials are professionally edited. ● Connect with your PBS LearningMedia content managers. They will work with you to ensure your materials are aligned to PBS standards and practices and designed to the content templates and user experience the website supports. ● Understand what content is already on the PBS LearningMedia platform to determine how your materials will be relevant and unique. Work with educators to focus on the stories that supplement their curriculum that haven’t been told before.

# Page 144^page=144
286 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 287 PRODUCING ORIGINAL CL ASSROOM CONTENT ● Media will be curricula-driven. ● Summarize key concepts throughout the video verbally, with graphics and text to assist in student comprehension of key topics addressed in the video. ● Explore the medium: the use of text on screen, the music and production values that appeal to both educators and students and support the learning. ● Creatively incorporate new and varied voices that aren’t close by; for example, you could conduct remote interviews or incorporate elements that are created by the community you are featuring, such as first-person show-and-tell pieces. ● Utilize hosts, where applicable, who appeal to a student-based audience. This may include the following elements: young hosts, engaging contemporary style and design elements, age-accessible writing. ● Create shorter videos for classroom use (three to nine minutes long). ● Review content with classroom educators to make sure it is appropriate for the classroom, grade level and age. ● Review mature content to ensure outdated language and inappropriate imagery are removed or flagged from the video in the beginning stages of production. CREATING EQUITABLE CL ASSROOM MATERIALS ● Embed research inputs into your process early and often. Look beyond the usual spaces to ensure that your team brings in a variety of experiences and perspectives early in the production to inform story choice. DESIGNING MATERIALS WITH A PBS LEARNINGMEDIA CONTENT MANAGER ● Resource titles should summarize the video and its curricular connection. ● Collaborate as a team to design content for classrooms and in support of the website’s learning templates. ● Make sure all materials are accessible and aligned to PBS standards. Let’s dive into a few case studies that illustrate these best practices. Explore the curriculum. What are students learning? What are the state-based curriculum* needs, including subject areas and grade level? What stories haven’t been told before? What other materials exist on the topic? Conduct a landscape analysis. *Curriculum will vary state-by-state. Audit PBS LearningMedia’s content. Work with a PBS LearningMedia content manager to identify content gaps and create materials that meet the needs of the platform. Partner with educators. Use teachers to inform the direction of your project. Identify core partners and stakeholders to test assumptions and inform the materials you produce; for example, incorporate educator and student feedback, as well as subject-matter experts. Use authentic voices. Consult with audiences who will be featured in the materials. Think about the community, culture, context and expertise needed to tell the story. Test pilot episodes with educators to ensure alignment to curriculum standards and grade-level accuracy and to inform future work. Practice windows and mirrors. Working with educators, select media and create classroom activities that help students “look in” and learn about other people’s experiences and also help them see a reflection of themselves in the content. Develop content that broadens understanding of a topic. Elevate stories that feature people and events that represent the diversity of America. Exercise caution when adapting from broadcast or YouTube- created content. Review with educators to confirm the appropriateness of media and the alignment to curriculum standards and the targeted grade level. Not all public media content is right for PBS LearningMedia. Your original media might have educational value outside of PBS LearningMedia and K–12 classrooms that is worth exploring through other partnerships. Featuring your content on the right platforms will result in much better viewer engagement than will promoting it on multiple platforms (including PBSLM). 7 Steps for Engaging Classroom-Ready Content for PBS LearningMedia Expanding Impact | Creating Impactful Classroom Content

288 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 289 Case Study Series: Game Changers Video: Youth Voting Rights Station Name: GBH Author Names: Elizabeth Gardner and Joshua Littenberg-Tobias Creating Effective Youth-Facing Content CASE STUDY At GBH Education, we use an “Equity Compass” to guide the development of all new content: ● Research, identify and respond to community needs. ● Include community members in the creation, production and implementation of content. ● Ensure content represents and reflects the community we are trying to reach. In 2024, GBH produced “Game Changers,” a new youth-facing digital series that tells the stories of everyday citizens who are exercising their constitutional rights. Implementing an iterative design process allowed us to gather input from community members and incorporate their feedback throughout the development cycle. When applied, the Equity Compass looked like this: ● Respond: To ensure we fulfilled a need for students and educators, we worked with the curriculum team to shape the concept and select story ideas. ● Include: The research team recruited youth and educators to give feedback via focus groups and surveys. ● Represent: To include community members in the series’ production, we enlisted featured talent to contribute self-shot footage that amplified their perspective. Based on the feedback we received from community members and youth, we made several key changes: ● Amplify authenticity by including more self-shot footage ● Use visual signposting but streamline the style ● Layer in Civics 101 information to provide context Applying the Equity Compass and coming up with a process to solicit and integrate feedback from community members helped us create a storytelling approach and visual style that we found resonates with youth audiences. If we did it again, we would use the same process we developed for “Game Changers” but build in additional rounds of feedback and revision to allow youth more opportunity to shape the final outcome. Case Study Series: Indigi-Genius and Sovereign Innovations Video: Waterproof Clothing Station Name: New Mexico PBS Author Names: Ashley Rammelsberg and Myah Wilmarth In 2022, New Mexico PBS created “Indigi-Genius,” a digital series devoted to telling the scientific and cultural impact of Indigenous creations and knowledge of the past and present, breaking down global Indigenous science, culture and history. The series was written and hosted by Lee Francis, Ph.D. (Pueblo and self-described “Indigi-Nerd”). It was a priority to have a Native voice driving the content. The station also wanted to reach a general audience, educators and school-aged children with stories that highlighted topics not typically covered in the classroom. To do this, Dr. Francis collaborated with a local educator to create video and educational support materials. Although we were able to create Native-centered educational content, the series, which performed very well on PBS LearningMedia, did not perform well on YouTube, which was discouraging. Telling Stories for, With and by the Target Audience CASE STUDY Include the community you are featuring as creators. Know how to speak to the audiences you are trying to reach using authentic voices, language, images, etc. In 2023, New Mexico PBS applied for a PBS Digital Studios grant to produce “Sovereign Innovations,” a digital series highlighting how Indigenous innovation and technology allow Indigenous communities to survive and thrive now and across the centuries. Hosted by Cheyenne Bearfoot (Chiricahua Apache), this series explores Native and Indigenous identities across the globe to unravel stereotypes that diminished innovations made by Indigenous peoples. “Sovereign Innovations” built on the concept of “Indigi-Genius,” but this time with the hiring of more Indigenous creators, access to more digital training through the grant program and a new core audience to build off. “Sovereign Innovations” performed extremely well, reaching large swaths of Native audiences and general audiences. Indigenous creators behind and in front of the camera created a richer content experience for viewers, taking them on a deep dive into educational topics like “Celebrating the Diversity of Indigenous Homes.” By leveraging PBSDS platforms and hiring multiple Indigenous creatives, this series not only advanced the show’s reach but also elevated its authenticity, accuracy and tone, making it successful. Expanding Impact | Creating Impactful Classroom Content

# Page 146^page=146
290 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 291 Case Study Series: Unladylike2020 | American Masters Video: Maggie Lena Walker Station Name: The WNET Group Author Name: Sandy Goldberg In 2020, WNET created digital resources that feature the rich history of 26 diverse, little-known Progressive-Era women who broke barriers in then-male-dominated fields. Touching on topics such as the labor movement, immigration, politics, civil rights and women’s suffrage, these resources develop students’ historical thinking skills and help them make connections between past and present. Understanding the Community, Culture, Context and Expertise Needed to Tell the Story CASE STUDY Develop content that broadens the understanding of a topic by elevating stories featuring people and events that represent the diversity of America. WNET collaborated closely with series producers to create content that: ● Applies the “windows” and “mirrors” approach that helps students “look in” to learn about other people’s experiences and “see a reflection” of themselves in the content ● Aligns with grade-level curriculum standards for middle- and high-school social studies ● Features primary-source documents in the video that are also examined in the supporting materials ● Reflects the historical accuracy of the series (series experts reviewed all written content) “This is a terrific SERIES, not just from a teacher’s perspective but [also in respect to] ‘life’ awareness!” – Teacher To achieve these goals, WNET made sure there was an effective feedback loop across two key collaborator groups: 1. Curriculum alignment: Education staff had access and feedback opportunities to script drafts and video rough cuts to ensure Progressive-Era curriculum alignment. 2. Content accuracy and inclusivity: Producers provided feedback on all support materials developed by WNET staff to confirm the content’s accuracy and inclusive language. The process worked so well because of the integrated collaboration between the series producers, content-area experts utilized in the videos and WNET education staff. The WNET education director and producers worked in tandem throughout the production process. As a result, the series producers really understood the educational value and requirements of these digital-first videos and could therefore make PBSLM resources as robust as possible for educators and students. As one teacher put it, “This is a terrific SERIES, not just from a teacher’s perspective but [also in respect to] ‘life’ awareness!” Expanding Impact | Creating Impactful Classroom Content

292 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 293 Case Study Series: Wisconsin Biographies Video: Geshe Lhundub Sopa: Carrying Teachings from Tibet Station Name: PBS Wisconsin Author Name: Megan Monday Convene unique advisory cohorts and community partnerships to create accurate, connected, reflective and relevant content for students today. Bringing History to Life for Today’s Learners CASE STUDY “Wisconsin Biographies” is a series that brings history to life with short, animated videos, digital books with read-along support, digitized primary sources and extension activity ideas that support academic standards. This collection uses a local history, place-based learning approach to spark social studies and English Language Arts explorations. Over time, we expanded the way we produce to be more collaborative. We now work with grade-band educators and cultural and content-area experts and hire production roles from within our communities when able. For the most recent stories, we identified opportunities to fill gaps in identities and geography to better mirror student populations in the state. For example, “Geshe Lhundub Sopa: Carrying Teachings from Tibet” was developed utilizing the following production guidelines: ● Create an advisory committee including monks from the temple Geshe Lhundub Sopa founded, Tibetan language and culture scholars, fourth-grade educators and fourth-grade learners. ● Have education staff write the animation script, book and educator guide and advisors review all the materials. ● Ask cultural advisors to review art and scripts to ensure accuracy at multiple points during production. ● Hire a Tibetan actor to voice the animation. ● Hire a Tibetan illustrator to create the art. ● Hire a Tibetan language scholar to translate the book. ● Hire Tibetan musicians to score and perform the music. ● Request self-reported demographic data when appropriate to ensure accuracy. In the end, we created a multimedia resource that used an engagement-led production model that is responsive to student populations and cultures and includes members of the community in the production of the resource. Case Study Series: Let’s Go! Video: Nash House Museum & Michigan Street Baptist Church Station Name: Buffalo Toronto Public Media Author Name: Beth Fronckowiak, WNED “Let’s Go!” is a digital field trip series that provides educators, children and families with interactive learning opportunities beyond traditional settings. Designed for children ages seven to eleven, each video is accompanied by vocabulary, teaching tips and activities to ensure accessible, equitable and engaging content. WNED partnered with 10 educational sites and kept them updated on our progress, including host selection, filming, release dates and curriculum development. This collaboration fostered a sense of ownership among partners, who actively shared the episodes and resources through their channels. Recognizing the need to leverage our partners more effectively, we organized “Let’s Go!” Day on April 2, 2024. The marketing blitz coincided with local schools’ spring breaks to attract families and educators. The campaign stretched across WNED platforms, including press releases, email, social media, radio and TV. Partners received promotional assets to distribute within their communities. The collaboration proved successful, with nearly all partners supporting “Let’s Go!” Day and expressing interest in future campaigns. However, we learned that a single day was insufficient to reach our audience fully. Certain platforms, like WBFO social media, did not perform as expected. Organic videos featuring our host Chrisena did not gain traction, indicating a need to more gradually familiarize our audience with this content style. Looking ahead, we aim to secure funding for a second season, expanding to rural or less-visited sites and including Canadian locations. To triage our initial challenges, we would: 1. Engage educator focus groups earlier in the process to better fill curriculum gaps and increase PBSLM online. 2. Expand “Let’s Go!” Day to a full week to provide more opportunities to reach our audience. 3. Create vertical companion videos paired to each episode to use during “Let’s Go!” Week. Tap Into Your Community CASE STUDY Develop meaningful educational resources and content by leveraging community partnerships that are central to the production process. Together, create meaningful community-based events and marketing strategies to reach those target audiences through new means. Expanding Impact | Creating Impactful Classroom Content

# Page 148^page=148
294 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 295 Congratulations! Now you’re ready to start creating high-quality, classroom-ready, media-based content. Parting Thoughts HOW TO BECOME A PBS LEARNINGMEDIA PRODUCING PARTNER PBS LearningMedia is an amazing way to expand the impact of your work when your materials are designed with classrooms in mind. Take these next steps to build a strong education network at your station and inform future work:  Explore the resources on PBSLearningMedia.org to learn more about the platform.  Build relationships with the education staff at your station.  Create advisory boards with teachers, community-based experts and students.  Use advisory boards to identify current state-curriculum topics and create resources highlighting the spotlit communities in the media.  Invite community in as production/project partners to help support distribution and engagement with PBS LearningMedia resources.  Work closely with PBS LearningMedia content managers to align your materials to PBS best practices. Interested in working with PBS LearningMedia? Contact us at pbslm. editorial@pbs.org. WHAT IS A MULTIPL ATFORM PROJECT ? A multiplatform project is a single project with original content deliverables that stretch across multiple platforms. It is not a broadcast show with only clips and promos posted to YouTube, Facebook or Instagram. Instead, it might be a broadcast show with an original digital companion series or a longform show with an app-first release plus a short-form series premiering on YouTube or a vertical video platform. Alternatively, there could be YouTube or vertical-first series with a companion podcast to dive deeper into the subject matter. Producing for multiple platforms at once means juggling many deliverables, specifications and distinct workflows. The key is knowing which platform requires which format and paired workflow and how you make sure they are intertwined from day one. This chapter dives into what multiplatform means to PBS today, best practices for producing a multiplatform project with PBS and beyond and case studies illustrating how producers have found success. Key Vocabulary: ● Longform content is typically 30+ minutes per episode and designed to be launched on YouTube, PBS App and PBS.org, and/or broadcast. ● Short form content is typically 5-15 minutes per episode and designed for digital-first launch. ● Longform vs. Broadcast: While broadcast is a longform format, not all longform content launches or airs on broadcast. Longform content can also be specifically geared towards the PBS App and PBS.org. ● Short form vs. YouTube: While YouTube is short form, not all short form content is on YouTube. Short form content includes platforms like Instagram and TikTok. Producing a PBS Multiplatform Project Multiplatform project best practices from PBS and beyond. Expanding Impact | Creating Impactful Classroom Content Expanding Impact

# Page 149^page=149
296 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 297 THE PBS MULTIPL ATFORM STRATEGY As PBS evolves beyond linear broadcast programs, content must be able to succeed across multiple platforms to meet audiences where they are. We adapt our content to meet the needs and expectations of audiences on each platform; a one-size-fits-all approach rarely works in a multiplatform context. Instead, we grow audiences and digital distribution channels through owned digital platforms such as the PBS App as well as third-party platforms such as YouTube or vertical video channels. We value experimenting with new formats and share that knowledge with the PBS system. At the time of publishing, key PBS platforms include the following: ● Owned digital platforms (PBS App, station sites and PBS.org) ● YouTube ● Broadcast and linear platforms ● Vertical video platforms (Instagram, TikTok and YouTube Shorts) ● Free ad-supported streaming TV (FAST) channels ● Podcast platforms (iOS and Spotify) What Makes a Good Multiplatform Project? Many multiplatform best practices mirror those of digital production highlighted throughout this guidebook, so the criteria may feel familiar. This makes digital fluency a strength for multiplatform work. Successful multiplatform projects typically feature some or all of the following elements: ● Multigenerational appeal based on the topic, format and/or a compelling host ● Digitally savvy on-camera talent who often have an existing online following c At the least there should be an interest in engaging with viewers online. Either option makes a longform project work better across multiple platforms. h For example: Baratunde Thurston (host of “America Outdoors”), Shane Campbell-Staton (host of “Human Footprint”) and Alejandra Ramos (host of “The Great American Recipe”) each were excited to interact with fans online, had dedicated audiences on social and brought a digital fluency that comes from being an online creator. This amplified their series alongside traditional marketing efforts from PBS and station partners. ● A production team with demonstrated experience producing both longform (linear or streaming platforms) and short form (YouTube or vertical). ● Chapter-based storytelling like easily excerpted chapters converted into self-contained stories for YouTube and social video h For example: Break up longform individual episodes into 5- to 15-minute chapters. Think about how a late-night show segments based on monologues, interviews or performances. ● Pacing that works across platforms h For example: “Weathered: Earth’s Extremes” used storytelling conventions and pacing that merged longform documentary approaches and YouTube best practices. Episodes were edited to feel more kinetic than a broadcast series but stopped short of being as dense and fast-paced as a YouTube series like “Crash Course.” Finding that middle ground helped this series succeed across YouTube, social and linear platforms and the PBS App. Expanding Impact | Producing a PBS Multiplatform Project

# Page 150^page=150
298 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 299 ● Mission-driven and data-informed editorial and audience development approach ● Timely subject matter ● A unique subject matter, format or niche not covered in other series ● Flexible digital rights that can better position a series to maximize reach on YouTube and social through longtail traffic and larger, global potential audiences c Offer long free video on demand (FVOD) terms between one and two years instead of 30 days. c When possible, securing global rights can ensure a project reaches the most viewers, especially on global digital platforms such as YouTube. Note that platforms such as TikTok don’t have geo-blocking features, so global rights are required to release on these platforms. c Work with your legal counsel to determine what is within the scope for your project. There are successful multiplatform projects that do not include all these factors. h For example: “FRONTLINE” has no on-camera host but performs extremely well on YouTube. Instead, they leverage a distinct, recognizable brand and digital best practices including effective YouTube packaging, global releases and timely rereleases. That said, the more these approaches are consistently used, the more successful a project tends to be across platforms. How to Maximize Reach and Impact Across Formats and Platforms How you maximize the impact, reach and resonance of your project depends on the platform(s) you choose at the beginning of your project. Each format has unique best practices for each platform. Below are select best practices especially related to YouTube. LONGFORM CONTENT Longform content typically features broadcast, YouTube, PBS App and PBS.org as key formats. ● Use YouTube’s Premiere function to build anticipation for high-priority projects. This also allows talent to engage with viewers through the Premiere chat function. “America Outdoors” and “Weathered: Earth’s Extremes” both had engaged live chats during their respective YouTube Premieres. This resulted in a sense of community and viewer loyalty that amplified the projects immediately on launch day. ● Release longform content on YouTube when it is most timely to maximize reach. For example, “Asian Americans” was rereleased on YouTube during Asian American Pacific Islander (AAPI) Heritage month and received hundreds of thousands of organic views. ● Older longform documentaries can sometimes find a new life on YouTube if they cover evergreen topics or are newly relevant. The 2012 Independent Lens film “Soul Food Junkies” explored evergreen stories regarding food and culture that audiences deeply related to. Upon rerelease on YouTube in 2024, it snagged 200,000+ views with little promotion! COMPANION SERIES Companion series are original digital-first series paired to a longform broadcast show(s), typically released on YouTube, podcast and vertical video platforms. ● Don’t treat these series only as marketing content! They are original, self-contained digital shows inspired by broadcast events. ● The content should complement but not duplicate the longform. ● Include call-to-actions to raise awareness of longform companion content and drive to streaming. ● Companion series can outlive the broadcast specials. “It’s Lit! on PBS Storied” was a companion series for “The Great American Read,” a one-off broadcast event that continued into a second season funded by the National Endowment for the Humanities. ● Work with influencers to immediately reach audiences at launch. Digital series “Future of Work,” “Stellar” and “It’s Lit!” featured popular online creators as hosts. Leveraging creators’ existing communities can help a great deal with audience development on a new series when marketing budgets are limited. Expanding Impact | Producing a PBS Multiplatform Project

# Page 151^page=151
300 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 301 ● Vertical video content typically encompasses TikTok, Instagram and YouTube Shorts and is unique, standalone content in its own right. ● Always create vertical format social video content rather than the outdated square or 16x9. ● Vertical videos have a high return on investment (ROI) and can cost less than a full YouTube episode. Even better, you can publish the same video on multiple platforms. ● Keep vertical videos under or around one minute, although platforms continue to experiment with time restrictions and expansions. Be sure you know which platform requires what. ● Varying vertical video styles and formats can succeed. For instance, platform-native, direct-to-camera, TikTok-style vertical video content can work well. But there is still a chance to integrate compelling, short lifts from broadcast projects cut into vertical content — as long as the video feels authentic to the platform! ● Segments are lifts or clips from a longform series and can be published on YouTube, the PBS App or PBS.org. What makes for a strong segment? h Go beyond 1- to 3-minute teaser clips and previews in favor of self-contained stories with a beginning, middle and end. h Use evergreen topics that generate longtail viewership well after broadcast. h 5- to 15-minute lifts are best! h Feature well-known figures or influencers. h Get right to the point, and don’t keep audiences waiting. h Thumbnails have a single, high-impact image that scales well for mobile and OTT apps such as the YouTube app on Roku or Apple TV and the PBS App. h Use titles that tease the best parts of a video and lead with content details with some personality as opposed to the longform or series title. h Publish only one to two segments per one-hour episode. What makes for a weak segment? h Too short (under five minutes) h Cuts off a story or performance midway through h Doesn’t deliver on the promise of the title h Missing what is appealing to a show’s core audience If a series resists segmenting, don’t force it. Instead, lean into the longform options on digital platforms as well as bespoke social and YouTube content whenever possible. The Power of a Digital Companion Series Companion series are a powerful tool to make projects succeed across multiple platforms. PBS has received tens of millions of views through companion series such as “Pan Pals” (paired to “The Great American Recipe”), “It’s Lit!” (paired to “The Great American Read”) and “America Outdoors: Understory” (paired to “America Outdoors”) among others, over the past decade. These companion series allow a project to meet audiences where they are by creating content tailored for YouTube, social media, podcasts or any other target platform — especially if the longform project does not have a format that performs well on digital platforms. Plenty of Style Options Companion content can come in a variety of forms. It can be an entirely bespoke digital series inspired by a longform project, like “Pan Pals,” that takes the themes of the broadcast version in exciting new directions. Or they could draw from the stories in the longform series but remix them in a way to better engage online audiences, like “Human Footprint’s shorts.” Reaching Target Audiences Ultimately, what ties all these companion series together is that they are engineered to better reach online audiences on platforms such as YouTube, Instagram or other third-party social apps. They are created as self-contained projects to build community around the themes and stories of the longform series that inspired them as well as drive streams and awareness for these series on our owned platforms through carefully crafted call-to-actions. Engagement Companion content can also help keep audiences engaged between longform seasons of a project. They can even sometimes continue well after a longform series has concluded. For example, “It’s Lit!” returned for a second season, continuing to grow the community of book lovers years after “The Great American Read’s” conclusion. Digital-First Principles This chapter is a primer on multiplatform best practices at PBS, which thrive on digital-first principles. Before engaging with a multiplatform project and/or companion series, either locally or with PBS, ensure the digital side of the project will be created according to best practices laid out in earlier chapters of this guidebook. To help imagine what your own multiplatform project could look like, let’s dive into a trio of successful companion series along with details on the tactics that helped them reach viewers and cross-promote their longform series. Expanding Impact | Producing a PBS Multiplatform Project

# Page 152^page=152
302 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 303 Case Study Series: Pan Pals Station Name: Austin PBS Author Name: Adam Dylewski Pairing Companion Series With PBS Broadcast Events CASE STUDY “Pan Pals” is a companion digital series created by food influencer Beryl Shereshewsky and co-produced by Austin PBS. The series features diverse cooks, influencers and participants from the PBS broadcast series “The Great American Recipe.” In each episode, two people swap recipes within a given theme. Each dish is inspired by a unique culture and culinary lens, leaving each “Pan Pal” with an opportunity to expand their worldview and learn new tips and tricks. During “Recipe: Season 1,” the digital strategy involved launching a few full episodes on YouTube plus show lifts or compilations. The longform series performed well on linear and streaming services, but its YouTube presence notably underperformed. We learned that this series resisted segmentation for YouTube and social channels. A Revamped Digital Strategy For “Recipe: Season 2,” we needed a revamped digital strategy. “Pan Pals” was pitched as a bespoke YouTube companion series to “The Great American Recipe” to launch alongside the broadcast’s second season. Inspired by the broadcast show’s theme of finding common ground through food, “Pan Pals” pairs home cooks, influencers and Recipe contestants and has them swap recipes and learn from one another. Leveraging the PBS Food-Themed Channel The team also wanted to try shifting from the PBS YouTube channel, which hosts lots of different content, and instead revive the PBS Food-themed channel with “Pan Pals.” PBS could leverage Beryl’s established presence on YouTube with 800,000 subscribers and use calls-to-action (CTAs) at the end of the episodes and social media posts to drive her loyal followers to PBS Food and raise awareness for “The Great American Recipe,” too. “Pan Pals” was a success, receiving more than 1.5 million views on PBS YouTube, TikTok and Instagram accounts and reinvigorating the PBS Food YouTube channel. Even better: Pan Pals viewers were successfully directed to the full-length episodes of “The Great American Recipe” on YouTube, driving views and engagement from Beryl’s fans to PBS longform programming! New end card for “Pan Pals” where Beryl engages audiences and asks them to check out “The Great American Recipe.” Case Study Series: America Outdoors: Understory Station Name: TPT — Twin Cities PBS Author Name: Adam Dylewski Tailoring Companion Series to Specific Audience Niches CASE STUDY “America Outdoors” with Baratunde Thurston is a longform series uncovering our complex relationships with the outdoors. “America Outdoors: Understory” was created as a bespoke companion series to launch on digital platforms alongside the longform to drive awareness for the series. The goal for “Understory” was to expand on the themes of the longform series, tell a new set of stories about the outdoors and continue to build the community around Baratunde and the “America Outdoors” brand. Because he has an approachable personality and strong online presence, Baratunde worked well as the host for the digital series in addition to the broadcast. The first season of “Understory” led to solid performance on YouTube, but there was inconsistent performance episode to episode. By analyzing the six episodes released on PBS-owned platforms and the PBS YouTube channel, the team believed that the wide variety of stories covered in the digital series contributed to inconsistent viewership. We hypothesized a narrower niche would enable future seasons to appeal to more specific audiences on YouTube. Season two prioritized a strategy shift in two areas: 1. The new season would anchor around a single theme, Our Changing Planet, telling stories at the intersection of climate change and the outdoors. 2. We moved the series to PBS Terra, the science-themed YouTube channel with audiences primed for such content. By theming the series, the team could tap into the science behind our rapidly shifting environment and feature passionate innovators who are working to preserve the outdoor spaces we, the public, love. This more focused approach on a new home channel was a success: Views increased by 592% and watch time by 844% year-over-year. Expanding Impact | Producing a PBS Multiplatform Project Thumbnails from two episodes from the latest, climate-focused season of “American Outdoors: Understory.”

# Page 153^page=153
304 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 305 Case Study Series: Human Footprint Company Name: Day’s Edge Productions Author Name: Adam Dylewski Remixing Longform Stories for Digital Platforms CASE STUDY “Human Footprint” is a longform series exploring humanity’s unexpected impacts on the world around us, hosted by evolutionary biologist Shane Campbell- Staton. “Human Footprint” is designed to have multigenerational appeal and work across multiple platforms. As a charismatic host, Shane is equally comfortable doing a broadcast-style standup shoot as he is filming TikToks on his phone. In a rare twist, this series didn’t require a separate bespoke companion series to capture audiences online because of Shane’s affable on-camera presence and the kinetic style and pacing of “Human Footprint.” The series was chapter-based, so lifting 10- to 15-minute segments from the broadcast version was a possibility for companion content. However, direct series lifts weren’t enough to make the show automatically work on the target channel, PBS Terra. The channel audience and YouTube science fans expect deeper discussions of STEM topics than what is typically offered on broadcast science series. So, we had to get creative. The final approach for the “Human Footprint” Shorts companion series was to remix the best stories from the broadcast and reengineer them for YouTube. The team reedited the stories to remove unnecessary exposition, cut with digital-friendly pacing and deepened the volume of science content in each episode. To make it fresh and more digital friendly, the team made four key changes to the content: 1. They recorded additional VO and captured soundbites from experts who didn’t make it to the broadcast version. 2. Day’s Edge filmed vlog-style intros and outros with the host to make the series feel more like a native YouTube science series. 3. The outros featured call-to-actions from Shane encouraging viewers to watch the series on the PBS app. 4. All episodes engaged in iterative thumbnail and title design. If an episode underperformed, the team released an alternate thumbnail and title, which typically leads to higher click-through rates and stronger performance. This was the first time PBS tried a remix approach to a companion series and was thrilled with the response. Since “Human Footprint” Shorts launched in summer 2023, the digital episodes have received more than 4 million views on YouTube alone. One episode, “Inside the World’s ONLY Whale Warehouse,” received more than 1.3 million views on YouTube and was one of PBS Terra’s most popular videos in 2023. The “Human Footprint” companion series helped make the overall project a multiplatform success with strong viewership across the PBS app, YouTube and social and linear platforms. Learn more in the next chapter... Expanding Impact | Producing a PBS Multiplatform Project

# Page 154^page=154
306 hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook hub.pbs.org/dsguidebook 307 PRODUCTION Every piece of your production plan — including fieldwork — should have your target audience and key platform(s) in mind.  Film your host’s direct-to-camera vertical video content on location for TikTok, Instagram and YouTube Shorts.  Record call-to-actions with on-camera talent for YouTube and social content, encouraging viewers to stream your project on the PBS App.  Photograph compelling stills for YouTube thumbnails.  Are your longform episodes broken up into self-contained chapters? h Consider lifting compelling, digital-friendly segments for YouTube and PBS-owned platforms.  Making a companion podcast? h Consider repurposing longer excerpts of interviews for the podcast. PUBLISHING AND PROMOTION Promotion should be considered and integrated into workflow long before you hit publish. ● Cross-promotion is critical on digital platforms. Working with relevant station channels, local partners, PBS social channels, PBS Digital Studios creators and online influencers to cross-promote your project is the best way to ensure it connects with audiences. ● A modest paid promotional campaign can have a big impact. h For example: spending $1,000 to $2,000 toward a single video in a well-targeted YouTube paid campaign can lead to tens of thousands of engaged views or more, which can then result in strong organic performance in the long term. h For example: “The Bigger Picture” used this approach in their successful first season and went on to get more than a million views on YouTube alone! ● Being featured on app and website playlists with similar content can help interested viewers discover your project. Reach out to your station’s digital team and/or your distributor to discuss how your project will be curated online. ● Call-to-actions can be embedded in the beginning or end of a digital video. They should create a sense of urgency, be short and sweet and focus on value. What makes for an effective call-to-action? h Engage audiences by calling back to episode themes. For example, “Pan Pals” had Beryl ask viewers to share what their favorite noodle dish was in the comments. h Leverage series talent to lead the calls-to- action. Make sure this is incorporated into their contract and captured during filming! h Make the call-to-action easily accessible with clickable end screen elements and links in the description. h Adapt the strongest longform footage or film custom digital footage to make the end card fit digital spaces. PRE-PRODUCTION ● You don’t have to be everywhere. Don’t stretch yourself too thin! At the start of a multiplatform project, pick the primary and secondary platforms that best suit your project. Consider target audiences, topic, format and other factors. Keep those platforms in mind from the beginning, and craft your project. ● Loop in all relevant collaborators from the beginning. Consider PBS LearningMedia curricula, revenue opportunities, potential distribution partners (including PBS) and/or local station collaborations early in the process. Make sure everyone is in the loop and can plan together. ● Set up kickoff meetings with the producers who will be responsible for the project’s digital content. ● Choose the YouTube channel that is the best venue for your project’s format, tone and topic. Meet with your station YouTube strategist, or reach out to PBS Digital Studios. If you will be distributing through PBS, themed channels currently include the following: Crafting Multiplatform-Friendly Production Phases Like digital-first work, multiplatform content requires tailored workflow and editorial adaptations to make a project suitable for multiplatform distribution. Shooting a broadcast film and uploading it to YouTube with clips and a trailer isn’t enough for these platforms. Intentionally creating content made for the platform is crucial to success. Infusing linear production workflow with digital-first best practices can help your projects reach a much larger audience online. Adapt and align with the expectations of your viewers and how they consume content on third-party platforms! Make your process conducive to a multiplatform project and its associated deliverables by following these tips during each production phase.
